In T13, the archetypal version of this Monster is a Bulmäs Pack member from the Wild Hunt, but any Werewolf or Animal Shifter would probably qualify, as would robot cowboys in theme parks that have gone wrong, and any fantasy world's Elven ranger. </td></tr><tr><td>Miasma</td><td><strong>Mutant</strong> —The monster is a mutant or chimerical creature made from parts of others. This monster type is the oldest perhaps, you’ll find examples throughout the ancient world, particularly in Greek myth. The Mutant will often be larger, and may be malformed or unsymmetrical, with odd numbers of limbs, eyes, horns, or even heads. The Chimera will blend multiple animals together to create a new creature that combines features of all the “parent” creatures, preserving the best of all species. Mutant Monsters start with Miasma Score Monster Pool. They add Monster Pool Size as the Boon of Proficiency less "Mutation" Annex that they can use to grow larger or mimic their “parents” abilities. 1950s B-movie monsters are often of this type, giant ants, lizards, spiders, wasps, and even rabbits have been used. Classically of course, Cyclopses, Centaurs, Minotaurs, Satyrs, Gryphons (or Griffins), hippogriffs, dragons, sphinxes, manticores and of course the Chimera itself are all examples. You can also find examples in superheroes, especially those created by radioactive arachnids, or those with an X-gene.</td></tr><tr><td>Trial</td><td><strong>Killer</strong> —The Monster is a deadly Combat specialist of some sort. Killers add Trial Draw cards to their Monster Pool each time they kill someone. Killers automatically add +1 Score/Pip per card of Monster Pool to any attack, usually as Penetration or Range costs. Killers must spend Monster Pool cards whenever they will make a Trick. Killers can be drug-addled assassins, crazed shooters, or just steel-souled soldiers.</td></tr><tr><td>Yonder</td><td><strong>Alien</strong> —The Monster is an alien, an outsider. It may just be a metaphor for alienation, or literally be an extra-(or ultra)-terrestrial. Such monsters are usually obvious and inhuman, not just to look at, but in the way they think and behave. They have apparently supernatural powers and abilities, but often make do with advanced technologies (in the form of additional Descendants). They may draw and play Yonder Draw cards to and from their Monster Pool with their alien / inhuman / weird actions. Their Monster Pool number of cards Reduced adds Success Levels to any action to do with motion, survival or the alien's technologies. From little grey Eusaiveans, through to pod people and pepper-pot shaped engines of hate, all the way to face-hugging xenomorphic space-wasps, there are plenty of aliens.</td></tr></tbody><tfoot><tr><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</td><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</td></tr></tfoot></table></div><p></p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Red/Purple Carnivore</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> Hunters, Broccen, Vermis, Labyrinthine, All-Walkers</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> All-path</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong> All-Walkers</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> The Paths of the All-Walkers can allow Bulmäs to do extraordinary things, like walking an individual’s life-path, so that all of their personal history is known to them. All-Walkers can assume even the forms and souls of other people by using these paths, without directly eating of the Soul. All-Walkers can even Doom Weave and play other cards from their hands as Wyrd Tarot cards, once they have played an Ace on themselves.</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-cardwrap-jsvg" id="cardrewritebox40" data-mode="jsvg" data-wyrd="1" data-yarn="1"><img src="https://www.cjmoseley.dev.cc/wp-content/plugins/t13ne/images/cards-svg/2S.svg" class="t13ne-svgcard"><div class="t13ne-svgpips"><div class="t13ne-textpips">2<!-- write suit--><span class="t13ne-symbol"><span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></span><details class="t13ne-suit"><summary></summary><p class="t13ne-name"><strong>Suit: </strong>Spades <span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></p><p><strong>Ordeal Trump: </strong><span class="t13ne-trump">Attack</span></p><p class="t13ne-desc"><strong>Description: </strong>The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.</p><p class="t13ne-tarot"><strong>Tarot Meaning: </strong>Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.</p><p class="t13ne-otext"><strong>Ordeal Text: </strong>Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.</p><p class="t13ne-parry"><strong>Parry: </strong><strong>Redirect-Attack:</strong> The damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.</p><p class="t13ne-soak"><strong>Soak: </strong><strong>Counter-Attack:</strong> The matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate. </p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-svg-facet"><div>Miasma</div></div><div class="t13ne-wound"><div><strong class="t13ne-woundname">Distract</strong><details><summary></summary><strong>Distract</strong><p class="t13ne-base-pips">Base Pips to summon: 2</p><p class="t13ne-min-pips">Minimum Pips Required: 2</p><p class="t13ne-desc">The Target is distracted by the attack.</p><details class="t13ne-roll"><summary>Rules</summary><strong>-6</strong> Score on next Roll the Character makes and then Discard.</details><details class="t13ne-ordeal"><summary>Ordeal Rules</summary>Lose a Success Level <ul><li>Draw -1 Card on next Draw and then Discard.</li><li> Or <strong>-3</strong> Pips to the next Card played and Discard.</li><li>Or <strong>+3</strong> Pips to the next Attack on the Character, then Discard.</li></ul></details><details class="t13ne-psych"><summary>Psych Wounds</summary><details class="t13ne-negative"><summary>Negative Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Mild Emotional influence (tears in eyes, heightened heart rate, etc) until Stabilized / Discarded.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Must scream, swear, or otherwise express the emotion until Stabilized or Discarded.</p></details><details class="t13ne-positive"><summary>Positive Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Mild Emotional Influence (Smile, Pupil Dilation, Eyebrow twitches, etc) until Stabilised / Discarded.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Must speak, laugh, gasp, moan, tick, or otherwise express the emotion until Stabilised or Discarded.</p></details></details></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-tarot-svg"><details><summary class="t13ne-wyrdtarot"><strong> Wyrd Tarot: </strong> Reroll</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong> Normal: </strong>Reroll any dice including a Proficiency Crisis.</p><p class="t13ne-crossed"><strong> Crossed: </strong>Automatic Proficiency Crisis. You can add a Proficiency to, or create, an Annex (if you have a slot free).</p></details></div><div class="t13ne-yarn-fancy">
			<details><summary><strong>Yarn</strong> <br>The Swamp</summary>
			<span class="t13ne-description">The Swamp is a card the represents a dichotomy. The Swamp is to be feared, it is a place of corruption, decay, stagnation, and many believe it magical, for those who enter it unprepared will die, but for those that know its secret ways, recognise its dangers, then medicinal plants, herbs, leeches, and other medicines and magics are revealed. The Swamp can represent potions and medicines that are as foul-tasting as swamp-water, and yet somehow help.</span>
			<span class="t13ne-significator">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-significator"><strong>Significator</strong></summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-plot"><strong>Plot: </strong> The Swamp Plot is about corruption and usually that means business and politics. It may be about crime and sin in general, but usually this is a story of greed and power.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-char"><strong>Character: </strong> The Swamp Significator indicates a Character who is corrupt, but powerful. They Gain Yin when try to corrupt or tempt another. They Gain Yang when they act selfishly or for themselves. They Gain Chi when they demonstrate their power (use their Power or Super-Annex). They also gain +1 Success Levels when they are performing immoral or illegal actions.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-scene"><strong>Scene: </strong> The Swamp Plot is about corruption and usually that means business and politics. It may be about crime and sin in general, but usually this is a story of greed and power.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-scene">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-scene-type">
						<strong>Scene: </strong> Test
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Some Scenes are nothing but a single Test, often with a lot of discussion around them. Tests are usually a single dice roll, like a Facet Test, and usually a Test of one of the Conflict Facets. A Ref should have some idea what the Difficulty is, what will occur on a pass or a fail, and what a Stalemate would look like. Occasionally Tests may be a single roll against an Opponent (such as the Villain). You can find example Tests in the Ordeal details for a card.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
						<strong>Conflict Embodiment: </strong> Rational
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Conflict embodies each side of the Conflict into Characters who are in direct Conflict.  This is the classic Heroes verses Bad Guys plot.This may follow a period of Tension, or not. Often the Rational Embodiment creates a Monster.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>

			<span class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
						<strong>Facet Embodiment: </strong> Personality
					</summary>
					<section class="t13ne-description">The Conflict Embodies one Facet as a Character Personality. Often the most satisfying embodiment, but can lead to easy identification, and unsubtle Plots.</section>
				</details>
			</span>
			 <div class="t13ne-archetypes"><details><summary><strong>Archetype Character: </strong>Witch(-Doctor)</summary> <details class="t13ne-Persona"><summary><strong>Persona: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Vandal</span>
      </summary>
      <details class="t13ne-motivation"><summary><strong>Motivation: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Vandals (or Trolls) like nothing more than inciting other people's anger and outrage, except perhaps the beautiful music of smashing glass and crashing noises. Vandals are driven by a serious desire to smash, destroy, degrade and insult, and enjoy watching it happen as much as doing it in most cases.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-avoid"><summary><strong>Avoid: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Apologies are the bane of the Vandal's existence. Not only are they no fun, but they effectively undo all the outrage that the Vandal has caused, and they never enjoy that.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-gain-chi"><summary><strong>Gain Chi: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description"><span>Gain Chi whenever you see or hear someone, or something being attacked, insulted, mocked, or degraded in some way, even if you are the one doing it.</span><p>Ordeal gain Chi when anyone plays any card that successfully Wounds anyone, or anything, except you and your Descendants.</p></span></details></details>   <details class="t13ne-core"><summary><strong>Core: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Traveller</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">The Character has an additional Annex (1-11 Skill, 12-21 Talent, 22-26 Power) that grants an extra mode of Travel. E.g. Fleet-footed Skill, Flying Talent or a Lea Walking Power.</span></details><details class="t13ne-hitch"><summary><strong>Hitch: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Offensive</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Some people are just rude, or overly competitive, others are actually bigoted, or prejudiced. Sometimes it can be how people behave, other times the offence can be to the senses.</span></details><p class="t13ne-description">The Witch (or less commonly Witch-Doctor) is a often a bitter outcast from society, who has learned the ways of the swamp, and the medicines found there. Sometimes they may be persuaded, paid or manipulated into helping others with their knowledge, often though they use their gifts to selfish ends. The Witch(-Doctor) is often accused of being able to fly or astral project themselves, and certainly can use herbs and plants to undergo psychotropic journeys. Some will have a Shaman Persona instead and be better socially adjusted.</p></details></div>
			 <span class="t13ne-hook">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-hook">
						<strong>Hook: </strong> What's That Smell?
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Story Hooks the Characters with a direct warning and a question, this is usually what's that smell? But it can be What's that noise, taste or weird light, just as easily. In any case the answer is usually a corpse, occasionally a Monster, and just occasionally a broken sewer.</aside>
					<details class="t13ne-hook-aspect">
						<summary><strong>Hook Aspect: </strong>Hitches</summary>
						<section class="t13ne-description">The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.</section>
					</details>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-obstacle-fancy"><details class="t13ne-obstacle"><summary><strong>Obstacle:</strong>Repulsive</summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way ahead is repulsive, icky or disgusting. A Swamp, sewer, river of blood, pile of manure, shower of vomit. Strong nerves, stronger stomachs and the strongest immune systems will be required.</p></details></span>
			<span class="t13ne-gain">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-gain">
						<strong>Gain </strong> 
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Dark Territory or Dark Matter Descendant</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-fray">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-fray">
						<strong>Fray: </strong> Quagmire
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">A Quagmire is a sticky morass or corruption which the PCs find themselves engulfed by. Quagmires can be literal swamps, so called Quicksand, and so on, or more likely they can be sticky situations, economic quagmires are rising debts, negative equity and falling wages, political quagmires are usually the opposite, with money from all sorts of dubious sources flooding in, who only want small concessions and favours. Either way, the PCs will need to extricate themselves somehow.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-snag">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-snag">
						<strong>Snag: </strong> Unique Situation
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Snag adds the complication to the Fray that the situation or Location is truly unique. It might be that the ingredients for the ritual required grow only in one place, that must be visited, or that the nightclub has the only indoor grass dance floor in the city. It is usual for the unique situation to either threaten the PCs or be threatened by the Opposed Embodiments.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-sweeping">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-sweeping">
						<strong>Sweeping: </strong> Shower Scene
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Shower Scene may not take place in a Shower or Bath, but represents the PCs trying to scrub free whatever they feel still clings to them. This may be some mud, or it could be an emotional taint. The Sweeping Scene allows the Character to treat their own wounds (especially Psych Wounds) with some ease (Halved Difficulty usually).</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
		 <details class="t13ne-revelation-fancy"><summary><strong>Revelation</strong></summary><details class="t13ne-about"><summary><strong>About: </strong> Dominant </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information revealed is about the Dominant Conflict Embodiment. Sometimes they are Characters, but almost as often they are Locations, Quests, Ordeals, or similar. Where this is true the Revelation should reveal details about future Quests the Pressed Characters must complete, or places they should visit. The Revelation can be little more than a call-back or foreshadowing if handled correct.</p></details><details class="t13ne-info"><summary><strong>Info: </strong> Corruption </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Corruption can range from a little bribery to actual demonic influences, and everything in between. Finding out about corruption is easy, although rarely will you get the details.</p></details><details class="t13ne-vector"><summary><strong>Vector: </strong> Scent </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Scents can tell us a lot, reminding us of places, people, animals and of course events. The lingering scent of gun-smoke or fire can pass more than enough information for action.</p></details><details class="t13ne-alternate"><summary><strong>Alternate: </strong> Influences </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information is about the influences that are working in the plot, or on the Character. These might be just the politics of their social media friends, the meddlings of some Seelie Noble, or the machinations of an Increated, you just never know.</p></details><details class="t13ne-detail"><summary><strong>Detail: </strong> Critical </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Critical details are either spotting flaws in an otherwise perfect planet killer space base, or a critique that one can use to improve one's performance.</p></details></details>
		 <details class="t13ne-ordeal-fancy"><summary><strong>Ordeal</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-ordeal-type"><strong>Ordeal-Type </strong> — Opposed Ordeal</p><p class="t13ne-stages"><strong>Stages </strong> — 4</p><p class="t13ne-ordeal-spread"><strong>Ordeal-Spread </strong> — Railroad</p><p class="t13ne-stakes"><strong>Stakes </strong> — Low</p><p class="t13ne-test"><strong>Test </strong> — Floating (Gossamer)</p><p class="t13ne-obstacles"><strong>Obstacles </strong> — 1 Obstacle in a Court Stage</p><p class="t13ne-suggested-action"><strong>Suggested-Action </strong> — Float / Swim</p><details class="t13ne-stage"><summary><strong>Stage: </strong> Broken glass </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The route ahead is dangerous, littered with sharp stones, broken glass, or even landmines.</p></details><details class="t13ne-motional"><summary><strong>Motional: </strong> Ground Vehicle Flee </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Hit the gas! They're right behind us!</p></details><details class="t13ne-fight"><summary><strong>Fight: </strong> Scare </summary><p class="t13ne-description">A Scare is a sudden Psych attack (such as a Duck attack), from a Vex or Chorus Monster that quickly retreats. <!-- t13ne-shortcode "%7B%22type%22%3A%22facetsuitaspect%22%2C%22suit%22%3A%224%22%2C%22aspect%22%3A%22Monster%22%7D"// --></p><div class="t13ne-tablewrap"><table class="t13ne-table Monster"><thead><tr><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</th><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Gossamer</td><td><strong>Wiry</strong> —The Monster is immensely physically powerful, although often its form may not reveal it. It often demonstrates supernatural power as well as strength and toughness. A small, strong Goblin, a robot that looks like a little girl, a cybernetically enhanced rat, or even a killer doll are all examples of a Gossamer Monster. The Wiry Monster can refill Gossamer Score cards to its Monster Pool whenever it is underestimated. A Wiry monster may add their current Monster Pool size to any Score or Pips to do with their Monstrous strength or toughness. The Monster must spend at least one card from their Monster Pool when they physically attack.</td></tr><tr><td>Inertia</td><td><strong>Unstoppable</strong> —The Monster is inexorable, it does not stop and cannot be killed by any normal means. They start play with a Monster Pool of Inertia Score cards. Unstoppable Monsters can ignore a number of Wounds equal to the number of cards in their Monster Pool. Wounds stacked beyond the Monster Pool affect them as normal. Inexorable Monsters can also ignore all Hitches with a Boon less than their Inertia Score while they have cards in their Monster Pool. Whenever an Unstoppable Monster is actually damaged it must spend cards from its Monster Pool while remaining motionless for Inertia Score as Chi Extend Duration to discard all Wounds less than Mortal in nature, and Stabilise all Mortal + Wounds (which may then be ignored if the Unstoppable has enough cards remaining in its Monster Pool). Examples of the Inertial Monster type include the Hungry Dead (Zombies) and most robotic assassins (especially ones from the future).</td></tr><tr><td>Key</td><td><strong>Hunter</strong> —The Monster is a hunter or tracker, it should favour harrying attacks and hit and run tactics. Typically such a Monster will vanish or flee if it is wounded, only to reappear when healed. The hunter may be fooled by false trails, but never for long. Hunters may add Key Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they spot a track, clue, or similar, of the subject of their hunt. Hunters receive a bonus equal to the number of cards in the Monster Pool to any tracking, attacking or hunting actions if they also spend a card from the Monster Pool on the action. Hunters are usually immune to Carnage and Mortal Wounds until the Zenith of the story (such wounds are taken as Crippling Wounds instead, but at a cost of one card from the Monster Pool). In T13, the archetypal version of this Monster is a Bulmäs Pack member from the Wild Hunt, but any Werewolf or Animal Shifter would probably qualify, as would robot cowboys in theme parks that have gone wrong, and any fantasy world's Elven ranger. </td></tr><tr><td>Miasma</td><td><strong>Mutant</strong> —The monster is a mutant or chimerical creature made from parts of others. This monster type is the oldest perhaps, you’ll find examples throughout the ancient world, particularly in Greek myth. The Mutant will often be larger, and may be malformed or unsymmetrical, with odd numbers of limbs, eyes, horns, or even heads. The Chimera will blend multiple animals together to create a new creature that combines features of all the “parent” creatures, preserving the best of all species. Mutant Monsters start with Miasma Score Monster Pool. They add Monster Pool Size as the Boon of Proficiency less "Mutation" Annex that they can use to grow larger or mimic their “parents” abilities. 1950s B-movie monsters are often of this type, giant ants, lizards, spiders, wasps, and even rabbits have been used. Classically of course, Cyclopses, Centaurs, Minotaurs, Satyrs, Gryphons (or Griffins), hippogriffs, dragons, sphinxes, manticores and of course the Chimera itself are all examples. You can also find examples in superheroes, especially those created by radioactive arachnids, or those with an X-gene.</td></tr><tr><td>Trial</td><td><strong>Killer</strong> —The Monster is a deadly Combat specialist of some sort. Killers add Trial Draw cards to their Monster Pool each time they kill someone. Killers automatically add +1 Score/Pip per card of Monster Pool to any attack, usually as Penetration or Range costs. Killers must spend Monster Pool cards whenever they will make a Trick. Killers can be drug-addled assassins, crazed shooters, or just steel-souled soldiers.</td></tr><tr><td>Yonder</td><td><strong>Alien</strong> —The Monster is an alien, an outsider. It may just be a metaphor for alienation, or literally be an extra-(or ultra)-terrestrial. Such monsters are usually obvious and inhuman, not just to look at, but in the way they think and behave. They have apparently supernatural powers and abilities, but often make do with advanced technologies (in the form of additional Descendants). They may draw and play Yonder Draw cards to and from their Monster Pool with their alien / inhuman / weird actions. Their Monster Pool number of cards Reduced adds Success Levels to any action to do with motion, survival or the alien's technologies. From little grey Eusaiveans, through to pod people and pepper-pot shaped engines of hate, all the way to face-hugging xenomorphic space-wasps, there are plenty of aliens.</td></tr></tbody><tfoot><tr><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</td><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</td></tr></tfoot></table></div><p></p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Red/Purple Carnivore</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> Hunters, Broccen, Vermis, Labyrinthine, All-Walkers</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> Microfauna</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong> Bacteria / Viral paths</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> Microscopic viral and bacterial paths, often overlooked.</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-cardwrap-jsvg" id="cardrewritebox41" data-mode="jsvg" data-wyrd="1" data-yarn="1"><img src="https://www.cjmoseley.dev.cc/wp-content/plugins/t13ne/images/cards-svg/3S.svg" class="t13ne-svgcard"><div class="t13ne-svgpips"><div class="t13ne-textpips">3<!-- write suit--><span class="t13ne-symbol"><span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></span><details class="t13ne-suit"><summary></summary><p class="t13ne-name"><strong>Suit: </strong>Spades <span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></p><p><strong>Ordeal Trump: </strong><span class="t13ne-trump">Attack</span></p><p class="t13ne-desc"><strong>Description: </strong>The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.</p><p class="t13ne-tarot"><strong>Tarot Meaning: </strong>Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.</p><p class="t13ne-otext"><strong>Ordeal Text: </strong>Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.</p><p class="t13ne-parry"><strong>Parry: </strong><strong>Redirect-Attack:</strong> The damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.</p><p class="t13ne-soak"><strong>Soak: </strong><strong>Counter-Attack:</strong> The matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate. </p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-svg-facet"><div>Key</div></div><div class="t13ne-wound"><div><strong class="t13ne-woundname">Distract</strong><details><summary></summary><strong>Distract</strong><p class="t13ne-base-pips">Base Pips to summon: 2</p><p class="t13ne-min-pips">Minimum Pips Required: 2</p><p class="t13ne-desc">The Target is distracted by the attack.</p><details class="t13ne-roll"><summary>Rules</summary><strong>-6</strong> Score on next Roll the Character makes and then Discard.</details><details class="t13ne-ordeal"><summary>Ordeal Rules</summary>Lose a Success Level <ul><li>Draw -1 Card on next Draw and then Discard.</li><li> Or <strong>-3</strong> Pips to the next Card played and Discard.</li><li>Or <strong>+3</strong> Pips to the next Attack on the Character, then Discard.</li></ul></details><details class="t13ne-psych"><summary>Psych Wounds</summary><details class="t13ne-negative"><summary>Negative Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Mild Emotional influence (tears in eyes, heightened heart rate, etc) until Stabilized / Discarded.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Must scream, swear, or otherwise express the emotion until Stabilized or Discarded.</p></details><details class="t13ne-positive"><summary>Positive Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Mild Emotional Influence (Smile, Pupil Dilation, Eyebrow twitches, etc) until Stabilised / Discarded.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Must speak, laugh, gasp, moan, tick, or otherwise express the emotion until Stabilised or Discarded.</p></details></details></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-tarot-svg"><details><summary class="t13ne-wyrdtarot"><strong> Wyrd Tarot: </strong> Breach</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong> Normal: </strong>Adds +10 Penetration Pips to an Attack card in play.</p><p class="t13ne-crossed"><strong> Crossed: </strong>Discards all cards of an incoming attack</p></details></div><div class="t13ne-yarn-fancy">
			<details><summary><strong>Yarn</strong> <br>The Key</summary>
			<span class="t13ne-description">The Key card is representative of a key itself, but also what a key can do, opening, unlocking, or revealing something that was locked away. The Key is not normally associated with the act of locking away again, except when the phrase "throw away the key" is used. It most often represents liberation, escape, or discovery.</span>
			<span class="t13ne-significator">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-significator"><strong>Significator</strong></summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-plot"><strong>Plot: </strong> The Key Plot is a plot to find something essential (the key to the Plot). Usually Key Plots are more puzzle lead treasure hunts rather than combat filled infiltrations, but both are possible.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-char"><strong>Character: </strong> The Key Significator indicates a Character who has a keen mind and nimble fingers. Gain Yin when they try to work on a puzzle or lock. They Gain Yang when they use tools or gadgets. They Gain Chi when they open a door, read an encoded message, or complete a puzzle. They gain +1 Success Levels to all actions that require dexterity or curiosity.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-scene"><strong>Scene: </strong> The Key Plot is a plot to find something essential (the key to the Plot). Usually Key Plots are more puzzle lead treasure hunts rather than combat filled infiltrations, but both are possible.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-scene">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-scene-type">
						<strong>Scene: </strong> Ordeal
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Ordeals test Characters in a complex way (more than a single dice roll or a Facet Test). Ordeals can range from Low Stakes Ordeals (like doing the Crossword), to High Stakes Ordeals, such as solving a crossword puzzle, while exchanging gunfire with enemies, during a car chase. Action generally, and Fights in particular, involve an Ordeal. Ordeals are usually created by playing a number of cards to define everything about the Ordeal (from the Type, Stakes, Number of Obstacles you should draw and add, to a suggested action that could be taken, or a description of a Stage).</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
						<strong>Conflict Embodiment: </strong> Rational
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Conflict embodies each side of the Conflict into Characters who are in direct Conflict.  This is the classic Heroes verses Bad Guys plot.This may follow a period of Tension, or not. Often the Rational Embodiment creates a Monster.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>

			<span class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
						<strong>Facet Embodiment: </strong> Core
					</summary>
					<section class="t13ne-description">The Conflict Embodies one Facet as a Character Core. This can create very satisfying embodiments that can be suitable for most Genres.</section>
				</details>
			</span>
			 <div class="t13ne-archetypes"><details><summary><strong>Archetype Character: </strong>Inquisitor</summary> <details class="t13ne-Persona"><summary><strong>Persona: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Sleuth</span>
      </summary>
      <details class="t13ne-motivation"><summary><strong>Motivation: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Sleuths love to solve mysteries. Nothing gets a Sleuth motivated like a good puzzle for them to solve. It may not be a crime, or even a scientific mystery, some sleuths just love a daily crossword, and working on it in breaks will keep them motivated throughout the day.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-avoid"><summary><strong>Avoid: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Sleuths avoid boredom like the plague. Their minds just cannot stand going without a challenge for even a few days. They may not be workaholics, but they will quickly find something to do even on vacation.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-gain-chi"><summary><strong>Gain Chi: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description"><span>Gain Chi when you ask or answer a question, look for clues, penetrate defences, or solve something (puzzle / equation/ mystery).</span><p>Ordeal gain Chi when you play a ♠ to prepare</p></span></details></details>   <details class="t13ne-core"><summary><strong>Core: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Barber</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">The character can move Wounds from one Character or Descendant to another with a cost equal to the Pips in Chi. This cost can be lowered by also moving a Wound the other way.</span></details><details class="t13ne-hitch"><summary><strong>Hitch: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Malformed</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Mutations, missing limbs, or just plain ugly.</span></details><p class="t13ne-description">Inquisitors are masters of asking questions and keeping the interrogated threatened and living. Some favour torture, usually with a strong psychological component, others rely on making friends with their subject, master Inquisitors use even more advanced techniques.</p></details></div>
			 <span class="t13ne-hook">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-hook">
						<strong>Hook: </strong> The Key To The Kingdom
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Conflict Hooks the Character with a promise of an incredible treasure or power. Alternatively the Hook may be a literal key, perhaps to a Location that has come into the PCs possession.</aside>
					<details class="t13ne-hook-aspect">
						<summary><strong>Hook Aspect: </strong>Hitches</summary>
						<section class="t13ne-description">The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.</section>
					</details>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-obstacle-fancy"><details class="t13ne-obstacle"><summary><strong>Obstacle:</strong>Lock</summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way ahead is blocked by a Lock, you will require a key, pass-code, or lock-picking skills to unlock the way.</p></details></span>
			<span class="t13ne-gain">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-gain">
						<strong>Gain </strong> 
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">A Clue or Key to an Obstacle or Location</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-fray">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-fray">
						<strong>Fray: </strong> The Lock
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Fray is about either opening or closing a lock. It may be a door, vault, safe, computer, chest or something more experimental that needs locking or unlocking. Usually you will require the correct key, password or codebook, or you will have to pick or solve the lock as a Puzzle. The Lock is usually a Solitary Low-Medium Stakes Ordeal, with at least one Obstacle, however storming a keep to drop the drawbridge and raise the portcullis would be a high or extreme Stakes Opposed Motional Ordeal.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-snag">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-snag">
						<strong>Snag: </strong> Crux
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Fray is made more complicated by being the key to the whole Story. This is the crux of the problem, and as such should have higher Stakes (with black twos wild), and an appearance of the Villain or their forces. If this crucial Fray is lost it must be returned to somehow later.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-sweeping">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-sweeping">
						<strong>Sweeping: </strong> Lock-In
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The PCs find somewhere safe and then secure the doors, by locking, bolting or barricading themselves in. They may rest easy inside for a while, especially if its after-hours in a country pub, not so much if its a supply cupboard during a terrorist incident.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
		 <details class="t13ne-revelation-fancy"><summary><strong>Revelation</strong></summary><details class="t13ne-about"><summary><strong>About: </strong> Dominant </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information revealed is about the Dominant Conflict Embodiment. Sometimes they are Characters, but almost as often they are Locations, Quests, Ordeals, or similar. Where this is true the Revelation should reveal details about future Quests the Pressed Characters must complete, or places they should visit. The Revelation can be little more than a call-back or foreshadowing if handled correct.</p></details><details class="t13ne-info"><summary><strong>Info: </strong> Aspects </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Information about an exploitable weakness or a warning about a strength.</p></details><details class="t13ne-vector"><summary><strong>Vector: </strong> Flashback </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information is revealed via a Flashback Scene. This might be played out, or just narrated, depending on how far back and how well planned the Ref is.</p></details><details class="t13ne-alternate"><summary><strong>Alternate: </strong> Sorrows </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information is about sad things. Perhaps it details how the young orphan was passed from abusive home to abusive home, until finally he was taken in by a clan of thieves. Or perhaps it details how an Assassin's first contract was his childhood friend. Or how much they loved that dog they had to shoot when it got rabid.</p></details><details class="t13ne-detail"><summary><strong>Detail: </strong> Woes </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Some people don't just have problems, they have Woes. Huge Hitches in some cases, when they create Demons, who are like Mercari but with Twisted abilities, but for others, their woes just huge mistakes that the Embodiment wishes it could undo.</p></details></details>
		 <details class="t13ne-ordeal-fancy"><summary><strong>Ordeal</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-ordeal-type"><strong>Ordeal-Type </strong> — Opposed Ordeal</p><p class="t13ne-stages"><strong>Stages </strong> — 4</p><p class="t13ne-ordeal-spread"><strong>Ordeal-Spread </strong> — Forking</p><p class="t13ne-stakes"><strong>Stakes </strong> — Medium</p><p class="t13ne-test"><strong>Test </strong> — Staying-power (Inertia)</p><p class="t13ne-obstacles"><strong>Obstacles </strong> — 1 Obstacle in a Court Stage</p><p class="t13ne-suggested-action"><strong>Suggested-Action </strong> — Balance</p><details class="t13ne-stage"><summary><strong>Stage: </strong> Balance </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way ahead is narrow, perhaps a thin, mountain-goat path, or a plank across a gap. Maybe the route is across ceiling beams or the girders of the top of a box-bridge.</p></details><details class="t13ne-motional"><summary><strong>Motional: </strong> Air Vehicle Flee </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Foxtrot-Bravo-Two you have Echo-Charlie on your Six, accelerate to point Seven and try to shake them.</p></details><details class="t13ne-fight"><summary><strong>Fight: </strong> 3 Way Battle </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The Fight is between 3 equal and unaligned forces that meet in a Square, Field, or other mostly open area.</p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Red/Purple Carnivore</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> Hunters, Broccen, Vermis, Labyrinthine, All-Walkers</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> Meiofauna</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong> Small Nematode worms</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> A lot of parasite carnivore paths at this level</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-cardwrap-jsvg" id="cardrewritebox42" data-mode="jsvg" data-wyrd="1" data-yarn="1"><img src="https://www.cjmoseley.dev.cc/wp-content/plugins/t13ne/images/cards-svg/4S.svg" class="t13ne-svgcard"><div class="t13ne-svgpips"><div class="t13ne-textpips">4<!-- write suit--><span class="t13ne-symbol"><span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></span><details class="t13ne-suit"><summary></summary><p class="t13ne-name"><strong>Suit: </strong>Spades <span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></p><p><strong>Ordeal Trump: </strong><span class="t13ne-trump">Attack</span></p><p class="t13ne-desc"><strong>Description: </strong>The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.</p><p class="t13ne-tarot"><strong>Tarot Meaning: </strong>Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.</p><p class="t13ne-otext"><strong>Ordeal Text: </strong>Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.</p><p class="t13ne-parry"><strong>Parry: </strong><strong>Redirect-Attack:</strong> The damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.</p><p class="t13ne-soak"><strong>Soak: </strong><strong>Counter-Attack:</strong> The matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate. </p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-svg-facet"><div>Yonder</div></div><div class="t13ne-wound"><div><strong class="t13ne-woundname">Distract</strong><details><summary></summary><strong>Distract</strong><p class="t13ne-base-pips">Base Pips to summon: 2</p><p class="t13ne-min-pips">Minimum Pips Required: 2</p><p class="t13ne-desc">The Target is distracted by the attack.</p><details class="t13ne-roll"><summary>Rules</summary><strong>-6</strong> Score on next Roll the Character makes and then Discard.</details><details class="t13ne-ordeal"><summary>Ordeal Rules</summary>Lose a Success Level <ul><li>Draw -1 Card on next Draw and then Discard.</li><li> Or <strong>-3</strong> Pips to the next Card played and Discard.</li><li>Or <strong>+3</strong> Pips to the next Attack on the Character, then Discard.</li></ul></details><details class="t13ne-psych"><summary>Psych Wounds</summary><details class="t13ne-negative"><summary>Negative Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Mild Emotional influence (tears in eyes, heightened heart rate, etc) until Stabilized / Discarded.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Must scream, swear, or otherwise express the emotion until Stabilized or Discarded.</p></details><details class="t13ne-positive"><summary>Positive Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Mild Emotional Influence (Smile, Pupil Dilation, Eyebrow twitches, etc) until Stabilised / Discarded.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Must speak, laugh, gasp, moan, tick, or otherwise express the emotion until Stabilised or Discarded.</p></details></details></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-tarot-svg"><details><summary class="t13ne-wyrdtarot"><strong> Wyrd Tarot: </strong> Enrage</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong> Normal: </strong>Automatic Failure of any social action (they won't believe the truth).</p><p class="t13ne-crossed"><strong> Crossed: </strong>Automatic Success on any social action (they believe the lie).</p></details></div><div class="t13ne-yarn-fancy">
			<details><summary><strong>Yarn</strong> <br>The Tower</summary>
			<span class="t13ne-description">The Tower card can represent physical buildings, places, and stability. Less commonly, in the form a Wizard's or Watchtower it may represent research, viewing, observation or looking-out. The Tower is a stable landmark, not prone to collapse, except when struck by lightning, which happens often before the lightning conductor, or earthquake, which is considered an act of the gods before the modern day also. The card has a hint of divine retribution about this meaning, which is why it may be called the Tower Struck By Lightning in negative positions.</span>
			<span class="t13ne-significator">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-significator"><strong>Significator</strong></summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-plot"><strong>Plot: </strong> The Tower Plot is one that builds up piece by piece. Such a Plot doesn't begin as a Cycle or Epic, it starts small, with a Single Story. The Plot will return, each time promoted, over and over until it dominates the whole Story. Such Plots reflect the emergence of new cultures, new technologies and new societies, it is also useful in world-building as each Story builds upon the last occurrence until the new world is defined.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-char"><strong>Character: </strong> The Tower Significator indicates a Character who is a prominent figure of their society. They Gain Yin when they speak calmly, suggest an alternative option, or prioritize problems. They Gain Yang when they keep silent, agree with another or organize assistance. Gain Chi when they demonstrate physical prowess. They gain +1 Success Levels when they perform physical actions (firing a gun doesn't count).</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-scene"><strong>Scene: </strong> The Tower Plot is one that builds up piece by piece. Such a Plot doesn't begin as a Cycle or Epic, it starts small, with a Single Story. The Plot will return, each time promoted, over and over until it dominates the whole Story. Such Plots reflect the emergence of new cultures, new technologies and new societies, it is also useful in world-building as each Story builds upon the last occurrence until the new world is defined.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-scene">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-scene-type">
						<strong>Scene: </strong> Gain
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Gains give something to one of the Characters to help move the Story on. Sometimes this might be payment for a job they did, or it might be a discovery that will directly connect to the Story. This may be information. It is normal to play a separate card to define the actual Gain that is made.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
						<strong>Conflict Embodiment: </strong> Rational
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Conflict embodies each side of the Conflict into Characters who are in direct Conflict.  This is the classic Heroes verses Bad Guys plot.This may follow a period of Tension, or not. Often the Rational Embodiment creates a Monster.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>

			<span class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
						<strong>Facet Embodiment: </strong> Hitch
					</summary>
					<section class="t13ne-description">The Conflict Embodies one Facet as a Character Hitch. This can create quite secretive and subtle embodiments that can be very difficult to spot. Other times Hitch Embodiments can be very obvious, and in the case of Woes, glaring.</section>
				</details>
			</span>
			 <div class="t13ne-archetypes"><details><summary><strong>Archetype Character: </strong>Privileged</summary> <details class="t13ne-Persona"><summary><strong>Persona: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Sloth</span>
      </summary>
      <details class="t13ne-motivation"><summary><strong>Motivation: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Nothing but laziness motivates the Sloth. They'd rather rest in bed, and avoid the situation if they can. If they are pressed into action, you can be sure they will do the bare minimum that they can get away with, before taking another break.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-avoid"><summary><strong>Avoid: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Work, especially the hard, or boring kind.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-gain-chi"><summary><strong>Gain Chi: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description"><span>Gain Chi when you succeed with the minimum of effort, get someone else to do it, or have a rest.</span><p>Ordeal gain Chi when you take half-Actions, rather than full.</p></span></details></details>   <details class="t13ne-core"><summary><strong>Core: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Poser</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Gain Chi whenever you recite a catchphrase, cliche, or strike a dramatic pose.</span></details><details class="t13ne-hitch"><summary><strong>Hitch: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Stagnation</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Things get old, battered, run down, ill, corrupt and slow. the longer something is left the more stagnant it becomes. This Hitch is for all those old things.</span></details><p class="t13ne-description">The Privileged are the high-born, indolent class, who live in fine towers. They live lives of boredom and often corruption, although some may find other hobbies and activities.</p></details></div>
			 <span class="t13ne-hook">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-hook">
						<strong>Hook: </strong> Lookout
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Story opens with a Lookout or Scout bringing news, of an impending threat, this might be "Land Ho!", or "Smoke!" or "Three Thousand Orcs and Ogres are marching South along the King's road. They will be here in two days, maybe less." or the PCs themselves may be the one's to see the threat, and have to raise the alarm.</aside>
					<details class="t13ne-hook-aspect">
						<summary><strong>Hook Aspect: </strong>Hitches</summary>
						<section class="t13ne-description">The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.</section>
					</details>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-obstacle-fancy"><details class="t13ne-obstacle"><summary><strong>Obstacle:</strong>Tower</summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way is blocked by a building, wall or tower. There may be guards, locked doors, or similar, but you have to go through, around, or over this obstacle.</p></details></span>
			<span class="t13ne-gain">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-gain">
						<strong>Gain </strong> 
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Location (often Real Estate or a Province where appropriate)</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-fray">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-fray">
						<strong>Fray: </strong> Tower Struck By Lightning
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">A terrible, traumatic, life changing, paradigm shifting event. Usually a disaster of this magnitude is a Force of Nature Extra (at least), in an Ordeal that has High-Paradoxical Stakes. It is common for new Hitches to result from this Fray, ideally that will deepen the hooks of the Conflict in the PCs, perhaps even causing them to internalise the actual Conflict within themselves.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-snag">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-snag">
						<strong>Snag: </strong> Hold!
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Fray ends / leads to a situation where the Embodiment must hold position against a force of lesser opponents (usually Vex and Chorus Extras) that outnumber the Embodiment and Allies. Usually this Snag Ordeal will have a limited duration, with either reinforcements arriving, or some extraction team en-route, or perhaps the enemies will stop with dawn's light, or at nightfall. Otherwise, the Vexes will come in waves, with rest periods in between.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-sweeping">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-sweeping">
						<strong>Sweeping: </strong> Stability
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The PCs have a stable situation for a while (this may be weeks or months)</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
		 <details class="t13ne-revelation-fancy"><summary><strong>Revelation</strong></summary><details class="t13ne-about"><summary><strong>About: </strong> Dominant </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information revealed is about the Dominant Conflict Embodiment. Sometimes they are Characters, but almost as often they are Locations, Quests, Ordeals, or similar. Where this is true the Revelation should reveal details about future Quests the Pressed Characters must complete, or places they should visit. The Revelation can be little more than a call-back or foreshadowing if handled correct.</p></details><details class="t13ne-info"><summary><strong>Info: </strong> Conclusions </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The available information points to a number of potential conclusions. Usually only one of them is true.</p></details><details class="t13ne-vector"><summary><strong>Vector: </strong> Vision </summary><p class="t13ne-description">A Hallucination that reveals information is common enough that it gets a mention. Of course, what we don't hear about are all the times hallucinations aren\t useful. </p></details><details class="t13ne-alternate"><summary><strong>Alternate: </strong> Safety </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information is about a place, or time where either Embodiment will feel, or be safe. If you need a safe space, this tells you where to find one, if you have a safe space, it tells you where your Opposed Embodiment feels safe.</p></details><details class="t13ne-detail"><summary><strong>Detail: </strong> Legal </summary><p class="t13ne-description">A Legal detail can be a point of law, a loophole, or miscarriage of justice that can be exploited somehow. You may not have the villain's address, but thanks to this restraining order you know who his lawyers are...</p></details></details>
		 <details class="t13ne-ordeal-fancy"><summary><strong>Ordeal</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-ordeal-type"><strong>Ordeal-Type </strong> — Opposed Ordeal</p><p class="t13ne-stages"><strong>Stages </strong> — 4</p><p class="t13ne-ordeal-spread"><strong>Ordeal-Spread </strong> — Pyramid</p><p class="t13ne-stakes"><strong>Stakes </strong> — High</p><p class="t13ne-test"><strong>Test </strong> — Sliding (Inertia)</p><p class="t13ne-obstacles"><strong>Obstacles </strong> — 1 Obstacle in a Court Stage</p><p class="t13ne-suggested-action"><strong>Suggested-Action </strong> — Slide</p><details class="t13ne-stage"><summary><strong>Stage: </strong> Slick </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way ahead is slick, perhaps a recently polished floor, or something has been spilt, like oil. Or maybe the ground is icy.</p></details><details class="t13ne-motional"><summary><strong>Motional: </strong> Ground Vehicle Flee </summary><p class="t13ne-description">They're right on top of us, take the wheel and keep down, I need to shoot back. Oh, try not to crash, yeah?</p></details><details class="t13ne-fight"><summary><strong>Fight: </strong> Infiltration </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The Fight is a Stealth mission, to penetrate a secure facility, achieve some goal (often an assassination or theft) and then escape. Opponents are usually Cast Extras (security guards) although they may be Chorus Monsters.<!-- t13ne-shortcode "%7B%22type%22%3A%22facetsuitaspect%22%2C%22suit%22%3A%224%22%2C%22aspect%22%3A%22Monster%22%7D"// --></p><div class="t13ne-tablewrap"><table class="t13ne-table Monster"><thead><tr><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</th><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Gossamer</td><td><strong>Wiry</strong> —The Monster is immensely physically powerful, although often its form may not reveal it. It often demonstrates supernatural power as well as strength and toughness. A small, strong Goblin, a robot that looks like a little girl, a cybernetically enhanced rat, or even a killer doll are all examples of a Gossamer Monster. The Wiry Monster can refill Gossamer Score cards to its Monster Pool whenever it is underestimated. A Wiry monster may add their current Monster Pool size to any Score or Pips to do with their Monstrous strength or toughness. The Monster must spend at least one card from their Monster Pool when they physically attack.</td></tr><tr><td>Inertia</td><td><strong>Unstoppable</strong> —The Monster is inexorable, it does not stop and cannot be killed by any normal means. They start play with a Monster Pool of Inertia Score cards. Unstoppable Monsters can ignore a number of Wounds equal to the number of cards in their Monster Pool. Wounds stacked beyond the Monster Pool affect them as normal. Inexorable Monsters can also ignore all Hitches with a Boon less than their Inertia Score while they have cards in their Monster Pool. Whenever an Unstoppable Monster is actually damaged it must spend cards from its Monster Pool while remaining motionless for Inertia Score as Chi Extend Duration to discard all Wounds less than Mortal in nature, and Stabilise all Mortal + Wounds (which may then be ignored if the Unstoppable has enough cards remaining in its Monster Pool). Examples of the Inertial Monster type include the Hungry Dead (Zombies) and most robotic assassins (especially ones from the future).</td></tr><tr><td>Key</td><td><strong>Hunter</strong> —The Monster is a hunter or tracker, it should favour harrying attacks and hit and run tactics. Typically such a Monster will vanish or flee if it is wounded, only to reappear when healed. The hunter may be fooled by false trails, but never for long. Hunters may add Key Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they spot a track, clue, or similar, of the subject of their hunt. Hunters receive a bonus equal to the number of cards in the Monster Pool to any tracking, attacking or hunting actions if they also spend a card from the Monster Pool on the action. Hunters are usually immune to Carnage and Mortal Wounds until the Zenith of the story (such wounds are taken as Crippling Wounds instead, but at a cost of one card from the Monster Pool). In T13, the archetypal version of this Monster is a Bulmäs Pack member from the Wild Hunt, but any Werewolf or Animal Shifter would probably qualify, as would robot cowboys in theme parks that have gone wrong, and any fantasy world's Elven ranger. </td></tr><tr><td>Miasma</td><td><strong>Mutant</strong> —The monster is a mutant or chimerical creature made from parts of others. This monster type is the oldest perhaps, you’ll find examples throughout the ancient world, particularly in Greek myth. The Mutant will often be larger, and may be malformed or unsymmetrical, with odd numbers of limbs, eyes, horns, or even heads. The Chimera will blend multiple animals together to create a new creature that combines features of all the “parent” creatures, preserving the best of all species. Mutant Monsters start with Miasma Score Monster Pool. They add Monster Pool Size as the Boon of Proficiency less "Mutation" Annex that they can use to grow larger or mimic their “parents” abilities. 1950s B-movie monsters are often of this type, giant ants, lizards, spiders, wasps, and even rabbits have been used. Classically of course, Cyclopses, Centaurs, Minotaurs, Satyrs, Gryphons (or Griffins), hippogriffs, dragons, sphinxes, manticores and of course the Chimera itself are all examples. You can also find examples in superheroes, especially those created by radioactive arachnids, or those with an X-gene.</td></tr><tr><td>Trial</td><td><strong>Killer</strong> —The Monster is a deadly Combat specialist of some sort. Killers add Trial Draw cards to their Monster Pool each time they kill someone. Killers automatically add +1 Score/Pip per card of Monster Pool to any attack, usually as Penetration or Range costs. Killers must spend Monster Pool cards whenever they will make a Trick. Killers can be drug-addled assassins, crazed shooters, or just steel-souled soldiers.</td></tr><tr><td>Yonder</td><td><strong>Alien</strong> —The Monster is an alien, an outsider. It may just be a metaphor for alienation, or literally be an extra-(or ultra)-terrestrial. Such monsters are usually obvious and inhuman, not just to look at, but in the way they think and behave. They have apparently supernatural powers and abilities, but often make do with advanced technologies (in the form of additional Descendants). They may draw and play Yonder Draw cards to and from their Monster Pool with their alien / inhuman / weird actions. Their Monster Pool number of cards Reduced adds Success Levels to any action to do with motion, survival or the alien's technologies. From little grey Eusaiveans, through to pod people and pepper-pot shaped engines of hate, all the way to face-hugging xenomorphic space-wasps, there are plenty of aliens.</td></tr></tbody><tfoot><tr><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</td><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</td></tr></tfoot></table></div><p></p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Red/Purple Carnivore</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> Hunters, Broccen, Vermis, Labyrinthine, All-Walkers</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> Mesofauna</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong> Midge / Flea / Bugs</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> Tiny Arthropods usually with biting mouth parts</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-cardwrap-jsvg" id="cardrewritebox43" data-mode="jsvg" data-wyrd="1" data-yarn="1"><img src="https://www.cjmoseley.dev.cc/wp-content/plugins/t13ne/images/cards-svg/5S.svg" class="t13ne-svgcard"><div class="t13ne-svgpips"><div class="t13ne-textpips">5<!-- write suit--><span class="t13ne-symbol"><span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></span><details class="t13ne-suit"><summary></summary><p class="t13ne-name"><strong>Suit: </strong>Spades <span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></p><p><strong>Ordeal Trump: </strong><span class="t13ne-trump">Attack</span></p><p class="t13ne-desc"><strong>Description: </strong>The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.</p><p class="t13ne-tarot"><strong>Tarot Meaning: </strong>Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.</p><p class="t13ne-otext"><strong>Ordeal Text: </strong>Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.</p><p class="t13ne-parry"><strong>Parry: </strong><strong>Redirect-Attack:</strong> The damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.</p><p class="t13ne-soak"><strong>Soak: </strong><strong>Counter-Attack:</strong> The matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate. </p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-svg-facet"><div>Gossamer</div></div><div class="t13ne-wound"><div><strong class="t13ne-woundname">Flesh</strong><details><summary></summary><strong>Flesh</strong><p class="t13ne-base-pips">Base Pips to summon: 5</p><p class="t13ne-min-pips">Minimum Pips Required: 5</p><p class="t13ne-desc">The Target is wounded and must stop and aknowledge that fact.</p><details class="t13ne-roll"><summary>Rules</summary>Lose one <strong>die</strong> from each pool on the next roll you make (e.g.3d6 + 2d10+5 becomes 2d6 + 1d10+5) then Discard</details><details class="t13ne-ordeal"><summary>Ordeal Rules</summary>Discard an Ordeal card from the next Full-Action or aggravate another Wound 1 Level and then Discard. </details><details class="t13ne-psych"><summary>Psych Wounds</summary><details class="t13ne-negative"><summary>Negative Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Cannot approach source of negative emotion until Stabilized / Discarded</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Paralysed (All Proficiency Dice are lost) until Stabilised / Discarded.</p></details><details class="t13ne-positive"><summary>Positive Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Unwilling to leave source of positive emotion until discarded</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Stimulated/Excited. Discard immediately, along with all GRT and RT Chips and act immediately (still with the dice or card penalties).</p></details></details></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-tarot-svg"><details><summary class="t13ne-wyrdtarot"><strong> Wyrd Tarot: </strong> Surprise</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong> Normal: </strong>Target may disregard current RT, GRT, Pool Size and take a Full-Action, now.</p><p class="t13ne-crossed"><strong> Crossed: </strong>Target is immediately affected by GRT of 2</p></details></div><div class="t13ne-yarn-fancy">
			<details><summary><strong>Yarn</strong> <br>The Sail</summary>
			<span class="t13ne-description">The Sail card represents the act of harnessing an external, or higher, power to your needs. Just as a ship's sail harnesses the elemental wind to drive the ship or mill, so the card can imply that your side will make headway, perhaps travel, by harnessing something they find or create.</span>
			<span class="t13ne-significator">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-significator"><strong>Significator</strong></summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-plot"><strong>Plot: </strong> The Sail Plot is about being at the mercy of greater powers, and learning to survive these immense happenings. The Sail relies on powerful forces from the Cycle or an Epic that drives the forces of the Plot. The Hooked Characters must exist alongside these powerful forces without being directly threatened, usually. The Sail represents harnessing these forces and in this way, you can create Dragon Riders, Demon-hunters, Elemental Bending Styles, or whatever, depending upon what the forces are.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-char"><strong>Character: </strong> The Sail Significator indicates a Character who has learned to harness some external power for their own ends. They Gain Yin when they have time to think, rest, or create. They Gain Yang when they are busy, working or have been given a task. They Gain Chi when they use they use external power to accomplish their goal (engines, sails, horses, whatever really), rather than their own personal power. They also gain +1 Success Levels to attempts to harness external power, and the results.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-scene"><strong>Scene: </strong> The Sail Plot is about being at the mercy of greater powers, and learning to survive these immense happenings. The Sail relies on powerful forces from the Cycle or an Epic that drives the forces of the Plot. The Hooked Characters must exist alongside these powerful forces without being directly threatened, usually. The Sail represents harnessing these forces and in this way, you can create Dragon Riders, Demon-hunters, Elemental Bending Styles, or whatever, depending upon what the forces are.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-scene">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-scene-type">
						<strong>Scene: </strong> Revelation
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Revelations reveal something about the Conflict or Plot to the Characters. Typically a Revelation tells the Characters something about the Villain, or the Prize they are after. Revelations usually consist of 2-5 cards that define: What the information is about, what the actual info is, the vector the Revelation is revealed by, and per haps some Alternative Information that might be revealed (in case you can't use the Info), and a detail that is involved somehow.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
						<strong>Conflict Embodiment: </strong> Rational
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Conflict embodies each side of the Conflict into Characters who are in direct Conflict.  This is the classic Heroes verses Bad Guys plot.This may follow a period of Tension, or not. Often the Rational Embodiment creates a Monster.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>

			<span class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
						<strong>Facet Embodiment: </strong> Resolved Hitch
					</summary>
					<section class="t13ne-description">The Conflict Embodies one Facet as a Character's Resolved Hitch. As with all Character Embodiments, these can be obvious under the right circumstances.</section>
				</details>
			</span>
			 <div class="t13ne-archetypes"><details><summary><strong>Archetype Character: </strong>Sailor</summary> <details class="t13ne-Persona"><summary><strong>Persona: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Scout</span>
      </summary>
      <details class="t13ne-motivation"><summary><strong>Motivation: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">The Scout is motivated by a desire to see new places and things. They like to move and be free to roam.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-avoid"><summary><strong>Avoid: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Scouts prefer to avoid being slowed or encumbered by heavy armour or equipment. They will always travel as light as they can to allow them the most freedom of movement.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-gain-chi"><summary><strong>Gain Chi: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description"><span>Gain Chi when you gain information, see a new place, answer a question, or tell someone what you have experienced.</span><p>Ordeal gain Chi when you act at Range (paying Range costs or using Range Nimbeds).</p></span></details></details>   <details class="t13ne-core"><summary><strong>Core: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Worker</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">If the character takes their time at some task they commonly do, they will automatically completely succeed (during an Ordeal add 2 Success Levels to each draw) for a cost of one Chi.</span></details><details class="t13ne-hitch"><summary><strong>Hitch: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Malformed</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Mutations, missing limbs, or just plain ugly.</span></details><p class="t13ne-description">The Sailor is always full of stories of far away places they watched glide by their ship. They are steady workers who have the occasional tendency to lose body parts, eyes, or gain Scars, and Exposed Tattoos</p></details></div>
			 <span class="t13ne-hook">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-hook">
						<strong>Hook: </strong> Embarkation
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Story Hooks the PCs by getting them all aboard the same vehicle. Often a boat (although trains and even cars will work). Often the other side will also have Embodiments on board the same craft, or that will be encountered at the planned stops or eventual destination.</aside>
					<details class="t13ne-hook-aspect">
						<summary><strong>Hook Aspect: </strong>Hitches</summary>
						<section class="t13ne-description">The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.</section>
					</details>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-obstacle-fancy"><details class="t13ne-obstacle"><summary><strong>Obstacle:</strong>Clouds</summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way ahead is blocked by fog, clouds, smoke or gas. A favourite of villains, who may choose deadly, knock-out, or hallucinogenic chemicals for Traps.</p></details></span>
			<span class="t13ne-gain">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-gain">
						<strong>Gain </strong> 
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Increase a Facet Boon</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-fray">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-fray">
						<strong>Fray: </strong> Travel
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Travelling can be a Motional Ordeal in its own sense, navigating, surviving, but there is more to this than the journey or the destination. Travel truly can broaden the mind, and learning can be part of the Ordeal, raising the Stakes to Soul or Paradoxical levels.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-snag">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-snag">
						<strong>Snag: </strong> Against the Wind
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Fray is complicated by the situation, sometimes the weather is against you, the wind is blowing you into greater danger, a situation that requires a change of tack. It may be a case of changing the PCs short term goals so they can still accomplish their long term goals.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-sweeping">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-sweeping">
						<strong>Sweeping: </strong> On The Beam
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The PCs are headed in the right direction. There's a good wind, and a good chance things are going their way, they might even make a Revelation or make a Gain on the way.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
		 <details class="t13ne-revelation-fancy"><summary><strong>Revelation</strong></summary><details class="t13ne-about"><summary><strong>About: </strong> Dominant </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information revealed is about the Dominant Conflict Embodiment. Sometimes they are Characters, but almost as often they are Locations, Quests, Ordeals, or similar. Where this is true the Revelation should reveal details about future Quests the Pressed Characters must complete, or places they should visit. The Revelation can be little more than a call-back or foreshadowing if handled correct.</p></details><details class="t13ne-info"><summary><strong>Info: </strong> Which? </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Information that reveals which Conclusion or option is correct. Like which way the next piece of the puzzle is, or which wire should be cut.</p></details><details class="t13ne-vector"><summary><strong>Vector: </strong> Deduction </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Facts appear that support the evidence, but it isn't directly stated. </p></details><details class="t13ne-alternate"><summary><strong>Alternate: </strong> Failures </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information is all about past failures. How did the first five test subjects die? Where are the original fixtures? How many people were in that building he designed, you know, the one that fell in the river?</p></details><details class="t13ne-detail"><summary><strong>Detail: </strong> Unreliable </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Unreliable details, sometimes the details don't point you in the right direction. They just might not be true, no matter how much you want to believe that the Vampires and Unicorns are working together on some Undead Faery Cavalry, they are natural enemies.</p></details></details>
		 <details class="t13ne-ordeal-fancy"><summary><strong>Ordeal</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-ordeal-type"><strong>Ordeal-Type </strong> — Opposed Ordeal</p><p class="t13ne-stages"><strong>Stages </strong> — 5</p><p class="t13ne-ordeal-spread"><strong>Ordeal-Spread </strong> — Forking</p><p class="t13ne-stakes"><strong>Stakes </strong> — High</p><p class="t13ne-test"><strong>Test </strong> — Lock-Picking (Key)</p><p class="t13ne-obstacles"><strong>Obstacles </strong> — 1 Obstacle in every Court Stage</p><p class="t13ne-suggested-action"><strong>Suggested-Action </strong> — Stab</p><details class="t13ne-stage"><summary><strong>Stage: </strong> Unstable </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way ahead is fraught with problems, you may be dealing with an unstable floor, or an unstable building, or perhaps the person you are dealing with is themselves unstable.</p></details><details class="t13ne-motional"><summary><strong>Motional: </strong> Water Vehicle Flee </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Keep rowing, I think we're losing them.</p></details><details class="t13ne-fight"><summary><strong>Fight: </strong> Pair of Monsters </summary><p class="t13ne-description">This is a Fight against a pair of Monsters (for example two Vampires or a Vampire and a Werewolf). Each Monster is at least equal to a PC in power and has 2 ♠ Monster Facets.<!-- t13ne-shortcode "%7B%22type%22%3A%22facetsuitaspect%22%2C%22suit%22%3A%224%22%2C%22aspect%22%3A%22Monster%22%7D"// --></p><div class="t13ne-tablewrap"><table class="t13ne-table Monster"><thead><tr><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</th><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Gossamer</td><td><strong>Wiry</strong> —The Monster is immensely physically powerful, although often its form may not reveal it. It often demonstrates supernatural power as well as strength and toughness.
