A small, strong Goblin, a robot that looks like a little girl, a cybernetically enhanced rat, or even a killer doll are all examples of a Gossamer Monster. The Wiry Monster can refill Gossamer Score cards to its Monster Pool whenever it is underestimated. A Wiry monster may add their current Monster Pool size to any Score or Pips to do with their Monstrous strength or toughness. The Monster must spend at least one card from their Monster Pool when they physically attack.</td></tr><tr><td>Inertia</td><td><strong>Unstoppable</strong> —The Monster is inexorable, it does not stop and cannot be killed by any normal means. They start play with a Monster Pool of Inertia Score cards. Unstoppable Monsters can ignore a number of Wounds equal to the number of cards in their Monster Pool. Wounds stacked beyond the Monster Pool affect them as normal. Inexorable Monsters can also ignore all Hitches with a Boon less than their Inertia Score while they have cards in their Monster Pool. Whenever an Unstoppable Monster is actually damaged it must spend cards from its Monster Pool while remaining motionless for Inertia Score as Chi Extend Duration to discard all Wounds less than Mortal in nature, and Stabilise all Mortal + Wounds (which may then be ignored if the Unstoppable has enough cards remaining in its Monster Pool). Examples of the Inertial Monster type include the Hungry Dead (Zombies) and most robotic assassins (especially ones from the future).</td></tr><tr><td>Key</td><td><strong>Hunter</strong> —The Monster is a hunter or tracker, it should favour harrying attacks and hit and run tactics. Typically such a Monster will vanish or flee if it is wounded, only to reappear when healed. The hunter may be fooled by false trails, but never for long. Hunters may add Key Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they spot a track, clue, or similar, of the subject of their hunt. Hunters receive a bonus equal to the number of cards in the Monster Pool to any tracking, attacking or hunting actions if they also spend a card from the Monster Pool on the action. Hunters are usually immune to Carnage and Mortal Wounds until the Zenith of the story (such wounds are taken as Crippling Wounds instead, but at a cost of one card from the Monster Pool). In T13, the archetypal version of this Monster is a Bulmäs Pack member from the Wild Hunt, but any Werewolf or Animal Shifter would probably qualify, as would robot cowboys in theme parks that have gone wrong, and any fantasy world's Elven ranger. </td></tr><tr><td>Miasma</td><td><strong>Mutant</strong> —The monster is a mutant or chimerical creature made from parts of others. This monster type is the oldest perhaps, you’ll find examples throughout the ancient world, particularly in Greek myth. The Mutant will often be larger, and may be malformed or unsymmetrical, with odd numbers of limbs, eyes, horns, or even heads. The Chimera will blend multiple animals together to create a new creature that combines features of all the “parent” creatures, preserving the best of all species. Mutant Monsters start with Miasma Score Monster Pool. They add Monster Pool Size as the Boon of Proficiency less "Mutation" Annex that they can use to grow larger or mimic their “parents” abilities. 1950s B-movie monsters are often of this type, giant ants, lizards, spiders, wasps, and even rabbits have been used. Classically of course, Cyclopses, Centaurs, Minotaurs, Satyrs, Gryphons (or Griffins), hippogriffs, dragons, sphinxes, manticores and of course the Chimera itself are all examples. You can also find examples in superheroes, especially those created by radioactive arachnids, or those with an X-gene.</td></tr><tr><td>Trial</td><td><strong>Killer</strong> —The Monster is a deadly Combat specialist of some sort. Killers add Trial Draw cards to their Monster Pool each time they kill someone. Killers automatically add +1 Score/Pip per card of Monster Pool to any attack, usually as Penetration or Range costs. Killers must spend Monster Pool cards whenever they will make a Trick. Killers can be drug-addled assassins, crazed shooters, or just steel-souled soldiers.</td></tr><tr><td>Yonder</td><td><strong>Alien</strong> —The Monster is an alien, an outsider. It may just be a metaphor for alienation, or literally be an extra-(or ultra)-terrestrial. Such monsters are usually obvious and inhuman, not just to look at, but in the way they think and behave. They have apparently supernatural powers and abilities, but often make do with advanced technologies (in the form of additional Descendants). They may draw and play Yonder Draw cards to and from their Monster Pool with their alien / inhuman / weird actions. Their Monster Pool number of cards Reduced adds Success Levels to any action to do with motion, survival or the alien's technologies. From little grey Eusaiveans, through to pod people and pepper-pot shaped engines of hate, all the way to face-hugging xenomorphic space-wasps, there are plenty of aliens.</td></tr></tbody><tfoot><tr><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</td><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</td></tr></tfoot></table></div><p></p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Red/Purple Carnivore</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> Hunters, Broccen, Vermis, Labyrinthine, All-Walkers</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> Macrofauna</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong> Spider / Centipede / Wasp / Scorpion / Songbird / Bat</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> Small often venomous predators, usually insectivores</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-cardwrap-jsvg" id="cardrewritebox44" data-mode="jsvg" data-wyrd="1" data-yarn="1"><img src="https://www.cjmoseley.dev.cc/wp-content/plugins/t13ne/images/cards-svg/6S.svg" class="t13ne-svgcard"><div class="t13ne-svgpips"><div class="t13ne-textpips">6<!-- write suit--><span class="t13ne-symbol"><span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></span><details class="t13ne-suit"><summary></summary><p class="t13ne-name"><strong>Suit: </strong>Spades <span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></p><p><strong>Ordeal Trump: </strong><span class="t13ne-trump">Attack</span></p><p class="t13ne-desc"><strong>Description: </strong>The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.</p><p class="t13ne-tarot"><strong>Tarot Meaning: </strong>Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.</p><p class="t13ne-otext"><strong>Ordeal Text: </strong>Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.</p><p class="t13ne-parry"><strong>Parry: </strong><strong>Redirect-Attack:</strong> The damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.</p><p class="t13ne-soak"><strong>Soak: </strong><strong>Counter-Attack:</strong> The matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate. </p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-svg-facet"><div>Inertia</div></div><div class="t13ne-wound"><div><strong class="t13ne-woundname">Flesh</strong><details><summary></summary><strong>Flesh</strong><p class="t13ne-base-pips">Base Pips to summon: 5</p><p class="t13ne-min-pips">Minimum Pips Required: 5</p><p class="t13ne-desc">The Target is wounded and must stop and aknowledge that fact.</p><details class="t13ne-roll"><summary>Rules</summary>Lose one <strong>die</strong> from each pool on the next roll you make (e.g.3d6 + 2d10+5 becomes 2d6 + 1d10+5) then Discard</details><details class="t13ne-ordeal"><summary>Ordeal Rules</summary>Discard an Ordeal card from the next Full-Action or aggravate another Wound 1 Level and then Discard. </details><details class="t13ne-psych"><summary>Psych Wounds</summary><details class="t13ne-negative"><summary>Negative Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Cannot approach source of negative emotion until Stabilized / Discarded</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Paralysed (All Proficiency Dice are lost) until Stabilised / Discarded.</p></details><details class="t13ne-positive"><summary>Positive Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Unwilling to leave source of positive emotion until discarded</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Stimulated/Excited. Discard immediately, along with all GRT and RT Chips and act immediately (still with the dice or card penalties).</p></details></details></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-tarot-svg"><details><summary class="t13ne-wyrdtarot"><strong> Wyrd Tarot: </strong> Unbalance</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong> Normal: </strong>Cancels a Stalemate with a random Success Level up or down. Card Draws are randomly +/- 1 Card. Or 1 automatic Distract</p><p class="t13ne-crossed"><strong> Crossed: </strong>Target may automatically Stalemate any Test (Scoring exactly the Difficulty). Automatically pay a Stage / Obstacle cost in an Ordeal</p></details></div><div class="t13ne-yarn-fancy">
			<details><summary><strong>Yarn</strong> <br>The Clock</summary>
			<span class="t13ne-description">The Clock card represents Time and timings, obviously, but a clock is a complex and difficult to construct mechanism, and so the card also represents long, complex tasks and procedures. The appearance of the card may indicate that a deadline is in place, that action must take place within a certain time, or that patience and waiting are the only way forwards.</span>
			<span class="t13ne-significator">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-significator"><strong>Significator</strong></summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-plot"><strong>Plot: </strong> The Clock Plot is generally a plot with a time limit. Deadlines, time-limits, ticking bombing countdowns are common enough examples. Of course, a time limit alone isn't the only thing about a Clock Plot. Clock plots also have lots of moving parts, complex set pieces and multiple sides with many Characters.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-char"><strong>Character: </strong> The Clock Significator indicates a Character who is regulated and unforgiving. They Gain Yin when they hold a grudge, recall an event, or talk about the past. they Gain Yang when they plan ahead, talk about the future, or make a schedule. They Gain Chi when they are on time, living in the moment, or check the time. They also gain +1 Success Level on any action to do with timing, punctuality or precision.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-scene"><strong>Scene: </strong> The Clock Plot is generally a plot with a time limit. Deadlines, time-limits, ticking bombing countdowns are common enough examples. Of course, a time limit alone isn't the only thing about a Clock Plot. Clock plots also have lots of moving parts, complex set pieces and multiple sides with many Characters.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-scene">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-scene-type">
						<strong>Scene: </strong> Warp
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Warps work the Conflict. They are broken into 3 parts (and require another 2 cards): <ul><li>The Ends (What the Players want, usually described as Quests ). Does not require a Yarn Card.</li><li>The Fray (what keeps the Characters from their Ends, how do they work through that Quest?). The Fray requires an extra Yarn Card.</li><li>The Snag (The complication that renders the actions of the Characters winning through the Fray moot somehow). The Snag also requires another Yarn Card.</li></ul>During Warps things tend to get worse for the Pressed side of the Conflict.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
						<strong>Conflict Embodiment: </strong> Rational
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Conflict embodies each side of the Conflict into Characters who are in direct Conflict.  This is the classic Heroes verses Bad Guys plot.This may follow a period of Tension, or not. Often the Rational Embodiment creates a Monster.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>

			<span class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
						<strong>Facet Embodiment: </strong> Monster
					</summary>
					<section class="t13ne-description">The Conflict Embodies one Facet as a Character Monster Facet.This Monster might be a Dragon, an Orc Horde, or just a human Villain. Monsters can be great fun, but are rarely subtle. Where possible when using Monster embodiments, the Plot may want to keep the exact nature of the Monster a secret for as long as it can.</section>
				</details>
			</span>
			 <div class="t13ne-archetypes"><details><summary><strong>Archetype Character: </strong>Historian</summary> <details class="t13ne-Persona"><summary><strong>Persona: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Sleuth</span>
      </summary>
      <details class="t13ne-motivation"><summary><strong>Motivation: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Sleuths love to solve mysteries. Nothing gets a Sleuth motivated like a good puzzle for them to solve. It may not be a crime, or even a scientific mystery, some sleuths just love a daily crossword, and working on it in breaks will keep them motivated throughout the day.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-avoid"><summary><strong>Avoid: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Sleuths avoid boredom like the plague. Their minds just cannot stand going without a challenge for even a few days. They may not be workaholics, but they will quickly find something to do even on vacation.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-gain-chi"><summary><strong>Gain Chi: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description"><span>Gain Chi when you ask or answer a question, look for clues, penetrate defences, or solve something (puzzle / equation/ mystery).</span><p>Ordeal gain Chi when you play a ♠ to prepare</p></span></details></details>   <details class="t13ne-core"><summary><strong>Core: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Crafter</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Add Craft Score (or die) to all Skill Annex rolls and all ♦ Ordeal cards.</span></details><details class="t13ne-hitch"><summary><strong>Hitch: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Offensive</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Some people are just rude, or overly competitive, others are actually bigoted, or prejudiced. Sometimes it can be how people behave, other times the offence can be to the senses.</span></details><p class="t13ne-description">The Historian or Chronicler investigates some events, and then turns those investigations into stories that they write and sell.</p></details></div>
			 <span class="t13ne-hook">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-hook">
						<strong>Hook: </strong> Countdown
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The story hooks the character with a countdown situation. There is limited time until something major happens. How major will vary on the Stakes, with low stakes being responding to a school reunion RSVP, and High Stakes being ambulances racing through traffic to get to a accident scene.</aside>
					<details class="t13ne-hook-aspect">
						<summary><strong>Hook Aspect: </strong>Hitches</summary>
						<section class="t13ne-description">The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.</section>
					</details>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-obstacle-fancy"><details class="t13ne-obstacle"><summary><strong>Obstacle:</strong>Mechanism</summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way is blocked by some mechanism (such as a raising or rotating bridge, crane or vehicle), often used to create complex mechanical traps, that usually have some timer and reset mechanism, such as crushing walls, swinging blades, spinning chains. Such mechanisms usually have some hidden reset or control panel.</p></details></span>
			<span class="t13ne-gain">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-gain">
						<strong>Gain </strong> 
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Time (some sense of an extension in a deadline or additional life to live)</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-fray">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-fray">
						<strong>Fray: </strong> Clockwork
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Precise, meticulous planning, preparation and timing is required for execution of clockwork, whether its building a clock, or robbing a bank, clockwork is a Complex Procedural and Motional Ordeal that can be of any Stakes. Timing is a natural part of the Clockwork Fray and you can model that with limited Phases per Stage.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-snag">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-snag">
						<strong>Snag: </strong> Time
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Fray may be affected by a Time Limit, this is usually applied as a Limit on the total number of Phases that the Ordeal must be completed in (or at least that a certain Stage must be completed by), note that this is in addition to any Phases per Stage Limit that may already be in place because of the Stakes). If a Time Limit is inappropriate for some reason, then the situation must be complicated by History, with past choices limiting future choices.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-sweeping">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-sweeping">
						<strong>Sweeping: </strong> Recuperation Time
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The PCs are not doing well, they need to rest badly, in fact they may be ill from festering wounds, the way everything keeps spinning... They come around in hospital, or at least a bed somewhere, being nursed back to health, they may still be injured, but their wounds have been cleaned and bandaged, and perhaps some antibiotic poultices have been applied, if you are lucky.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
		 <details class="t13ne-revelation-fancy"><summary><strong>Revelation</strong></summary><details class="t13ne-about"><summary><strong>About: </strong> Dominant </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information revealed is about the Dominant Conflict Embodiment. Sometimes they are Characters, but almost as often they are Locations, Quests, Ordeals, or similar. Where this is true the Revelation should reveal details about future Quests the Pressed Characters must complete, or places they should visit. The Revelation can be little more than a call-back or foreshadowing if handled correct.</p></details><details class="t13ne-info"><summary><strong>Info: </strong> By What Means? </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Information about how far someone may go to achieve their Ends.</p></details><details class="t13ne-vector"><summary><strong>Vector: </strong> Reasoning </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The Character will be given evidence that points towards the information, but they will have to put the pieces together themselves (and probably get it wrong at least once).</p></details><details class="t13ne-alternate"><summary><strong>Alternate: </strong> Ways </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information is about ways, ways things can be done, ways things can go, and ways into. and out of, town without being noticed by the guard.</p></details><details class="t13ne-detail"><summary><strong>Detail: </strong> Axiom </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The detail is a truism, or at least an aphorism, or saying, that strongly applies to the situation.</p></details></details>
		 <details class="t13ne-ordeal-fancy"><summary><strong>Ordeal</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-ordeal-type"><strong>Ordeal-Type </strong> — Opposed Ordeal</p><p class="t13ne-stages"><strong>Stages </strong> — 5</p><p class="t13ne-ordeal-spread"><strong>Ordeal-Spread </strong> — Pyramid</p><p class="t13ne-stakes"><strong>Stakes </strong> — High</p><p class="t13ne-test"><strong>Test </strong> — Perception (Key)</p><p class="t13ne-obstacles"><strong>Obstacles </strong> — 1 Obstacle in every Court Stage</p><p class="t13ne-suggested-action"><strong>Suggested-Action </strong> — Spot</p><details class="t13ne-stage"><summary><strong>Stage: </strong> Dark </summary><p class="t13ne-description">There's a lot of potential dangers in the darkness, ranging from the occasional Lego brick, or upturned plug, or nail, to holes and of course, monsters.</p></details><details class="t13ne-motional"><summary><strong>Motional: </strong> Flying Riding Flee </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The hover-board gangs boards roared as they engaged their plasma burners, and they raced for us, as I took the ornithopter over a grav-transport and onto the north-bound express level.</p></details><details class="t13ne-fight"><summary><strong>Fight: </strong> Major Trouble </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The Fight is a seriously dangerous one. High or Extreme Stakes against a stream of Cast Monsters (with at least 3 Monster Facets). <!-- t13ne-shortcode "%7B%22type%22%3A%22facetsuitaspect%22%2C%22suit%22%3A%224%22%2C%22aspect%22%3A%22Monster%22%7D"// --></p><div class="t13ne-tablewrap"><table class="t13ne-table Monster"><thead><tr><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</th><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Gossamer</td><td><strong>Wiry</strong> —The Monster is immensely physically powerful, although often its form may not reveal it. It often demonstrates supernatural power as well as strength and toughness. A small, strong Goblin, a robot that looks like a little girl, a cybernetically enhanced rat, or even a killer doll are all examples of a Gossamer Monster. The Wiry Monster can refill Gossamer Score cards to its Monster Pool whenever it is underestimated. A Wiry monster may add their current Monster Pool size to any Score or Pips to do with their Monstrous strength or toughness. The Monster must spend at least one card from their Monster Pool when they physically attack.</td></tr><tr><td>Inertia</td><td><strong>Unstoppable</strong> —The Monster is inexorable, it does not stop and cannot be killed by any normal means. They start play with a Monster Pool of Inertia Score cards. Unstoppable Monsters can ignore a number of Wounds equal to the number of cards in their Monster Pool. Wounds stacked beyond the Monster Pool affect them as normal. Inexorable Monsters can also ignore all Hitches with a Boon less than their Inertia Score while they have cards in their Monster Pool. Whenever an Unstoppable Monster is actually damaged it must spend cards from its Monster Pool while remaining motionless for Inertia Score as Chi Extend Duration to discard all Wounds less than Mortal in nature, and Stabilise all Mortal + Wounds (which may then be ignored if the Unstoppable has enough cards remaining in its Monster Pool). Examples of the Inertial Monster type include the Hungry Dead (Zombies) and most robotic assassins (especially ones from the future).</td></tr><tr><td>Key</td><td><strong>Hunter</strong> —The Monster is a hunter or tracker, it should favour harrying attacks and hit and run tactics. Typically such a Monster will vanish or flee if it is wounded, only to reappear when healed. The hunter may be fooled by false trails, but never for long. Hunters may add Key Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they spot a track, clue, or similar, of the subject of their hunt. Hunters receive a bonus equal to the number of cards in the Monster Pool to any tracking, attacking or hunting actions if they also spend a card from the Monster Pool on the action. Hunters are usually immune to Carnage and Mortal Wounds until the Zenith of the story (such wounds are taken as Crippling Wounds instead, but at a cost of one card from the Monster Pool). In T13, the archetypal version of this Monster is a Bulmäs Pack member from the Wild Hunt, but any Werewolf or Animal Shifter would probably qualify, as would robot cowboys in theme parks that have gone wrong, and any fantasy world's Elven ranger. </td></tr><tr><td>Miasma</td><td><strong>Mutant</strong> —The monster is a mutant or chimerical creature made from parts of others. This monster type is the oldest perhaps, you’ll find examples throughout the ancient world, particularly in Greek myth. The Mutant will often be larger, and may be malformed or unsymmetrical, with odd numbers of limbs, eyes, horns, or even heads. The Chimera will blend multiple animals together to create a new creature that combines features of all the “parent” creatures, preserving the best of all species. Mutant Monsters start with Miasma Score Monster Pool. They add Monster Pool Size as the Boon of Proficiency less "Mutation" Annex that they can use to grow larger or mimic their “parents” abilities. 1950s B-movie monsters are often of this type, giant ants, lizards, spiders, wasps, and even rabbits have been used. Classically of course, Cyclopses, Centaurs, Minotaurs, Satyrs, Gryphons (or Griffins), hippogriffs, dragons, sphinxes, manticores and of course the Chimera itself are all examples. You can also find examples in superheroes, especially those created by radioactive arachnids, or those with an X-gene.</td></tr><tr><td>Trial</td><td><strong>Killer</strong> —The Monster is a deadly Combat specialist of some sort. Killers add Trial Draw cards to their Monster Pool each time they kill someone. Killers automatically add +1 Score/Pip per card of Monster Pool to any attack, usually as Penetration or Range costs. Killers must spend Monster Pool cards whenever they will make a Trick. Killers can be drug-addled assassins, crazed shooters, or just steel-souled soldiers.</td></tr><tr><td>Yonder</td><td><strong>Alien</strong> —The Monster is an alien, an outsider. It may just be a metaphor for alienation, or literally be an extra-(or ultra)-terrestrial. Such monsters are usually obvious and inhuman, not just to look at, but in the way they think and behave. They have apparently supernatural powers and abilities, but often make do with advanced technologies (in the form of additional Descendants). They may draw and play Yonder Draw cards to and from their Monster Pool with their alien / inhuman / weird actions. Their Monster Pool number of cards Reduced adds Success Levels to any action to do with motion, survival or the alien's technologies. From little grey Eusaiveans, through to pod people and pepper-pot shaped engines of hate, all the way to face-hugging xenomorphic space-wasps, there are plenty of aliens.</td></tr></tbody><tfoot><tr><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</td><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</td></tr></tfoot></table></div><p></p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Red/Purple Carnivore</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> Hunters, Broccen, Vermis, Labyrinthine, All-Walkers</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> Megafauna – Tiny</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong>  Snake / Domestic Cat / Owl / Hawk</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> Animals smaller than 40 Kilograms, usually eat small mammals and birds. Very common path in human occupied territories.</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-cardwrap-jsvg" id="cardrewritebox45" data-mode="jsvg" data-wyrd="1" data-yarn="1"><img src="https://www.cjmoseley.dev.cc/wp-content/plugins/t13ne/images/cards-svg/7S.svg" class="t13ne-svgcard"><div class="t13ne-svgpips"><div class="t13ne-textpips">7<!-- write suit--><span class="t13ne-symbol"><span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></span><details class="t13ne-suit"><summary></summary><p class="t13ne-name"><strong>Suit: </strong>Spades <span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></p><p><strong>Ordeal Trump: </strong><span class="t13ne-trump">Attack</span></p><p class="t13ne-desc"><strong>Description: </strong>The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.</p><p class="t13ne-parry"><strong>Parry: </strong><strong>Redirect-Attack:</strong> The damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.</p><p class="t13ne-soak"><strong>Soak: </strong><strong>Counter-Attack:</strong> The matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate. </p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-svg-facet"><div>Trial</div></div><div class="t13ne-wound"><div><strong class="t13ne-woundname">Flesh</strong><details><summary></summary><strong>Flesh</strong><p class="t13ne-base-pips">Base Pips to summon: 5</p><p class="t13ne-min-pips">Minimum Pips Required: 5</p><p class="t13ne-desc">The Target is wounded and must stop and aknowledge that fact.</p><details class="t13ne-roll"><summary>Rules</summary>Lose one <strong>die</strong> from each pool on the next roll you make (e.g.3d6 + 2d10+5 becomes 2d6 + 1d10+5) then Discard</details><details class="t13ne-ordeal"><summary>Ordeal Rules</summary>Discard an Ordeal card from the next Full-Action or aggravate another Wound 1 Level and then Discard. </details><details class="t13ne-psych"><summary>Psych Wounds</summary><details class="t13ne-negative"><summary>Negative Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Cannot approach source of negative emotion until Stabilized / Discarded</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Paralysed (All Proficiency Dice are lost) until Stabilised / Discarded.</p></details><details class="t13ne-positive"><summary>Positive Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Unwilling to leave source of positive emotion until discarded</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Stimulated/Excited. Discard immediately, along with all GRT and RT Chips and act immediately (still with the dice or card penalties).</p></details></details></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-tarot-svg"><details><summary class="t13ne-wyrdtarot"><strong> Wyrd Tarot: </strong> Rise</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong> Normal: </strong>+2 Success Levels to one roll, or Ordeal Card.</p><p class="t13ne-crossed"><strong> Crossed: </strong>Target rolls a Complete Failure or Draws minimum.</p></details></div><div class="t13ne-yarn-fancy">
			<details><summary><strong>Yarn</strong> <br>The Sword</summary>
			<span class="t13ne-description">The Sword Card represents danger, combat and peril. The Sword is a weapon, and so it represents warfare, weaponry and attacking forces. Sometimes arguments escalate, the Sword shows us the precise moment that things escalate to High Stakes.</span>
			<span class="t13ne-significator">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-significator"><strong>Significator</strong></summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-plot"><strong>Plot: </strong> The Sword Plot is a War Story. It is a tale of attackers and assaults, tactics and defences.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-char"><strong>Character: </strong> The Sword Significator indicates a Character who is quick to attack and deadly with it. They Gain Yin when they are in peril. They Gain Yang when they prepare for action. They Gain Chi when they attack another. They also gain +1 Success Levels to all attacks they make.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-scene"><strong>Scene: </strong> The Sword Plot is a War Story. It is a tale of attackers and assaults, tactics and defences.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-scene">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-scene-type">
						<strong>Scene: </strong> Weft
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Wefts work the Conflict through 3 Parts: <ul><li>First by the Characters Recoiling from the Snag (They emotionally respond to the previous event) which does not require a Yarn card</li><li>Secondly Sweeping (where the Characters recover from what happened) which does require another Yarn Card</li><li>Finally, Picking (Where the Characters will have a choice how the Story will proceed) which does not require Yarn Cards, but can be represented by more cards during card play.</li></ul>During Wefts the Pressed side makes a little headway, or gets a little breather.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
						<strong>Conflict Embodiment: </strong> Rational
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Conflict embodies each side of the Conflict into Characters who are in direct Conflict.  This is the classic Heroes verses Bad Guys plot.This may follow a period of Tension, or not. Often the Rational Embodiment creates a Monster.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>

			<span class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
						<strong>Facet Embodiment: </strong> Proficiency
					</summary>
					<section class="t13ne-description">The Conflict Embodies one Facet as a single Proficiency from that Facet. Characters or Descendants that have the Proficiency may be Hooked to that side. This is a very subtle Embodiment when handled gently.</section>
				</details>
			</span>
			 <div class="t13ne-archetypes"><details><summary><strong>Archetype Character: </strong>Warrior</summary> <details class="t13ne-Persona"><summary><strong>Persona: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Hero</span>
      </summary>
      <details class="t13ne-motivation"><summary><strong>Motivation: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Heroes are extraordinary individuals who are noted for their great acts of courage, and enormous achievements. Heroes often act selflessly to protect others in harms way.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-avoid"><summary><strong>Avoid: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Heroes tend to avoid situations where they show weakness to others.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-gain-chi"><summary><strong>Gain Chi: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description"><span>Gain Chi when you risk something, help another, volunteer, or are praised for your actions. </span><p>Ordeal gain Chi when attacked.</p></span></details></details>   <details class="t13ne-core"><summary><strong>Core: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Attacker</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Character has a “special move” that when used adds two success levels to the result automatically. In Ordeals this means the Special move can draw and play an extra two cards, or add one or two levels to one or two cards</span></details><details class="t13ne-hitch"><summary><strong>Hitch: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Offensive</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Some people are just rude, or overly competitive, others are actually bigoted, or prejudiced. Sometimes it can be how people behave, other times the offence can be to the senses.</span></details><p class="t13ne-description">Warriors are military men, usually heroes (although Scouts, Vandals and Gladiators are also possible). All Warriors have a "Special Move" that is drilled into them, usually a standard for that nation's military training, or their combat role.</p></details></div>
			 <span class="t13ne-hook">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-hook">
						<strong>Hook: </strong> Attack!
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Story hooks the character with a sudden attack. This attack should be connected (even if only peripherally) to the main Villain, so a trail of revenge would lead the PCs back. Of course, the attack on the PCs may not have been deliberate, but just be a domino chain of events.</aside>
					<details class="t13ne-hook-aspect">
						<summary><strong>Hook Aspect: </strong>Hitches</summary>
						<section class="t13ne-description">The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.</section>
					</details>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-obstacle-fancy"><details class="t13ne-obstacle"><summary><strong>Obstacle:</strong>Death-Trap</summary><p class="t13ne-description">A Death-Trap is a deadly obstacle that is not intended to be survived. Land-Mines, poison coated spikes, decapitating blades, quantum annihilation fields, black mist filled mirrors, and unshielded black-holes within arms length, are all example Death-Traps.</p></details></span>
			<span class="t13ne-gain">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-gain">
						<strong>Gain </strong> 
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Lower a Hitch Boon by one or several Boons.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-fray">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-fray">
						<strong>Fray: </strong> Battle
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Battle is a massive, Extreme stakes Opposed Ordeal, where at least two armed forces exchange wounds. There will be deaths on both sides usually, or worse, enemies may come in waves, a steady stream, or may be separated by Stages.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-snag">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-snag">
						<strong>Snag: </strong> A Sword Raised
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">A Powerful Character (often a Monster with at least TWO Monster Facets and more normally around 5) such as a Cyborg Commando, Griffin and rider, Dragon, Vampire Assassin, Chimera or Demon, who opposes the PCs. This opposition may be temporary, and the Character may turn out to be a potential Ally, but first there will be a fight. This is usually the result of a misunderstanding, that will not be resolved until after they have engaged in battle.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-sweeping">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-sweeping">
						<strong>Sweeping: </strong> The Sweeping Sword
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The PCs get to take it easy, as another side (or new Embodiment) suddenly engages the (old) Opposed Embodiment in a direct conflict (usually of raised Stakes, often of High or Extreme). The PCs old enemy may easily defeat this accidental ally, or may fall to this new threat, in which case, expect a Revelation.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
		 <details class="t13ne-revelation-fancy"><summary><strong>Revelation</strong></summary><details class="t13ne-about"><summary><strong>About: </strong> Dominant </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information revealed is about the Dominant Conflict Embodiment. Sometimes they are Characters, but almost as often they are Locations, Quests, Ordeals, or similar. Where this is true the Revelation should reveal details about future Quests the Pressed Characters must complete, or places they should visit. The Revelation can be little more than a call-back or foreshadowing if handled correct.</p></details><details class="t13ne-info"><summary><strong>Info: </strong> With What? </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Information about a Descendant, that may help or be hindering.</p></details><details class="t13ne-vector"><summary><strong>Vector: </strong> Residue </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Maybe there's gunpowder burns on their fingers, or perhaps a smear of a specific engine grease on their glove, maybe some left over "product" has turned up on the streets. Some residue points to the information others need.</p></details><details class="t13ne-alternate"><summary><strong>Alternate: </strong> With Whom? </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The Embodiment is never alone, and they keep the strangest of company. "You heard right, I said he goes everywhere with two ninja, one dressed in white and one in red.... Yeah, I asked where's the one in black, and you know he just smiled!"</p></details><details class="t13ne-detail"><summary><strong>Detail: </strong> Fascinating </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Fascinating details draw someone's attention to themselves. They may even cause you to overlook more boring details. "He's known for roasting and eating his enemies and famously he has a glass eye that is made from a single giant emerald."</p></details></details>
		 <details class="t13ne-ordeal-fancy"><summary><strong>Ordeal</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-ordeal-type"><strong>Ordeal-Type </strong> — Opposed Ordeal</p><p class="t13ne-stages"><strong>Stages </strong> — 5</p><p class="t13ne-ordeal-spread"><strong>Ordeal-Spread </strong> — Block</p><p class="t13ne-stakes"><strong>Stakes </strong> — High</p><p class="t13ne-test"><strong>Test </strong> — Contagion (Miasma)</p><p class="t13ne-obstacles"><strong>Obstacles </strong> — 1 Obstacle in every Court Stage</p><p class="t13ne-suggested-action"><strong>Suggested-Action </strong> — Poison / Envenom</p><details class="t13ne-stage"><summary><strong>Stage: </strong> Suspect </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way ahead is suspect somehow, maybe the clouds of vapour are just steam, or maybe they are poisonous. Perhaps the Stage involves tracking or finding a suspect or dealing with a suspicious package or place.</p></details><details class="t13ne-motional"><summary><strong>Motional: </strong> Water Riding Flee </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Don't stop now, their squid-riders are gaining!</p></details><details class="t13ne-fight"><summary><strong>Fight: </strong> Great Threat </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The Fight is against a powerful enemy such as a Greater Demon or Horde of Monsters. <!-- t13ne-shortcode "%7B%22type%22%3A%22facetsuitaspect%22%2C%22suit%22%3A%224%22%2C%22aspect%22%3A%22Monster%22%7D"// --></p><div class="t13ne-tablewrap"><table class="t13ne-table Monster"><thead><tr><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</th><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Gossamer</td><td><strong>Wiry</strong> —The Monster is immensely physically powerful, although often its form may not reveal it. It often demonstrates supernatural power as well as strength and toughness. A small, strong Goblin, a robot that looks like a little girl, a cybernetically enhanced rat, or even a killer doll are all examples of a Gossamer Monster. The Wiry Monster can refill Gossamer Score cards to its Monster Pool whenever it is underestimated. A Wiry monster may add their current Monster Pool size to any Score or Pips to do with their Monstrous strength or toughness. The Monster must spend at least one card from their Monster Pool when they physically attack.</td></tr><tr><td>Inertia</td><td><strong>Unstoppable</strong> —The Monster is inexorable, it does not stop and cannot be killed by any normal means. They start play with a Monster Pool of Inertia Score cards. Unstoppable Monsters can ignore a number of Wounds equal to the number of cards in their Monster Pool. Wounds stacked beyond the Monster Pool affect them as normal. Inexorable Monsters can also ignore all Hitches with a Boon less than their Inertia Score while they have cards in their Monster Pool. Whenever an Unstoppable Monster is actually damaged it must spend cards from its Monster Pool while remaining motionless for Inertia Score as Chi Extend Duration to discard all Wounds less than Mortal in nature, and Stabilise all Mortal + Wounds (which may then be ignored if the Unstoppable has enough cards remaining in its Monster Pool). Examples of the Inertial Monster type include the Hungry Dead (Zombies) and most robotic assassins (especially ones from the future).</td></tr><tr><td>Key</td><td><strong>Hunter</strong> —The Monster is a hunter or tracker, it should favour harrying attacks and hit and run tactics. Typically such a Monster will vanish or flee if it is wounded, only to reappear when healed. The hunter may be fooled by false trails, but never for long. Hunters may add Key Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they spot a track, clue, or similar, of the subject of their hunt. Hunters receive a bonus equal to the number of cards in the Monster Pool to any tracking, attacking or hunting actions if they also spend a card from the Monster Pool on the action. Hunters are usually immune to Carnage and Mortal Wounds until the Zenith of the story (such wounds are taken as Crippling Wounds instead, but at a cost of one card from the Monster Pool). In T13, the archetypal version of this Monster is a Bulmäs Pack member from the Wild Hunt, but any Werewolf or Animal Shifter would probably qualify, as would robot cowboys in theme parks that have gone wrong, and any fantasy world's Elven ranger. </td></tr><tr><td>Miasma</td><td><strong>Mutant</strong> —The monster is a mutant or chimerical creature made from parts of others. This monster type is the oldest perhaps, you’ll find examples throughout the ancient world, particularly in Greek myth. The Mutant will often be larger, and may be malformed or unsymmetrical, with odd numbers of limbs, eyes, horns, or even heads. The Chimera will blend multiple animals together to create a new creature that combines features of all the “parent” creatures, preserving the best of all species. Mutant Monsters start with Miasma Score Monster Pool. They add Monster Pool Size as the Boon of Proficiency less "Mutation" Annex that they can use to grow larger or mimic their “parents” abilities. 1950s B-movie monsters are often of this type, giant ants, lizards, spiders, wasps, and even rabbits have been used. Classically of course, Cyclopses, Centaurs, Minotaurs, Satyrs, Gryphons (or Griffins), hippogriffs, dragons, sphinxes, manticores and of course the Chimera itself are all examples. You can also find examples in superheroes, especially those created by radioactive arachnids, or those with an X-gene.</td></tr><tr><td>Trial</td><td><strong>Killer</strong> —The Monster is a deadly Combat specialist of some sort. Killers add Trial Draw cards to their Monster Pool each time they kill someone. Killers automatically add +1 Score/Pip per card of Monster Pool to any attack, usually as Penetration or Range costs. Killers must spend Monster Pool cards whenever they will make a Trick. Killers can be drug-addled assassins, crazed shooters, or just steel-souled soldiers.</td></tr><tr><td>Yonder</td><td><strong>Alien</strong> —The Monster is an alien, an outsider. It may just be a metaphor for alienation, or literally be an extra-(or ultra)-terrestrial. Such monsters are usually obvious and inhuman, not just to look at, but in the way they think and behave. They have apparently supernatural powers and abilities, but often make do with advanced technologies (in the form of additional Descendants). They may draw and play Yonder Draw cards to and from their Monster Pool with their alien / inhuman / weird actions. Their Monster Pool number of cards Reduced adds Success Levels to any action to do with motion, survival or the alien's technologies. From little grey Eusaiveans, through to pod people and pepper-pot shaped engines of hate, all the way to face-hugging xenomorphic space-wasps, there are plenty of aliens.</td></tr></tbody><tfoot><tr><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</td><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</td></tr></tfoot></table></div><p></p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Red/Purple Carnivore</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> Hunters, Broccen, Vermis, Labyrinthine, All-Walkers</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> Megafauna – Small</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong> Dog / Wolf / Eagle / Cobra</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> The most common animal path in human occupied territory.</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-cardwrap-jsvg" id="cardrewritebox46" data-mode="jsvg" data-wyrd="1" data-yarn="1"><img src="https://www.cjmoseley.dev.cc/wp-content/plugins/t13ne/images/cards-svg/8S.svg" class="t13ne-svgcard"><div class="t13ne-svgpips"><div class="t13ne-textpips">8<!-- write suit--><span class="t13ne-symbol"><span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></span><details class="t13ne-suit"><summary></summary><p class="t13ne-name"><strong>Suit: </strong>Spades <span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></p><p><strong>Ordeal Trump: </strong><span class="t13ne-trump">Attack</span></p><p class="t13ne-desc"><strong>Description: </strong>The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.</p><p class="t13ne-tarot"><strong>Tarot Meaning: </strong>Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.</p><p class="t13ne-otext"><strong>Ordeal Text: </strong>Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.</p><p class="t13ne-parry"><strong>Parry: </strong><strong>Redirect-Attack:</strong> The damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.</p><p class="t13ne-soak"><strong>Soak: </strong><strong>Counter-Attack:</strong> The matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate. </p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-svg-facet"><div>Trial</div></div><div class="t13ne-wound"><div><strong class="t13ne-woundname">Maim</strong><details><summary></summary><strong>Maim</strong><p class="t13ne-base-pips">Base Pips to summon: 8</p><p class="t13ne-min-pips">Minimum Pips Required: 8</p><p class="t13ne-desc">The Target is injured enough for it to affect their abilities.</p><details class="t13ne-roll"><summary>Rules</summary>Lose 1 Proficiency Slot from affected Annex (including Personality Proficiencies) and recalculate. If all Proficiencies are Maimed, the Annex is Crippled.</details><details class="t13ne-ordeal"><summary>Ordeal Rules</summary>Maimed Annexes lose Proficiency Slots too, but may lose access to Nimbeds affected or take a -1 Card Draw Penalty until Healed / Recalculated. If all Proficiencies are Maimed, the Annex is Crippled.</details><details class="t13ne-psych"><summary>Psych Wounds</summary><details class="t13ne-negative"><summary>Negative Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Proficiency lost from Personality Profs. Must try to flee source of negative emotion until Stabilised or Discarded.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Proficiency lost from Personality Profs. All Proficiency Dice are reduced one dice size (1d6 becomes 1d4) until Stabilised.</p></details><details class="t13ne-positive"><summary>Positive Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Gain 1 Proficiency (permanently) and must approach / follow source of positive emotion until Stabilised.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Gain 2 Proficiencies (permanently), plus must drop to their knees (or simlar) to express the emotion until Stabilised.</p></details></details></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-tarot-svg"><details><summary class="t13ne-wyrdtarot"><strong> Wyrd Tarot: </strong> Doom-theft</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong> Normal: </strong>Take a random card from the Target's Hand and add it to your own. They may draw a replacement.</p><p class="t13ne-crossed"><strong> Crossed: </strong>Add a card from your Hand to the Target's Hand and Draw 2 cards.</p></details></div><div class="t13ne-yarn-fancy">
			<details><summary><strong>Yarn</strong> <br>The Rival</summary>
			<span class="t13ne-description">The Rival card represents another Character that rivals or opposes a Player or Non-Player Character in some way. The Rival may be an opponent, a competitor, an enemy, or just interested in the same love interest. Rivals can be strangers or friends, and the card can indicate a rivalry that is growing within a group, or indicate an external threat. In many ways the Rival card be tied to the Friend or Foe Wyrd Tarot cards, as Rivals can become friends, as well as friends become Rivals.</span>
			<span class="t13ne-significator">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-significator"><strong>Significator</strong></summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-plot"><strong>Plot: </strong> The Rival Plot is a plot that focuses upon a Character and their Rival, in the case of a Party of Adventurers or Team the Rival plot will consist of a Rival team (usually made of the various types of Rival with a Rival for each Character). The Rivals will have similar or opposed goals to the Party, which will naturally bring them into conflict, such as wanting to win the same league, or control the kingdom themselves.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-char"><strong>Character: </strong> The Rival Significator indicates a Character who feels compelled to compete against others. They Gain Yin when they enter a competition, disagree, or oppose another. They Gain Yang when they acknowledge or greet someone they acknowledge as a rival. They Gain Chi when they act in competition against an acknowledged rival. They also gain +1 Success Levels when they compete against an acknowledged rival.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-scene"><strong>Scene: </strong> The Rival Plot is a plot that focuses upon a Character and their Rival, in the case of a Party of Adventurers or Team the Rival plot will consist of a Rival team (usually made of the various types of Rival with a Rival for each Character). The Rivals will have similar or opposed goals to the Party, which will naturally bring them into conflict, such as wanting to win the same league, or control the kingdom themselves.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-scene">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-scene-type">
						<strong>Scene: </strong> Warp
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Warps work the Conflict. They are broken into 3 parts (and require another 2 cards): <ul><li>The Ends (What the Players want, usually described as Quests ). Does not require a Yarn Card.</li><li>The Fray (what keeps the Characters from their Ends, how do they work through that Quest?). The Fray requires an extra Yarn Card.</li><li>The Snag (The complication that renders the actions of the Characters winning through the Fray moot somehow). The Snag also requires another Yarn Card.</li></ul>During Warps things tend to get worse for the Pressed side of the Conflict.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
						<strong>Conflict Embodiment: </strong> Rational
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Conflict embodies each side of the Conflict into Characters who are in direct Conflict.  This is the classic Heroes verses Bad Guys plot.This may follow a period of Tension, or not. Often the Rational Embodiment creates a Monster.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>

			<span class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
						<strong>Facet Embodiment: </strong> Annex
					</summary>
					<section class="t13ne-description">The Conflict Embodies one Facet within an Annex, this could be a Subtle Nimbed or Umbral, or be as obvious as being Root, Channel and at least one Umbral. The Annex may be within Descendants, or Characters.</section>
				</details>
			</span>
			 <div class="t13ne-archetypes"><details><summary><strong>Archetype Character: </strong>Competitor</summary> <details class="t13ne-Persona"><summary><strong>Persona: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Gladiator</span>
      </summary>
      <details class="t13ne-motivation"><summary><strong>Motivation: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">The Gladiator or “Contestant” is driven by a desire to compete against others. They usually want to win, but that isn't actually important to most Gladiators. Gladiators may have elaborate codes of honour that force them to obey all the rules of a contest, but others will cheat to win, there really is no way to tell one from the other.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-avoid"><summary><strong>Avoid: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">The Gladiator avoids boredom by finding a contest or game to play.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-gain-chi"><summary><strong>Gain Chi: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description"><span>Gain Chi when you compete against another, argue, or fight.</span><p>Ordeal gain Chi when you play a ♠.</p></span></details></details>   <details class="t13ne-core"><summary><strong>Core: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Poser</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Gain Chi whenever you recite a catchphrase, cliche, or strike a dramatic pose.</span></details><details class="t13ne-hitch"><summary><strong>Hitch: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Taboo</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Whether religious, superstitious or just dietary, Taboos are things your character just doesn't want to do</span></details><p class="t13ne-description">Competitors are those people who dedicate themselves to a particular sport, game or activity, trying to be the best at some competition. Unspecified Rivals are often Competitors, and sometimes a Competitor may raise to full Rival status.</p></details></div>
			 <span class="t13ne-hook">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-hook">
						<strong>Hook: </strong> Rival Arrives
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Story opens with a Rival arriving, or being revealed. This often signifies some sort of competition is occurring (even if neither side is aware of it).</aside>
					<details class="t13ne-hook-aspect">
						<summary><strong>Hook Aspect: </strong>Hitches</summary>
						<section class="t13ne-description">The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.</section>
					</details>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-obstacle-fancy"><details class="t13ne-obstacle"><summary><strong>Obstacle:</strong>Rival</summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way is blocked by one of the Character's Rivals, this will usually block the path of only the Characters with Rivalries with that Rival, depending upon the Rival.</p></details></span>
			<span class="t13ne-gain">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-gain">
						<strong>Gain </strong> 
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Gain an Ally of a former Rival, or Resolve a Hitch</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-fray">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-fray">
						<strong>Fray: </strong> Competition
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Competitions are usually Opposed Ordeals (Stakes vary based on the Competition. Competitions can be friendly rivalries between comrades, or they can be the reason for bitter Rivalries that destroy lives. Almost any event can become a Competition if Characters are willing. Drinking, Sports, Quizzes, Driving, Racing, and so on. Tournaments are a special type of Competition that includes multiple Rounds of Ordeals with each Round representing a single competition / game / match. Tournaments are usually a Story in their own right rather than just a single Fray, but bigger Conflicts may have to use Tournaments for any Competition.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-snag">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-snag">
						<strong>Snag: </strong> Rivalry
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Fray is complicated by at least one of the PCs Rivals being involved somehow. They may just be present, but immediately start acting to mislead, distract or engage the PC depending upon the nature of the Rivalry. If no Rival is available then an Ally will complicate the Fray by being involved with the Opposed Side somehow.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-sweeping">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-sweeping">
						<strong>Sweeping: </strong> The Enemy of my Enemy
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The PCs may make contact with one or more of the Opposed Embodiments Rivals, or a Rival may be entangled in the Plot. Perhaps offering the PCs a place to stay, or some assistance, simply to get back at their Rival. This Rival may even subject the Opposed Embodiment to a Rivalry Snag during their next or current Fray.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
		 <details class="t13ne-revelation-fancy"><summary><strong>Revelation</strong></summary><details class="t13ne-about"><summary><strong>About: </strong> Dominant </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information revealed is about the Dominant Conflict Embodiment. Sometimes they are Characters, but almost as often they are Locations, Quests, Ordeals, or similar. Where this is true the Revelation should reveal details about future Quests the Pressed Characters must complete, or places they should visit. The Revelation can be little more than a call-back or foreshadowing if handled correct.</p></details><details class="t13ne-info"><summary><strong>Info: </strong> What? </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Information about the Opposition's Ends, what are their goals, or plans.</p></details><details class="t13ne-vector"><summary><strong>Vector: </strong> Traces </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Maybe there are trace materials or chemicals found by forensic scientists, or maybe the police traced the call, or maybe you saw through a piece of paper.</p></details><details class="t13ne-alternate"><summary><strong>Alternate: </strong> Weaponry </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information is about the weaponry, offensive capability, or defensibility of the Embodiment.</p></details><details class="t13ne-detail"><summary><strong>Detail: </strong> Mixed </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Mixed details are the worst. Important facts mixed in with useless lies, and properly hard to tell the one from the other, until it is proven, one way or another.</p></details></details>
		 <details class="t13ne-ordeal-fancy"><summary><strong>Ordeal</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-ordeal-type"><strong>Ordeal-Type </strong> — Opposed Ordeal</p><p class="t13ne-stages"><strong>Stages </strong> — 6</p><p class="t13ne-ordeal-spread"><strong>Ordeal-Spread </strong> — Forking</p><p class="t13ne-stakes"><strong>Stakes </strong> — High</p><p class="t13ne-test"><strong>Test </strong> — Rotting (Miasma)</p><p class="t13ne-obstacles"><strong>Obstacles </strong> — 1 Obstacle in every Stage</p><p class="t13ne-suggested-action"><strong>Suggested-Action </strong> — Wait</p><details class="t13ne-stage"><summary><strong>Stage: </strong> Fetid </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way ahead stinks. It may not necessarily be a foul smell, but it usually is. A perfume store is less likely than an open sewer, or pile of trash, but not impossible.</p></details><details class="t13ne-motional"><summary><strong>Motional: </strong> Ground Riding Flee </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The horses are sweating, foaming as the bit, and you should stop, and rest them, but the Warg-Riders are somewhere behind you and already trying to flank.</p></details><details class="t13ne-fight"><summary><strong>Fight: </strong> Gang Warfare </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The enemy is a large group of rag-tag, undisciplined, but often surprisingly strong, well-trained or heavily-armed Cast Extras.</p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Red/Purple Carnivore</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> Hunters, Broccen, Vermis, Labyrinthine, All-Walkers</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> Megafauna – Medium</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong> Lion / Large Snake / Shark</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> These paths are uncommon in human occupied areas, and tend to be end in Zoos.</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-cardwrap-jsvg" id="cardrewritebox47" data-mode="jsvg" data-wyrd="1" data-yarn="1"><img src="https://www.cjmoseley.dev.cc/wp-content/plugins/t13ne/images/cards-svg/9S.svg" class="t13ne-svgcard"><div class="t13ne-svgpips"><div class="t13ne-textpips">9<!-- write suit--><span class="t13ne-symbol"><span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></span><details class="t13ne-suit"><summary></summary><p class="t13ne-name"><strong>Suit: </strong>Spades <span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></p><p><strong>Ordeal Trump: </strong><span class="t13ne-trump">Attack</span></p><p class="t13ne-desc"><strong>Description: </strong>The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.</p><p class="t13ne-tarot"><strong>Tarot Meaning: </strong>Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.</p><p class="t13ne-otext"><strong>Ordeal Text: </strong>Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.</p><p class="t13ne-parry"><strong>Parry: </strong><strong>Redirect-Attack:</strong> The damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.</p><p class="t13ne-soak"><strong>Soak: </strong><strong>Counter-Attack:</strong> The matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate. </p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-svg-facet"><div>Inertia</div></div><div class="t13ne-wound"><div><strong class="t13ne-woundname">Maim</strong><details><summary></summary><strong>Maim</strong><p class="t13ne-base-pips">Base Pips to summon: 8</p><p class="t13ne-min-pips">Minimum Pips Required: 8</p><p class="t13ne-desc">The Target is injured enough for it to affect their abilities.</p><details class="t13ne-roll"><summary>Rules</summary>Lose 1 Proficiency Slot from affected Annex (including Personality Proficiencies) and recalculate. If all Proficiencies are Maimed, the Annex is Crippled.</details><details class="t13ne-ordeal"><summary>Ordeal Rules</summary>Maimed Annexes lose Proficiency Slots too, but may lose access to Nimbeds affected or take a -1 Card Draw Penalty until Healed / Recalculated. If all Proficiencies are Maimed, the Annex is Crippled.</details><details class="t13ne-psych"><summary>Psych Wounds</summary><details class="t13ne-negative"><summary>Negative Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Proficiency lost from Personality Profs. Must try to flee source of negative emotion until Stabilised or Discarded.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Proficiency lost from Personality Profs. All Proficiency Dice are reduced one dice size (1d6 becomes 1d4) until Stabilised.</p></details><details class="t13ne-positive"><summary>Positive Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Gain 1 Proficiency (permanently) and must approach / follow source of positive emotion until Stabilised.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Gain 2 Proficiencies (permanently), plus must drop to their knees (or simlar) to express the emotion until Stabilised.</p></details></details></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-tarot-svg"><details><summary class="t13ne-wyrdtarot"><strong> Wyrd Tarot: </strong> Distractions</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong> Normal: </strong>Target takes 1d4 Unsoakable Distract Wounds.</p><p class="t13ne-crossed"><strong> Crossed: </strong>Target removes <strong>all</strong> Distract Wounds</p></details></div><div class="t13ne-yarn-fancy">
			<details><summary><strong>Yarn</strong> <br>The Robot</summary>
			<span class="t13ne-description">The Robot card represents the automatons, and workers, be they real robots, or people who live rigid, regimented lives, like the Eusaiveans. The Robot can represent the unconscious mind, which operates the body for the consciousness, or the mechanistic body itself. It can also represent those who are unawakened (or ain't woke) to what is really happening. They might be muggles, grunts, slaves, serfs, or actual robots.</span>
			<span class="t13ne-significator">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-significator"><strong>Significator</strong></summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-plot"><strong>Plot: </strong> The Robot Plot is a special sort of plot that is built with conditional events and essentially is preprogrammed. This sort of Plot can be great for pre-planned Scenarios, but represent a lot of work for the author.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-char"><strong>Character: </strong> The Robot Significator indicates a Character who follows orders and rules. They Gain Yin when they follow rules that they are subject to. They Gain Yang when they follow legal orders that they have been given. They Gain Chi when they perform actions automatically (they have practised an action enough that it no longer requires conscious thought). They also gain +1 Success Levels to any task they perform automatically.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-scene"><strong>Scene: </strong> The Robot Plot is a special sort of plot that is built with conditional events and essentially is preprogrammed. This sort of Plot can be great for pre-planned Scenarios, but represent a lot of work for the author.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-scene">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-scene-type">
						<strong>Scene: </strong> Weft
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Wefts work the Conflict through 3 Parts: <ul><li>First by the Characters Recoiling from the Snag (They emotionally respond to the previous event) which does not require a Yarn card</li><li>Secondly Sweeping (where the Characters recover from what happened) which does require another Yarn Card</li><li>Finally, Picking (Where the Characters will have a choice how the Story will proceed) which does not require Yarn Cards, but can be represented by more cards during card play.</li></ul>During Wefts the Pressed side makes a little headway, or gets a little breather.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
						<strong>Conflict Embodiment: </strong> Rational
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Conflict embodies each side of the Conflict into Characters who are in direct Conflict.  This is the classic Heroes verses Bad Guys plot.This may follow a period of Tension, or not. Often the Rational Embodiment creates a Monster.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>

			<span class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
						<strong>Facet Embodiment: </strong> Lore
					</summary>
					<section class="t13ne-description">The Conflict embodies as a special type of Descendant called a Lore. Lores are the rumours the Players hear about the enemy and themselves as the story goes on. Lores might grant extra Descendants (including Pacts and Locations) to any Character, as it is revealed that they are a powerful wizard, a Necromancer, with a skeleton horde, armed with magickal longsword, etc.</section>
				</details>
			</span>
			 <div class="t13ne-archetypes"><details><summary><strong>Archetype Character: </strong>Robot</summary> <details class="t13ne-Persona"><summary><strong>Persona: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Sphinx</span>
      </summary>
      <details class="t13ne-motivation"><summary><strong>Motivation: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Sphinxes live for a puzzled expression on another's face. They like nothing more than a good question, or riddle that they can ask. Sphinxes may also enjoy offering cryptic advice and tips.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-avoid"><summary><strong>Avoid: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Sphinxes hate giving straight answers to questions, they'll even rather answer a different question to the one asked, if they can get away with it.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-gain-chi"><summary><strong>Gain Chi: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description"><span>Gain Chi when you confuse another Character or ask (a question / a riddle).</span><p>Ordeal gain Chi when you play any Distract card.</p></span></details></details>   <details class="t13ne-core"><summary><strong>Core: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Worker</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">If the character takes their time at some task they commonly do, they will automatically completely succeed (during an Ordeal add 2 Success Levels to each draw) for a cost of one Chi.</span></details><details class="t13ne-hitch"><summary><strong>Hitch: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Duty</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Whether its a duty to family, the law, or king and Country, some people are just driven by something greater than themselves.</span></details><p class="t13ne-description">The Robot is usually an android servant or digital assistant. It has some AI and may even be sentient in Science-Fiction. In some genres Robot Archetypes may also be used for slaves, zombies and hypnotized or enchanted servants.</p></details></div>
			 <span class="t13ne-hook">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-hook">
						<strong>Hook: </strong> Previously...
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">This Story is a continuation, sequel and possibly a resolution to a previous Story. It can help to have a little recap, where was that Story left, what did they PCs know, how much do the Players remember? This Hook is that chance to recap.</aside>
					<details class="t13ne-hook-aspect">
						<summary><strong>Hook Aspect: </strong>Hitches</summary>
						<section class="t13ne-description">The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.</section>
					</details>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-obstacle-fancy"><details class="t13ne-obstacle"><summary><strong>Obstacle:</strong>Robotic</summary><p class="t13ne-description">Robotic traps come in all shapes and sizes, from automatic spike traps, to actual robots. The robotic trap usually targets a moving individual and wounds them. Then it selects a new moving target (or the same target randomly if they're still moving) and wounds them. The trap may be limited in duration, or range, as well as Limit.</p></details></span>
			<span class="t13ne-gain">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-gain">
						<strong>Gain </strong> 
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Powerful, but subservient Cast or Force of Nature Ally (Supernatural Servant / Golem / Robot / AI / Toon)</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-fray">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-fray">
						<strong>Fray: </strong> An Ordinary Day
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">An Ordinary Day, just going about doing what you normally do. So much of the day is normally performed by the body and mind on autopilot, imagine if it wasn't, imagine trying to do everything in a normal day consciously, as though for the first time. Usually this sort of Fray is part of early world-building to establish the Character, later this Ordinary Day is subverted as the Character has changed and developed, their old Ordinary Day is no longer normal and they may expect Demons or Wizards to burst into the office at any moment, and have to focus on the HR Memo that's doing the rounds.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-snag">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-snag">
						<strong>Snag: </strong> Automatic
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Automatic Snag indicates that the problem that complicates the Fray is an automatic process, this could be indicative of a machine or process that is our of control, or be a failure of a mechanism or computer system that the PCs were relying on (Damage to a crucial Descendant can qualify), or it could be the result of the PCs own unconscious processes rebelling against their conscious decisions.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-sweeping">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-sweeping">
						<strong>Sweeping: </strong> Systems
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The PCs are helped by systems they or someone else has put in place, maybe the Police turn up and help, maybe the guy on Tech Support really knows his stuff and fixed the issues, or maybe the insurance pays out, covering that nasty damages or hospital bill.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
		 <details class="t13ne-revelation-fancy"><summary><strong>Revelation</strong></summary><details class="t13ne-about"><summary><strong>About: </strong> Dominant </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information revealed is about the Dominant Conflict Embodiment. Sometimes they are Characters, but almost as often they are Locations, Quests, Ordeals, or similar. Where this is true the Revelation should reveal details about future Quests the Pressed Characters must complete, or places they should visit. The Revelation can be little more than a call-back or foreshadowing if handled correct.</p></details><details class="t13ne-info"><summary><strong>Info: </strong> When? </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Information about an event that is happening, will happen, or already did, take place.</p></details><details class="t13ne-vector"><summary><strong>Vector: </strong> Argument </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Perhaps you overhear an argument that just happens to contain info you need, or maybe while you are having an argument something comes to light.</p></details><details class="t13ne-alternate"><summary><strong>Alternate: </strong> Wants </summary><p class="t13ne-description">What does the Embodiment want?
