You may not know how, or why, they are going to steal the Sceptre of Brass, but you do know they are after it.</p></details><details class="t13ne-detail"><summary><strong>Detail: </strong> Doubtful </summary><p class="t13ne-description">These details are probably mostly lies. Unlikely to be true, they hint at something that, at the very least, you hope cannot be.</p></details></details>
		 <details class="t13ne-ordeal-fancy"><summary><strong>Ordeal</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-ordeal-type"><strong>Ordeal-Type </strong> — Opposed Ordeal</p><p class="t13ne-stages"><strong>Stages </strong> — 6</p><p class="t13ne-ordeal-spread"><strong>Ordeal-Spread </strong> — Block</p><p class="t13ne-stakes"><strong>Stakes </strong> — Extreme</p><p class="t13ne-test"><strong>Test </strong> — Compete (Trial)</p><p class="t13ne-obstacles"><strong>Obstacles </strong> — 1 Obstacle in every Stage</p><p class="t13ne-suggested-action"><strong>Suggested-Action </strong> — Attack</p><details class="t13ne-stage"><summary><strong>Stage: </strong> Competition </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The route ahead involves some extra competition. Maybe the race you are in suddenly has a stampede cross it, or you have to reach the level crossing before the train, the road runs along side. Then again it could just mean that what you thought was a dash is now something else.</p></details><details class="t13ne-motional"><summary><strong>Motional: </strong> Dangerous Riding Flee </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Ride! Pedal faster! We need to get to high ground, ahead of the flood-waters.</p></details><details class="t13ne-fight"><summary><strong>Fight: </strong> Bullies and Thugs </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The enemy is a larger group of Cast Extras (or Force-of-Nature for powerful Characters) who attempt to inflict harm on the Party, usually trying to humiliate or steal from the PCs. They rarely intend to kill, and will usually retreat if any PC or member is seriously injured.</p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Red/Purple Carnivore</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> Hunters, Broccen, Vermis, Labyrinthine, All-Walkers</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> Megafauna – Large</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong> Tiger / Python / Alligator / Polar Bear / Large Shark</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> These Paths are usually lost in any area where the Labyrinth is strong.</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-cardwrap-jsvg" id="cardrewritebox48" data-mode="jsvg" data-wyrd="1" data-yarn="1"><img src="https://www.cjmoseley.dev.cc/wp-content/plugins/t13ne/images/cards-svg/10S.svg" class="t13ne-svgcard"><div class="t13ne-svgpips"><div class="t13ne-textpips">10<!-- write suit--><span class="t13ne-symbol"><span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></span><details class="t13ne-suit"><summary></summary><p class="t13ne-name"><strong>Suit: </strong>Spades <span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></p><p><strong>Ordeal Trump: </strong><span class="t13ne-trump">Attack</span></p><p class="t13ne-desc"><strong>Description: </strong>The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.</p><p class="t13ne-tarot"><strong>Tarot Meaning: </strong>Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.</p><p class="t13ne-otext"><strong>Ordeal Text: </strong>Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.</p><p class="t13ne-parry"><strong>Parry: </strong><strong>Redirect-Attack:</strong> The damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.</p><p class="t13ne-soak"><strong>Soak: </strong><strong>Counter-Attack:</strong> The matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate. </p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-svg-facet"><div>Gossamer</div></div><div class="t13ne-wound"><div><strong class="t13ne-woundname">Cripple</strong><details><summary></summary><strong>Cripple</strong><p class="t13ne-base-pips">Base Pips to summon: 12</p><p class="t13ne-min-pips">Minimum Pips Required: 10</p><p class="t13ne-desc">The Target is seriously wounded, losing access to whole abilities. This can even result in Coma.</p><details class="t13ne-roll"><summary>Rules</summary>Lose 1 Annex Slot (usually the largest one involved in the Roll) until Healed. Once healed Proficiencies may be repurchased for 1 Chi each. Then the Annex will regenerate at Phoenix / Incarna Score Boons per Day. It may be purchased back for the normal cost (Boon in Chi + 2/5/8/12) instantly (or altered and purchased back as a new Annex, if narratively appropriate).</details><details class="t13ne-ordeal"><summary>Ordeal Rules</summary>Crippled Annexes cannot be used as long as the Wound is in play. If applied to a Personality Annex (or a Descendant Master Annex) then the Alternate is unconscious (or the Descendant is lost or useless). They may not use that Alternate (or Descendant) until the Wound is healed (or found / fixed).</details><details class="t13ne-psych"><summary>Psych Wounds</summary><details class="t13ne-negative"><summary>Negative Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Automatically affects the Personality Annex. No Proficiencies or Annexes are lost, but Character loses consciousness at sight of the source until healed.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Automatically affects the Personality Annex. Loses consciousness until Healed (not just when the source is present).</p></details><details class="t13ne-positive"><summary>Positive Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Consciousness is interrupted for a few moments (1 Phase). May Gain a Skill Annex/Descendant or 3 Proficiencies.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Target loses consciousness (or at least responsiveness) until Stabilised. Gains a new Skill Annex/Descendant.</p></details></details></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-tarot-svg"><details><summary class="t13ne-wyrdtarot"><strong> Wyrd Tarot: </strong> Wound</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong> Normal: </strong>Draw and Apply one Unsoakable Wound to the Target.</p><p class="t13ne-crossed"><strong> Crossed: </strong>Remove the largest Wound from the Target.</p></details></div><div class="t13ne-yarn-fancy">
			<details><summary><strong>Yarn</strong> <br>The Lion</summary>
			<span class="t13ne-description">The Lion card represents strength, not just individually (which it does) but also strength in numbers. The Lion may indicate also heroic deeds and actions or proud behaviour. The Lion can be seen as a symbol of success and power, but more often it represents a test of that strength, and shows that pride comes before a fall.</span>
			<span class="t13ne-significator">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-significator"><strong>Significator</strong></summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-plot"><strong>Plot: </strong> The Lion plot is a plot about strength (personal or numeric), heroics (hunting or battle prowess are likely) and often pride (usually as part of a dramatic fall).</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-char"><strong>Character: </strong> The Lion Significator indicates a Character who is proud and regal. They Gain Yin when they act proud or mention their accomplishments. They Gain Yang when they demonstrate their strength or physical ability. They also gain +1 Success Levels to any action that demonstrates physical prowess and strength.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-scene"><strong>Scene: </strong> The Lion plot is a plot about strength (personal or numeric), heroics (hunting or battle prowess are likely) and often pride (usually as part of a dramatic fall).</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-scene">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-scene-type">
						<strong>Scene: </strong> Finale
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Finale is a final Ordeal (or Quest or Warp), a great battle, or Revelation. This is where the Conflict comes to a head. The Stakes will be higher (High or Extreme) and there will terrible consequences for failure.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
						<strong>Conflict Embodiment: </strong> Rational
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Conflict embodies each side of the Conflict into Characters who are in direct Conflict.  This is the classic Heroes verses Bad Guys plot.This may follow a period of Tension, or not. Often the Rational Embodiment creates a Monster.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>

			<span class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
						<strong>Facet Embodiment: </strong> Incarna
					</summary>
					<section class="t13ne-description">The Conflict Embodies one Facet as a Character or Descendant Incarna. Often the easiest Embodiment to create Fantastical Characters, but not often suitable to all Genres and Conflicts. Also this tends to to make for a very obvious Conflict.</section>
				</details>
			</span>
			 <div class="t13ne-archetypes"><details><summary><strong>Archetype Character: </strong>Soldier</summary> <details class="t13ne-Persona"><summary><strong>Persona: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Gladiator</span>
      </summary>
      <details class="t13ne-motivation"><summary><strong>Motivation: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">The Gladiator or “Contestant” is driven by a desire to compete against others. They usually want to win, but that isn't actually important to most Gladiators. Gladiators may have elaborate codes of honour that force them to obey all the rules of a contest, but others will cheat to win, there really is no way to tell one from the other.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-avoid"><summary><strong>Avoid: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">The Gladiator avoids boredom by finding a contest or game to play.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-gain-chi"><summary><strong>Gain Chi: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description"><span>Gain Chi when you compete against another, argue, or fight.</span><p>Ordeal gain Chi when you play a ♠.</p></span></details></details>   <details class="t13ne-core"><summary><strong>Core: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Wielder</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">When the character is holding/wearing or using a specific descendant/trophy/monolith/artefact add a success level.</span></details><details class="t13ne-hitch"><summary><strong>Hitch: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Duty</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Whether its a duty to family, the law, or king and Country, some people are just driven by something greater than themselves.</span></details><p class="t13ne-description">The Soldier is a more refined Warrior, usually with better weapons and armour, more Duties and less Scars. Soldiers usually specialise according to the weapons they carry, and may be called Archer, Rifleman, Sniper, Paladin, Knight, etc</p></details></div>
			 <span class="t13ne-hook">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-hook">
						<strong>Hook: </strong> Pride Comes Before A Fall
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Story Hooks the Character with giving them recognition for some earlier story, this Story is often a Sequel to the earlier episode. Usually makes a Gain (often a Prize, Medal or Award) as acknowledgement of the previous actions of the PC.</aside>
					<details class="t13ne-hook-aspect">
						<summary><strong>Hook Aspect: </strong>Hitches</summary>
						<section class="t13ne-description">The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.</section>
					</details>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-obstacle-fancy"><details class="t13ne-obstacle"><summary><strong>Obstacle:</strong>Lion</summary><p class="t13ne-description">The Obstacle may be a Beast Guardian (such as a dog or actual lion), but is more normally a test of either the physical body (strength) or humility and true sense of worth (pride). Something may have to be lifted, or something given up to proceed.</p></details></span>
			<span class="t13ne-gain">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-gain">
						<strong>Gain </strong> 
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Powerful Monster Ally, Super (or Power) Annex or Super-Skill Descendant</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-fray">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-fray">
						<strong>Fray: </strong> Encounter
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">An Encounter is a Fight against a smaller number of similar powered foes, or a larger group of weaker foes. The Encounter is intended to insert a bit of action, and drama without risking the PCs too much. An Encounter should wear at the party, maybe Crippling or Maiming a few Characters and perhaps giving one Character a new Hitch (Mortal+), Extras (of any size) may die, including Cast Extras. You may want to kill a PC during this Fray if the Stakes are already elevated.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-snag">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-snag">
						<strong>Snag: </strong> Test of Strength
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Lion Snag is a test of strength, this might be a literal strength test, where the PC must break in a door, or lift a gate, or it may be a more subtle complication where the PCs specific abilities are tested by a Rival, Ally, Mentor, or Opposed Embodiment. This complication can involve asking questions like "How many troops can the PC defeat at once?" and testing that theory. Note that the Test is usually not intended to destroy, and you can capture the party with a "Captured" Confined Hitch rather than a "Dead" Scars Hitch.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-sweeping">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-sweeping">
						<strong>Sweeping: </strong> The Lion Sleeps
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The PCs get to rest and recover, but so does the Opposed Embodiment. In fact the Opposed Embodiment and PCs may both make a Gain or Revelation during this Sweeping.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
		 <details class="t13ne-revelation-fancy"><summary><strong>Revelation</strong></summary><details class="t13ne-about"><summary><strong>About: </strong> Dominant </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information revealed is about the Dominant Conflict Embodiment. Sometimes they are Characters, but almost as often they are Locations, Quests, Ordeals, or similar. Where this is true the Revelation should reveal details about future Quests the Pressed Characters must complete, or places they should visit. The Revelation can be little more than a call-back or foreshadowing if handled correct.</p></details><details class="t13ne-info"><summary><strong>Info: </strong> Where? </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Information about a Location.</p></details><details class="t13ne-vector"><summary><strong>Vector: </strong> Exchange </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Maybe you overhear an exchanging of words, or maybe you are part of the exchange, trading information you know, for information back.</p></details><details class="t13ne-alternate"><summary><strong>Alternate: </strong> Ruins </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information may be about a location, fallen into disrepair, or some hint of the Hitches of the Embodiment.</p></details><details class="t13ne-detail"><summary><strong>Detail: </strong> Coherent </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Coherent details all point to the same conclusion. All the details line up pointed at what must appear a forgone conclusion. Of course, just because it all lines up doesn't mean it can't be wrong or misinterpreted.</p></details></details>
		 <details class="t13ne-ordeal-fancy"><summary><strong>Ordeal</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-ordeal-type"><strong>Ordeal-Type </strong> — Opposed Ordeal</p><p class="t13ne-stages"><strong>Stages </strong> — 6</p><p class="t13ne-ordeal-spread"><strong>Ordeal-Spread </strong> — Pyramid</p><p class="t13ne-stakes"><strong>Stakes </strong> — Extreme</p><p class="t13ne-test"><strong>Test </strong> — Confrontation (Trial)</p><p class="t13ne-obstacles"><strong>Obstacles </strong> — 1 Obstacle in every Non-Court Stage, 2 in every Court Stage</p><p class="t13ne-suggested-action"><strong>Suggested-Action </strong> — Hack</p><details class="t13ne-stage"><summary><strong>Stage: </strong> Encounter </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way ahead involves an encounter, and often a fight, perhaps with the Dominant Embodiment, or perhaps with a different foe. Wiles, or weapons, may be needed to get by, and it is bound to slow you down. This might be a place where the route arcs back, allowing shots to be fired between Chaser and Chasee, but more often is a third party encounter that both sides must contend with (unaffiliated street gang / raiders, bear etc)</p></details><details class="t13ne-motional"><summary><strong>Motional: </strong> Foot Flee </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The Robots are hunting, they've got guns, they're coming.</p></details><details class="t13ne-fight"><summary><strong>Fight: </strong> Army, </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Large scale military battles involve Armies. Usually two of them, but PCs may not always have the resources to create their own army. The opposition however has hundreds of Grunts (or Cast Extras) who have military grade equipment suitable for their time period (Chain-mail and sword, tac-vest and assault rifle, power-armour and a pew-pew-pulsed-plasma-pistol).</p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Red/Purple Carnivore</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> Hunters, Broccen, Vermis, Labyrinthine, All-Walkers</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> Megafauna – Largest</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong> Salt Water Crocodile/ Whale / Dinosaur</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> These Paths tend to be lost on Earth, although a few cling on.</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-cardwrap-jsvg" id="cardrewritebox49" data-mode="jsvg" data-wyrd="1" data-yarn="1"><img src="https://www.cjmoseley.dev.cc/wp-content/plugins/t13ne/images/cards-svg/JS.svg" class="t13ne-svgcard"><div class="t13ne-svgpips"><div class="t13ne-textpips">11<!-- write suit--><span class="t13ne-symbol"><span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></span><details class="t13ne-suit"><summary></summary><p class="t13ne-name"><strong>Suit: </strong>Spades <span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></p><p><strong>Ordeal Trump: </strong><span class="t13ne-trump">Attack</span></p><p class="t13ne-desc"><strong>Description: </strong>The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.</p><p class="t13ne-tarot"><strong>Tarot Meaning: </strong>Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.</p><p class="t13ne-otext"><strong>Ordeal Text: </strong>Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.</p><p class="t13ne-parry"><strong>Parry: </strong><strong>Redirect-Attack:</strong> The damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.</p><p class="t13ne-soak"><strong>Soak: </strong><strong>Counter-Attack:</strong> The matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate. </p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-svg-facet"><div>Yonder</div></div><div class="t13ne-wound"><div><strong class="t13ne-woundname">Cripple</strong><details><summary></summary><strong>Cripple</strong><p class="t13ne-base-pips">Base Pips to summon: 12</p><p class="t13ne-min-pips">Minimum Pips Required: 10</p><p class="t13ne-desc">The Target is seriously wounded, losing access to whole abilities. This can even result in Coma.</p><details class="t13ne-roll"><summary>Rules</summary>Lose 1 Annex Slot (usually the largest one involved in the Roll) until Healed. Once healed Proficiencies may be repurchased for 1 Chi each. Then the Annex will regenerate at Phoenix / Incarna Score Boons per Day. It may be purchased back for the normal cost (Boon in Chi + 2/5/8/12) instantly (or altered and purchased back as a new Annex, if narratively appropriate).</details><details class="t13ne-ordeal"><summary>Ordeal Rules</summary>Crippled Annexes cannot be used as long as the Wound is in play. If applied to a Personality Annex (or a Descendant Master Annex) then the Alternate is unconscious (or the Descendant is lost or useless). They may not use that Alternate (or Descendant) until the Wound is healed (or found / fixed).</details><details class="t13ne-psych"><summary>Psych Wounds</summary><details class="t13ne-negative"><summary>Negative Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Automatically affects the Personality Annex. No Proficiencies or Annexes are lost, but Character loses consciousness at sight of the source until healed.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Automatically affects the Personality Annex. Loses consciousness until Healed (not just when the source is present).</p></details><details class="t13ne-positive"><summary>Positive Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Consciousness is interrupted for a few moments (1 Phase). May Gain a Skill Annex/Descendant or 3 Proficiencies.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Target loses consciousness (or at least responsiveness) until Stabilised. Gains a new Skill Annex/Descendant.</p></details></details></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-tarot-svg"><details><summary class="t13ne-wyrdtarot"><strong> Wyrd Tarot: </strong> Gun</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong> Normal: </strong>Target may ignore Range Difficulties and add +1 Success Levels when acting at Range while atop pile.</p><p class="t13ne-crossed"><strong> Crossed: </strong>Target takes -2 Success Levels and Range costs +18 Pips (+36 Score) to all actions while atop pile.</p></details></div><div class="t13ne-yarn-fancy">
			<details><summary><strong>Yarn</strong> <br>The Explorer</summary>
			<span class="t13ne-description">The Explorer card represents the urge to wander, to seek out new places, and make new discoveries. Exploration, investigation and travel are all indicated by the card, but there is another side to the Explorer, sometimes they come as an invader, or a crusader. If you are the Explorer it warns you that you may not be seen as just a tourist by the natives.</span>
			<span class="t13ne-significator">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-significator"><strong>Significator</strong></summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-plot"><strong>Plot: </strong> The Explorer Plot is another good world-builder (although the Yarn-Teller does have to a little more work. The Explorer plot is about exploring a blank map. The map may be of a new land or new world, but the Characters are Hooked to explore and investigate the map, of course each area they go may consider them invaders.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-char"><strong>Character: </strong> The Explorer Significator indicates a Character that lives to explore and investigate. They Gain Yin when they are travel more than 1 kilometre. They Gain Yang when they explore or investigate a place or person. They Gain Chi when they make notes, drawings or maps of a location, person or thing. They also gain +1 Success Levels when they are in unfamiliar territory.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-scene"><strong>Scene: </strong> The Explorer Plot is another good world-builder (although the Yarn-Teller does have to a little more work. The Explorer plot is about exploring a blank map. The map may be of a new land or new world, but the Characters are Hooked to explore and investigate the map, of course each area they go may consider them invaders.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-scene">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-scene-type">
						<strong>Scene: </strong> Gain
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Gains give something to one of the Characters to help move the Story on. Sometimes this might be payment for a job they did, or it might be a discovery that will directly connect to the Story. This may be information. It is normal to play a separate card to define the actual Gain that is made.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
						<strong>Conflict Embodiment: </strong> Rational
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Conflict embodies each side of the Conflict into Characters who are in direct Conflict.  This is the classic Heroes verses Bad Guys plot.This may follow a period of Tension, or not. Often the Rational Embodiment creates a Monster.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>

			<span class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
						<strong>Facet Embodiment: </strong> Monster
					</summary>
					<section class="t13ne-description">The Conflict Embodies one Facet as a Character Monster Facet.This Monster might be a Dragon, an Orc Horde, or just a human Villain. Monsters can be great fun, but are rarely subtle. Where possible when using Monster embodiments, the Plot may want to keep the exact nature of the Monster a secret for as long as it can.</section>
				</details>
			</span>
			 <div class="t13ne-archetypes"><details><summary><strong>Archetype Character: </strong>Captain</summary> <details class="t13ne-Persona"><summary><strong>Persona: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Scout</span>
      </summary>
      <details class="t13ne-motivation"><summary><strong>Motivation: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">The Scout is motivated by a desire to see new places and things. They like to move and be free to roam.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-avoid"><summary><strong>Avoid: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Scouts prefer to avoid being slowed or encumbered by heavy armour or equipment. They will always travel as light as they can to allow them the most freedom of movement.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-gain-chi"><summary><strong>Gain Chi: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description"><span>Gain Chi when you gain information, see a new place, answer a question, or tell someone what you have experienced.</span><p>Ordeal gain Chi when you act at Range (paying Range costs or using Range Nimbeds).</p></span></details></details>   <details class="t13ne-core"><summary><strong>Core: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Dominator</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Gain Chi when you give orders, exert authority or power.</span></details><details class="t13ne-hitch"><summary><strong>Hitch: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Duty</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Whether its a duty to family, the law, or king and Country, some people are just driven by something greater than themselves.</span></details><p class="t13ne-description">Captains are commanding officers who follow others orders and act as eyes and ears abroad. They are investigators, scouts and spies as much as they are explorers or leaders.</p></details></div>
			 <span class="t13ne-hook">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-hook">
						<strong>Hook: </strong> Visitor
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Story opens with an unexpected visitor that may  bring the PCs a Revelation and a Gain. This Visitor is usually a friend or Ally, rather than a Rival or enemy, but occasionally it will be a stranger, who claims a connection that will later be validated or proved a lie.</aside>
					<details class="t13ne-hook-aspect">
						<summary><strong>Hook Aspect: </strong>Hitches</summary>
						<section class="t13ne-description">The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.</section>
					</details>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-obstacle-fancy"><details class="t13ne-obstacle"><summary><strong>Obstacle:</strong>Maze</summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way is blocked, but by a maze or labyrinth that represents a complex, difficult to memorise or work out route. A Maze can be handled as an Ordeal within an Ordeal (with finer detail on the Stages, corridors instead of roads, or cubicles instead of rooms), or simply as a Navigation test, fail and you are lost in the maze... Fail again and you may be lost more than that.</p></details></span>
			<span class="t13ne-gain">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-gain">
						<strong>Gain </strong> 
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Discover a new or lost Location / Real Estate / Province / Dark Territory</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-fray">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-fray">
						<strong>Fray: </strong> Explore
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Explore Fray is an exploration of the Conflict, it is time to explore some aspect of the interactions of the Facets in the Conflict. This may be done by actually exploring a Locations that Embodies the Conflict directly, or investigating a Descendant that has an Annex like the Conflict's.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-snag">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-snag">
						<strong>Snag: </strong> Lost
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The PCs actions are complicated by there being no map, no GPS or the streets just seem different. The PCs or those they seek are lost, this can be due to High Strangeness like a Yarn-Teller or Time-Traveller side-stepping or ret-conning the area and making the map change.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-sweeping">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-sweeping">
						<strong>Sweeping: </strong> Map
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The PCs discover, explore, or gain access to a map or directions, that includes a secret passage, short cut or hidden way. This will allow them to appear or escape via this short cut and avoid any police, guards or enemy forces. It may allow them to skip Stages of the next Fray Ordeal or similar.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
		 <details class="t13ne-revelation-fancy"><summary><strong>Revelation</strong></summary><details class="t13ne-info"><summary><strong>Info: </strong> Who? </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Information about a Character, usually an Embodiment. It might be their name, or genealogy, perhaps their CV.</p></details><p class="t13ne-about"><strong>About </strong> — Opposed Embodiment / A Rival</p><details class="t13ne-vector"><summary><strong>Vector: </strong> Communications </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information comes via a communications technology, perhaps the radio, or a cell phone call, or in another age a messenger or pigeon.</p></details><details class="t13ne-alternate"><summary><strong>Alternate: </strong> Changes </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information is about changes that have occurred to or because of the Embodiment. It can also signify that the information itself changes. Appearing as one thing to begin with, but then being later revealed by a detail to be something else (or could magically change).</p></details><details class="t13ne-detail"><summary><strong>Detail: </strong> Delicate </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Delicate details are the finer points, little bits of information that might be overlooked easily, and could even be destroyed accidentally before they can be properly examined. A delicate detail might indicate a quirk, vulnerability, or flaw in some cases, but will be hard to spot. "He was a rakish, whip of a man, dressed in black leather, mail and silks. His eyes were dark and tired, close set, above a thin nose, broad cheekbones and confident grin..." </p></details></details>
		 <details class="t13ne-ordeal-fancy"><summary><strong>Ordeal</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-ordeal-type"><strong>Ordeal-Type </strong> — Opposed Ordeal</p><p class="t13ne-stages"><strong>Stages </strong> — 7</p><p class="t13ne-ordeal-spread"><strong>Ordeal-Spread </strong> — Map-Grid</p><p class="t13ne-stakes"><strong>Stakes </strong> — Blended Low-Extreme</p><p class="t13ne-test"><strong>Test </strong> — Shoot (Yonder)</p><p class="t13ne-obstacles"><strong>Obstacles </strong> — 1 Obstacle in every Non-Court Stage, 2 in every Court Stage</p><p class="t13ne-suggested-action"><strong>Suggested-Action </strong> — Shoot</p><details class="t13ne-stage"><summary><strong>Stage: </strong> Jump Stunt </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Jump (or balance) over danger. Leaping between catwalks over molten metal, missing slats on rope bridges over unlikely crocodile-filled rapids, or a rooftop run over a burning building, are all good examples.</p></details><details class="t13ne-motional"><summary><strong>Motional: </strong> Swim Flee </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Swim faster! They're right behind us!</p></details><details class="t13ne-fight"><summary><strong>Fight: </strong> Knight </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The Fight is against a powerful soldier or military type, well equipped, with Attack and Defence dedicated Annexes. The Knight is at least equal in power to the most powerful PC, and has Armour and at least two Weapon Descendants. The Knight may also be a Monster.<!-- t13ne-shortcode "%7B%22type%22%3A%22facetsuitaspect%22%2C%22suit%22%3A%224%22%2C%22aspect%22%3A%22Monster%22%7D"// --></p><div class="t13ne-tablewrap"><table class="t13ne-table Monster"><thead><tr><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</th><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Gossamer</td><td><strong>Wiry</strong> —The Monster is immensely physically powerful, although often its form may not reveal it. It often demonstrates supernatural power as well as strength and toughness. A small, strong Goblin, a robot that looks like a little girl, a cybernetically enhanced rat, or even a killer doll are all examples of a Gossamer Monster. The Wiry Monster can refill Gossamer Score cards to its Monster Pool whenever it is underestimated. A Wiry monster may add their current Monster Pool size to any Score or Pips to do with their Monstrous strength or toughness. The Monster must spend at least one card from their Monster Pool when they physically attack.</td></tr><tr><td>Inertia</td><td><strong>Unstoppable</strong> —The Monster is inexorable, it does not stop and cannot be killed by any normal means. They start play with a Monster Pool of Inertia Score cards. Unstoppable Monsters can ignore a number of Wounds equal to the number of cards in their Monster Pool. Wounds stacked beyond the Monster Pool affect them as normal. Inexorable Monsters can also ignore all Hitches with a Boon less than their Inertia Score while they have cards in their Monster Pool. Whenever an Unstoppable Monster is actually damaged it must spend cards from its Monster Pool while remaining motionless for Inertia Score as Chi Extend Duration to discard all Wounds less than Mortal in nature, and Stabilise all Mortal + Wounds (which may then be ignored if the Unstoppable has enough cards remaining in its Monster Pool). Examples of the Inertial Monster type include the Hungry Dead (Zombies) and most robotic assassins (especially ones from the future).</td></tr><tr><td>Key</td><td><strong>Hunter</strong> —The Monster is a hunter or tracker, it should favour harrying attacks and hit and run tactics. Typically such a Monster will vanish or flee if it is wounded, only to reappear when healed. The hunter may be fooled by false trails, but never for long. Hunters may add Key Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they spot a track, clue, or similar, of the subject of their hunt. Hunters receive a bonus equal to the number of cards in the Monster Pool to any tracking, attacking or hunting actions if they also spend a card from the Monster Pool on the action. Hunters are usually immune to Carnage and Mortal Wounds until the Zenith of the story (such wounds are taken as Crippling Wounds instead, but at a cost of one card from the Monster Pool). In T13, the archetypal version of this Monster is a Bulmäs Pack member from the Wild Hunt, but any Werewolf or Animal Shifter would probably qualify, as would robot cowboys in theme parks that have gone wrong, and any fantasy world's Elven ranger. </td></tr><tr><td>Miasma</td><td><strong>Mutant</strong> —The monster is a mutant or chimerical creature made from parts of others. This monster type is the oldest perhaps, you’ll find examples throughout the ancient world, particularly in Greek myth. The Mutant will often be larger, and may be malformed or unsymmetrical, with odd numbers of limbs, eyes, horns, or even heads. The Chimera will blend multiple animals together to create a new creature that combines features of all the “parent” creatures, preserving the best of all species. Mutant Monsters start with Miasma Score Monster Pool. They add Monster Pool Size as the Boon of Proficiency less "Mutation" Annex that they can use to grow larger or mimic their “parents” abilities. 1950s B-movie monsters are often of this type, giant ants, lizards, spiders, wasps, and even rabbits have been used. Classically of course, Cyclopses, Centaurs, Minotaurs, Satyrs, Gryphons (or Griffins), hippogriffs, dragons, sphinxes, manticores and of course the Chimera itself are all examples. You can also find examples in superheroes, especially those created by radioactive arachnids, or those with an X-gene.</td></tr><tr><td>Trial</td><td><strong>Killer</strong> —The Monster is a deadly Combat specialist of some sort. Killers add Trial Draw cards to their Monster Pool each time they kill someone. Killers automatically add +1 Score/Pip per card of Monster Pool to any attack, usually as Penetration or Range costs. Killers must spend Monster Pool cards whenever they will make a Trick. Killers can be drug-addled assassins, crazed shooters, or just steel-souled soldiers.</td></tr><tr><td>Yonder</td><td><strong>Alien</strong> —The Monster is an alien, an outsider. It may just be a metaphor for alienation, or literally be an extra-(or ultra)-terrestrial. Such monsters are usually obvious and inhuman, not just to look at, but in the way they think and behave. They have apparently supernatural powers and abilities, but often make do with advanced technologies (in the form of additional Descendants). They may draw and play Yonder Draw cards to and from their Monster Pool with their alien / inhuman / weird actions. Their Monster Pool number of cards Reduced adds Success Levels to any action to do with motion, survival or the alien's technologies. From little grey Eusaiveans, through to pod people and pepper-pot shaped engines of hate, all the way to face-hugging xenomorphic space-wasps, there are plenty of aliens.</td></tr></tbody><tfoot><tr><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</td><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</td></tr></tfoot></table></div><p></p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Red/Purple Carnivore</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> Hunters, Broccen, Vermis, Labyrinthine, All-Walkers</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> Man-paths/Broccen Territory</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong> Human / Groonians / Eelafin / Eusaivean / Mer-person / Bigfoot / Giant / Goblin, etc</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> Broccen may use Jack’s to dig new tunnels in the Lea, creating species to fill niches. Jacks may also be used to create Chimerical changes, blending parts of different animals together, each Jack adds another place the Bulmäs can play Lea cards.</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-cardwrap-jsvg" id="cardrewritebox50" data-mode="jsvg" data-wyrd="1" data-yarn="1"><img src="https://www.cjmoseley.dev.cc/wp-content/plugins/t13ne/images/cards-svg/QS.svg" class="t13ne-svgcard"><div class="t13ne-svgpips"><div class="t13ne-textpips">12<!-- write suit--><span class="t13ne-symbol"><span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></span><details class="t13ne-suit"><summary></summary><p class="t13ne-name"><strong>Suit: </strong>Spades <span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></p><p><strong>Ordeal Trump: </strong><span class="t13ne-trump">Attack</span></p><p class="t13ne-desc"><strong>Description: </strong>The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.</p><p class="t13ne-tarot"><strong>Tarot Meaning: </strong>Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.</p><p class="t13ne-otext"><strong>Ordeal Text: </strong>Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.</p><p class="t13ne-parry"><strong>Parry: </strong><strong>Redirect-Attack:</strong> The damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.</p><p class="t13ne-soak"><strong>Soak: </strong><strong>Counter-Attack:</strong> The matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate. </p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-svg-facet"><div>Key</div></div><div class="t13ne-wound"><div><strong class="t13ne-woundname">Mortal</strong><details><summary></summary><strong>Mortal</strong><p class="t13ne-base-pips">Base Pips to summon: 16</p><p class="t13ne-min-pips">Minimum Pips Required: 12</p><p class="t13ne-desc">The Target is now dying from the wound.</p><details class="t13ne-roll"><summary>Rules</summary>Create a "Dying / Broken / Burning" quirk Hitch that slowly grows +1 Hitch Boon per Miasma Boon as Chi Limit Duration) until healed. If no Hitch can be increased by the Mortal Wound then decrease an appropriate Facet (Hitch, Incarna, Persona or Core usually) by a Boon equal to the Wounded Character’s Miasma Score. I-Ching, Personality, Proficiency slots and Annexes affected should be recalculated. If the Hitch becomes a Flaw the Wound will automatically upgrade to a Carnage Wound.</details><details class="t13ne-ordeal"><summary>Ordeal Rules</summary>Treat as though the Personality Annex or Master Annex has been Crippled, until the end of the Round. Then add a new "Dying / Broken / Burning" quirk Hitch with a Boon of Miasma Score (to a Max of Half the Incarna Boon). Or increase a quirk Hitch by 1 Boon at the end of each Round.</details><details class="t13ne-psych"><summary>Psych Wounds</summary><details class="t13ne-negative"><summary>Negative Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>As Normal but does not create a "Dying" Hitch, but instead a Scars (Mental/Emotional) / Fear / Fatigued / Lament / Taboo / Offensive / Doubt,  etc Hitch.  Plus Consciousness is usually lost until Stabilised.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Creates any normal Hitch type. At Yarn-Teller's discretion Mortal Wounds can also alter the Persona, or Core Facet choice (Ref arbitrates). Plus Consciousness is usually lost until healed.</p></details><details class="t13ne-positive"><summary>Positive Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>As Normal but creates a Scars (Mental/Emotional) / Duty / Dependance / Paranoia / Devotion / Madness / Vague, etc quirk Hitch.  Plus Consciousness is usually lost until Stabilised. May Gain a new Talent Annex / Descendant or Resolve a quirk Hitch.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>May create any Flaw Hitch. At Yarn-Teller's discretion Mortal Wounds can also alter the wounded character’s Persona, or Core Facet choices. Consciousness is usually lost until healed. Gains a new Talent Annex / Descendant or Resolves a quirk Handicap.</p></details></details></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-tarot-svg"><details><summary class="t13ne-wyrdtarot"><strong> Wyrd Tarot: </strong> Spot Hidden</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong> Normal: </strong>+2 Success Levels (+38 Score) to all Target's tests to do with perception and senses while atop pile.</p><p class="t13ne-crossed"><strong> Crossed: </strong>+2 Success Levels (+38 Score) to all Target's tests to do with hiding, sneaking, etc. while atop pile.</p></details></div><div class="t13ne-yarn-fancy">
			<details><summary><strong>Yarn</strong> <br>The Scope</summary>
			<span class="t13ne-description">The Scope card represents vision, examination, and focus. A scope can be used for assisted vision, it might be a telescope, a microscope, a periscope, reading glasses or a magnifying lens. It is a card that teaches us to focus on details, and to closely examine the facts or the scene.</span>
			<span class="t13ne-significator">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-significator"><strong>Significator</strong></summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-plot"><strong>Plot: </strong> The Scope Plot is a plot about investigation. Investigations can vary in type from genre and era, religious inquisitions, criminal investigations, scientific discoveries are all with in the scope of The Scope.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-char"><strong>Character: </strong> The Scope Significator indicates a Character that revels in watching and investigating. They Gain Yin when they make themselves comfortable, settle down, or adjust their position. They Gain Yang when they discuss events they have witnessed, take notes, or record information. They Gain Chi when they watch or observe an event, show, or process from beginning to end, focus on details or draw conclusions. They gain +1 Success Levels to all actions to do with sensing, recording, or understanding information.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-scene"><strong>Scene: </strong> The Scope Plot is a plot about investigation. Investigations can vary in type from genre and era, religious inquisitions, criminal investigations, scientific discoveries are all with in the scope of The Scope.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-scene">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-scene-type">
						<strong>Scene: </strong> Revelation
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Revelations reveal something about the Conflict or Plot to the Characters. Typically a Revelation tells the Characters something about the Villain, or the Prize they are after. Revelations usually consist of 2-5 cards that define: What the information is about, what the actual info is, the vector the Revelation is revealed by, and per haps some Alternative Information that might be revealed (in case you can't use the Info), and a detail that is involved somehow.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
						<strong>Conflict Embodiment: </strong> Rational
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Conflict embodies each side of the Conflict into Characters who are in direct Conflict.  This is the classic Heroes verses Bad Guys plot.This may follow a period of Tension, or not. Often the Rational Embodiment creates a Monster.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>

			<span class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
						<strong>Facet Embodiment: </strong> Monster
					</summary>
					<section class="t13ne-description">The Conflict Embodies one Facet as a Character Monster Facet.This Monster might be a Dragon, an Orc Horde, or just a human Villain. Monsters can be great fun, but are rarely subtle. Where possible when using Monster embodiments, the Plot may want to keep the exact nature of the Monster a secret for as long as it can.</section>
				</details>
			</span>
			 <div class="t13ne-archetypes"><details><summary><strong>Archetype Character: </strong>Spy</summary> <details class="t13ne-Persona"><summary><strong>Persona: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Scout</span>
      </summary>
      <details class="t13ne-motivation"><summary><strong>Motivation: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">The Scout is motivated by a desire to see new places and things. They like to move and be free to roam.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-avoid"><summary><strong>Avoid: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Scouts prefer to avoid being slowed or encumbered by heavy armour or equipment. They will always travel as light as they can to allow them the most freedom of movement.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-gain-chi"><summary><strong>Gain Chi: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description"><span>Gain Chi when you gain information, see a new place, answer a question, or tell someone what you have experienced.</span><p>Ordeal gain Chi when you act at Range (paying Range costs or using Range Nimbeds).</p></span></details></details>   <details class="t13ne-core"><summary><strong>Core: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Pretender</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Character may choose a second Persona and gain Chi from it also. Characters may change this second Persona for Heresy Boon points of Yang (Heresy Score Chi).</span></details><details class="t13ne-hitch"><summary><strong>Hitch: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Dependence</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">We all have things that depend on us and things on which we depend. From family and society to addictions, or just having to plug a device in to get it too work. Children depend on their parents, and both can represent this with a Dependence Hitch.</span></details><p class="t13ne-description">The Spy may be a foreign agent, or could just be the curtain-twitcher down the street.</p></details></div>
			 <span class="t13ne-hook">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-hook">
						<strong>Hook: </strong> Hints
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Hook hints at the Story with clues, background details, and no direct exposition of any sort. The Hook may even modify the Revelations of the Frame to make them into more specific details and less broad exposition. These Hooks may require setting multiple times during other stories before they finally catch the PC.</aside>
					<details class="t13ne-hook-aspect">
						<summary><strong>Hook Aspect: </strong>Hitches</summary>
						<section class="t13ne-description">The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.</section>
					</details>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-obstacle-fancy"><details class="t13ne-obstacle"><summary><strong>Obstacle:</strong>Watched</summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way ahead is watched, usually by motion sensitive alarms, security cameras, guards or perhaps scrying. Any unhidden motion into the area (and some hidden probably) will cause alarms to be raised and security to completely block this route.</p></details></span>
			<span class="t13ne-gain">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-gain">
						<strong>Gain </strong> 
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Improve Alternate Integration</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-fray">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-fray">
						<strong>Fray: </strong> Watch
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Watch Frays are Ordeals where PCs have to watch, observe and perceive something. It could be that they are watching their favourite TV show, or a film, the Ordeal will be the Psychological effects of the movie and the story, or perhaps the PCs are on a stake out, watching the enemy base, then again military watches are something else again. Who knows what might attack the party during the night.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-snag">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-snag">
						<strong>Snag: </strong> Scrutiny
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The PCs are being watched, observed by someone, and it could complicate the Fray, for example speak-easy's always had lookouts who would warn of the Police approaching. Perhaps the scrutiny follows the Fray, with cameras, lights and microphones being thrust in their face moments after the Fray, as they are asked how they let the bad guys get away, or how you justify so many dead and injured...</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-sweeping">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-sweeping">
						<strong>Sweeping: </strong> Vision
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The PCs finds, sees, or experiences some Revelation, and gives them a clue (usually a Key Test to spot) to a pair of Gains.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
		 <details class="t13ne-revelation-fancy"><summary><strong>Revelation</strong></summary><details class="t13ne-about"><summary><strong>About: </strong> Dominant </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information revealed is about the Dominant Conflict Embodiment. Sometimes they are Characters, but almost as often they are Locations, Quests, Ordeals, or similar. Where this is true the Revelation should reveal details about future Quests the Pressed Characters must complete, or places they should visit. The Revelation can be little more than a call-back or foreshadowing if handled correct.</p></details><details class="t13ne-info"><summary><strong>Info: </strong> Why? </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Information that answers a Why question, like why are they doing it, or why is it/they that way?</p></details><details class="t13ne-vector"><summary><strong>Vector: </strong> Letters </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information is written, usually as a letter or email. It may be a correspondence to the Character, or be something they found or intercepted.</p></details><details class="t13ne-alternate"><summary><strong>Alternate: </strong> Perceptions </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Perceptions represent the information that the Embodiment has about the Player/PCs. "I knew, that you knew. I also know what you planned to do about it."</p></details><details class="t13ne-detail"><summary><strong>Detail: </strong> Quick </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Quick details are of the blink and you'll miss it variety. They can be missed at first, and sometimes that will mean they are missed forever. Perhaps there was a flash of the face behind the mask, just after the smoke bomb went off. If the Player is lucky there may be video footage, a scrying attempt, or femite recordings that can be viewed later, in slow motion.</p></details></details>
		 <details class="t13ne-ordeal-fancy"><summary><strong>Ordeal</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-ordeal-type"><strong>Ordeal-Type </strong> — Opposed Ordeal</p><p class="t13ne-stages"><strong>Stages </strong> — 7</p><p class="t13ne-ordeal-spread"><strong>Ordeal-Spread </strong> — Map-Hex</p><p class="t13ne-stakes"><strong>Stakes </strong> — Blended Extreme-Soul</p><p class="t13ne-test"><strong>Test </strong> — Communicate (Yonder)</p><p class="t13ne-obstacles"><strong>Obstacles </strong> — 2 Obstacles in every Non-Court Stage, 3 in every Court Stage</p><p class="t13ne-suggested-action"><strong>Suggested-Action </strong> — Talk</p><details class="t13ne-stage"><summary><strong>Stage: </strong> Cliff Stunt </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The route ahead is very dangerous, mists hide details, the moisture coated rocks are treacherous underfoot, you are high, over broken spires of mist-shrouded rock, clinging to rough foot and handholds that were carved long ago.</p></details><details class="t13ne-motional"><summary><strong>Motional: </strong> Air Winged Flee </summary><p class="t13ne-description">You may not know who is chasing you, or what they may do, if they catch up to your Hippogriff...</p></details><details class="t13ne-fight"><summary><strong>Fight: </strong> Queen </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Queens come in two main types, politically powerful women (who may have a flair for violence, dark magic, manipulation or poisoning) and matriarchs noted for their huge (and surprisingly often violent, monstrous, or cannibalistic) families. Queens are often Monsters <!-- t13ne-shortcode "%7B%22type%22%3A%22facetsuitaspect%22%2C%22suit%22%3A%224%22%2C%22aspect%22%3A%22Monster%22%7D"// --></p><div class="t13ne-tablewrap"><table class="t13ne-table Monster"><thead><tr><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</th><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Gossamer</td><td><strong>Wiry</strong> —The Monster is immensely physically powerful, although often its form may not reveal it. It often demonstrates supernatural power as well as strength and toughness. A small, strong Goblin, a robot that looks like a little girl, a cybernetically enhanced rat, or even a killer doll are all examples of a Gossamer Monster. The Wiry Monster can refill Gossamer Score cards to its Monster Pool whenever it is underestimated. A Wiry monster may add their current Monster Pool size to any Score or Pips to do with their Monstrous strength or toughness. The Monster must spend at least one card from their Monster Pool when they physically attack.</td></tr><tr><td>Inertia</td><td><strong>Unstoppable</strong> —The Monster is inexorable, it does not stop and cannot be killed by any normal means. They start play with a Monster Pool of Inertia Score cards. Unstoppable Monsters can ignore a number of Wounds equal to the number of cards in their Monster Pool. Wounds stacked beyond the Monster Pool affect them as normal. Inexorable Monsters can also ignore all Hitches with a Boon less than their Inertia Score while they have cards in their Monster Pool. Whenever an Unstoppable Monster is actually damaged it must spend cards from its Monster Pool while remaining motionless for Inertia Score as Chi Extend Duration to discard all Wounds less than Mortal in nature, and Stabilise all Mortal + Wounds (which may then be ignored if the Unstoppable has enough cards remaining in its Monster Pool). Examples of the Inertial Monster type include the Hungry Dead (Zombies) and most robotic assassins (especially ones from the future).</td></tr><tr><td>Key</td><td><strong>Hunter</strong> —The Monster is a hunter or tracker, it should favour harrying attacks and hit and run tactics. Typically such a Monster will vanish or flee if it is wounded, only to reappear when healed. The hunter may be fooled by false trails, but never for long. Hunters may add Key Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they spot a track, clue, or similar, of the subject of their hunt. Hunters receive a bonus equal to the number of cards in the Monster Pool to any tracking, attacking or hunting actions if they also spend a card from the Monster Pool on the action. Hunters are usually immune to Carnage and Mortal Wounds until the Zenith of the story (such wounds are taken as Crippling Wounds instead, but at a cost of one card from the Monster Pool). In T13, the archetypal version of this Monster is a Bulmäs Pack member from the Wild Hunt, but any Werewolf or Animal Shifter would probably qualify, as would robot cowboys in theme parks that have gone wrong, and any fantasy world's Elven ranger. </td></tr><tr><td>Miasma</td><td><strong>Mutant</strong> —The monster is a mutant or chimerical creature made from parts of others. This monster type is the oldest perhaps, you’ll find examples throughout the ancient world, particularly in Greek myth. The Mutant will often be larger, and may be malformed or unsymmetrical, with odd numbers of limbs, eyes, horns, or even heads. The Chimera will blend multiple animals together to create a new creature that combines features of all the “parent” creatures, preserving the best of all species. Mutant Monsters start with Miasma Score Monster Pool. They add Monster Pool Size as the Boon of Proficiency less "Mutation" Annex that they can use to grow larger or mimic their “parents” abilities. 1950s B-movie monsters are often of this type, giant ants, lizards, spiders, wasps, and even rabbits have been used. Classically of course, Cyclopses, Centaurs, Minotaurs, Satyrs, Gryphons (or Griffins), hippogriffs, dragons, sphinxes, manticores and of course the Chimera itself are all examples. You can also find examples in superheroes, especially those created by radioactive arachnids, or those with an X-gene.</td></tr><tr><td>Trial</td><td><strong>Killer</strong> —The Monster is a deadly Combat specialist of some sort. Killers add Trial Draw cards to their Monster Pool each time they kill someone. Killers automatically add +1 Score/Pip per card of Monster Pool to any attack, usually as Penetration or Range costs. Killers must spend Monster Pool cards whenever they will make a Trick. Killers can be drug-addled assassins, crazed shooters, or just steel-souled soldiers.</td></tr><tr><td>Yonder</td><td><strong>Alien</strong> —The Monster is an alien, an outsider. It may just be a metaphor for alienation, or literally be an extra-(or ultra)-terrestrial. Such monsters are usually obvious and inhuman, not just to look at, but in the way they think and behave. They have apparently supernatural powers and abilities, but often make do with advanced technologies (in the form of additional Descendants). They may draw and play Yonder Draw cards to and from their Monster Pool with their alien / inhuman / weird actions. Their Monster Pool number of cards Reduced adds Success Levels to any action to do with motion, survival or the alien's technologies. From little grey Eusaiveans, through to pod people and pepper-pot shaped engines of hate, all the way to face-hugging xenomorphic space-wasps, there are plenty of aliens.</td></tr></tbody><tfoot><tr><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</td><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</td></tr></tfoot></table></div><p></p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Red/Purple Carnivore</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> Hunters, Broccen, Vermis, Labyrinthine, All-Walkers</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> Black paths and Migratory</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong> Native Form — Dragon</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> These paths open and close naturally through the Lea from time to time, they are easily walked by all Bulmäs, but Vermis and All-Walkers may walk them in Swarm or Dragon configurations.</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-cardwrap-jsvg" id="cardrewritebox51" data-mode="jsvg" data-wyrd="1" data-yarn="1"><img src="https://www.cjmoseley.dev.cc/wp-content/plugins/t13ne/images/cards-svg/KS.svg" class="t13ne-svgcard"><div class="t13ne-svgpips"><div class="t13ne-textpips">13<!-- write suit--><span class="t13ne-symbol"><span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></span><details class="t13ne-suit"><summary></summary><p class="t13ne-name"><strong>Suit: </strong>Spades <span class="t13ne-blackcard">♠</span></p><p><strong>Ordeal Trump: </strong><span class="t13ne-trump">Attack</span></p><p class="t13ne-desc"><strong>Description: </strong>The Spades Suit is the suit of suffering, said to be good for the soul, but miserable for a Monday. In the tarot, the Suit is replaced with Swords, which (depending on the tradition) may represent either damaging Fire, or more commonly the cutting intellect of Air.</p><p class="t13ne-tarot"><strong>Tarot Meaning: </strong>Misfortune, Suffering, Loss, Mourning, Treachery, Betrayal, War, Lawsuit, Conflict.</p><p class="t13ne-otext"><strong>Ordeal Text: </strong>Ordeal Types: Confrontation (e.g. Warfare, Lawsuit), Perception (Spot Hidden). Spades act as Trumps for the Following Action Types: Attacking, Balance, Climbing, Contagion, Desecration, Destruction, Feats of Strength, Lock-picking, Navigation, Perception (Search/Spot), Staying power, and Stoicism. Spades can be used for any type of attack.</p><p class="t13ne-parry"><strong>Parry: </strong><strong>Redirect-Attack:</strong> The damage from the original attack (less the Parried Card) is redirected by the Defender to the Attacker, their Descendants or their Allies.</p><p class="t13ne-soak"><strong>Soak: </strong><strong>Counter-Attack:</strong> The matched Attack card is reflected back at the Attacker, or their Descendant, as Narratively appropriate. </p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-svg-facet"><div>Miasma</div></div><div class="t13ne-wound"><div><strong class="t13ne-woundname">Mortal</strong><details><summary></summary><strong>Mortal</strong><p class="t13ne-base-pips">Base Pips to summon: 16</p><p class="t13ne-min-pips">Minimum Pips Required: 12</p><p class="t13ne-desc">The Target is now dying from the wound.</p><details class="t13ne-roll"><summary>Rules</summary>Create a "Dying / Broken / Burning" quirk Hitch that slowly grows +1 Hitch Boon per Miasma Boon as Chi Limit Duration) until healed. If no Hitch can be increased by the Mortal Wound then decrease an appropriate Facet (Hitch, Incarna, Persona or Core usually) by a Boon equal to the Wounded Character’s Miasma Score. I-Ching, Personality, Proficiency slots and Annexes affected should be recalculated. If the Hitch becomes a Flaw the Wound will automatically upgrade to a Carnage Wound.</details><details class="t13ne-ordeal"><summary>Ordeal Rules</summary>Treat as though the Personality Annex or Master Annex has been Crippled, until the end of the Round. Then add a new "Dying / Broken / Burning" quirk Hitch with a Boon of Miasma Score (to a Max of Half the Incarna Boon). Or increase a quirk Hitch by 1 Boon at the end of each Round.</details><details class="t13ne-psych"><summary>Psych Wounds</summary><details class="t13ne-negative"><summary>Negative Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>As Normal but does not create a "Dying" Hitch, but instead a Scars (Mental/Emotional) / Fear / Fatigued / Lament / Taboo / Offensive / Doubt,  etc Hitch.  Plus Consciousness is usually lost until Stabilised.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Creates any normal Hitch type. At Yarn-Teller's discretion Mortal Wounds can also alter the Persona, or Core Facet choice (Ref arbitrates). Plus Consciousness is usually lost until healed.</p></details><details class="t13ne-positive"><summary>Positive Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>As Normal but creates a Scars (Mental/Emotional) / Duty / Dependance / Paranoia / Devotion / Madness / Vague, etc quirk Hitch.  Plus Consciousness is usually lost until Stabilised. May Gain a new Talent Annex / Descendant or Resolve a quirk Hitch.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>May create any Flaw Hitch. At Yarn-Teller's discretion Mortal Wounds can also alter the wounded character’s Persona, or Core Facet choices. Consciousness is usually lost until healed. Gains a new Talent Annex / Descendant or Resolves a quirk Handicap.</p></details></details></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-tarot-svg"><details><summary class="t13ne-wyrdtarot"><strong> Wyrd Tarot: </strong> Fear</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong> Normal: </strong>Cause Fear. +2 cards to Psych Attacks and +1 card to Psychology Tests the Target causes while atop the pile.</p><p class="t13ne-crossed"><strong> Crossed: </strong>Self-Control. -1 Card from Psychology test (Immunity to Fear) and -2 from Psych Attacks on the Target while atop the pile.</p></details></div><div class="t13ne-yarn-fancy">
			<details><summary><strong>Yarn</strong> <br>The Butcher</summary>
			<span class="t13ne-description">The Butcher card represents blood, death and carrion, but it also represents sustenance, food and support within a community. The hard part of reading the card is knowing when the card is a sign of disaster and when it means a mighty feast, or more importantly who that feast is for.</span>
			<span class="t13ne-significator">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-significator"><strong>Significator</strong></summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-plot"><strong>Plot: </strong> The Butcher Plot is usually set within a small community, and tends to be about survival, this may be survival against a harsh environment, like a drought or harsh winter, or more normally a killer is loose in the community. This killer is usually a serial killer and a member of the community, but it may be a Monster from outside the community in the horror genre.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-char"><strong>Character: </strong> The Butcher Significator indicates a Character who is a killer, but often for the good of the community. They Gain Yin when they provide for the hungry, poor, or in need. They Gain Yang when they care about how clean their hands are (wash, wipe, wear gloves). They Gain Chi when they execute, or prepare an attack or piece of meat. They also gain +1 Success Levels to all physical attacks.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-scene"><strong>Scene: </strong> The Butcher Plot is usually set within a small community, and tends to be about survival, this may be survival against a harsh environment, like a drought or harsh winter, or more normally a killer is loose in the community. This killer is usually a serial killer and a member of the community, but it may be a Monster from outside the community in the horror genre.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-scene">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-scene-type">
						<strong>Scene: </strong> Completion
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">How the Story Completes and Resolves. depending on how the Characters do the Plot may be destroyed, demoted, or promoted.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
						<strong>Conflict Embodiment: </strong> Rational
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Conflict embodies each side of the Conflict into Characters who are in direct Conflict.  This is the classic Heroes verses Bad Guys plot.This may follow a period of Tension, or not. Often the Rational Embodiment creates a Monster.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>

			<span class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
						<strong>Facet Embodiment: </strong> Monster
					</summary>
					<section class="t13ne-description">The Conflict Embodies one Facet as a Character Monster Facet.This Monster might be a Dragon, an Orc Horde, or just a human Villain. Monsters can be great fun, but are rarely subtle. Where possible when using Monster embodiments, the Plot may want to keep the exact nature of the Monster a secret for as long as it can.</section>
				</details>
			</span>
			 <div class="t13ne-archetypes"><details><summary><strong>Archetype Character: </strong>Butcher</summary> <details class="t13ne-Persona"><summary><strong>Persona: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Vandal</span>
      </summary>
      <details class="t13ne-motivation"><summary><strong>Motivation: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Vandals (or Trolls) like nothing more than inciting other people's anger and outrage, except perhaps the beautiful music of smashing glass and crashing noises. Vandals are driven by a serious desire to smash, destroy, degrade and insult, and enjoy watching it happen as much as doing it in most cases.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-avoid"><summary><strong>Avoid: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Apologies are the bane of the Vandal's existence. Not only are they no fun, but they effectively undo all the outrage that the Vandal has caused, and they never enjoy that.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-gain-chi"><summary><strong>Gain Chi: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description"><span>Gain Chi whenever you see or hear someone, or something being attacked, insulted, mocked, or degraded in some way, even if you are the one doing it.</span><p>Ordeal gain Chi when anyone plays any card that successfully Wounds anyone, or anything, except you and your Descendants.</p></span></details></details>   <details class="t13ne-core"><summary><strong>Core: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Barber</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">The character can move Wounds from one Character or Descendant to another with a cost equal to the Pips in Chi. This cost can be lowered by also moving a Wound the other way.</span></details><details class="t13ne-hitch"><summary><strong>Hitch: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Vague</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Maybe its their unintelligible accent, or maybe they stammer, stutter, or just won't get to the point</span></details><p class="t13ne-description">The Butcher may engage in slaughtering animals, dressing their meat, or selling the cuts. Butchers can also be killers and careless surgeons.</p></details></div>
			 <span class="t13ne-hook">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-hook">
						<strong>Hook: </strong> Danger
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Hook just consists of some enormous danger, it isn't looming, it isn't coming, it is right here, right now.</aside>
					<details class="t13ne-hook-aspect">
						<summary><strong>Hook Aspect: </strong>Hitches</summary>
						<section class="t13ne-description">The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.</section>
					</details>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-obstacle-fancy"><details class="t13ne-obstacle"><summary><strong>Obstacle:</strong>Bloody</summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way is blocked by a deadly trap and the remains of its last victim. The Trap must be Extreme Stakes and be primed with at least one Mortal or Carnage Wound. The remains will make a one, two or three card psych attack automatically depending upon how long ago the trap last fired.</p></details></span>
			<span class="t13ne-gain">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-gain">
						<strong>Gain </strong> 
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Increase Scale</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-fray">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-fray">
						<strong>Fray: </strong> Hunted
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Being Hunted (or hunting) is a High Stakes Ordeal, that may begin with a Stalk or Chase, that explodes into a Fight. A Hunt is usually a team sport, with the Hunter being superior in power level to the Prey.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-snag">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-snag">
						<strong>Snag: </strong> Big Bad
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Opposed Embodiment or at least one Force of Nature Monster (with at least 2 Monster Facets - usually either the Dominant Facet and it's Anti-Facet or the Pressed Facet) Extra engages the party during the Fray. At any time except their Story's Zenith Act, the Big Bad is protected by actual 'Plot Armour' meaning that the Plot Demon will Soak Wounds, etc. for them. Powerful Character's can still defeat them (and the Plot by extension) during the early Acts, so Ref's should handle with care, and usually use a FON Extra instead. </aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-sweeping">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-sweeping">
						<strong>Sweeping: </strong> Feast
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Sweeping grants the PCs a huge feast or festival, there may be gains, in the form of gifts as well as food, drink, music and dancing, and there will be meat.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
		 <details class="t13ne-revelation-fancy"><summary><strong>Revelation</strong></summary><details class="t13ne-about"><summary><strong>About: </strong> Dominant </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information revealed is about the Dominant Conflict Embodiment. Sometimes they are Characters, but almost as often they are Locations, Quests, Ordeals, or similar. Where this is true the Revelation should reveal details about future Quests the Pressed Characters must complete, or places they should visit. The Revelation can be little more than a call-back or foreshadowing if handled correct.</p></details><details class="t13ne-info"><summary><strong>Info: </strong> How? </summary><p class="t13ne-description">How exactly is what they plan to do, going to be done? You may get the full plan or just the important pieces. You might get How to stop it, but not what they do. It all depends on the situation at hand.</p></details><details class="t13ne-vector"><summary><strong>Vector: </strong> Subtlety </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Some sweet, subtle details, or a very tricky to decipher clue, conveys the information. It may be the things that were left unsaid in a conversation, a tone of voice or an expression used, but something gives away the information.</p></details><details class="t13ne-alternate"><summary><strong>Alternate: </strong> Designs </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Designs are sometimes detailed plans, other times they are broad sketches, depending how early in the process the designs were made. Designs can also be what the Embodiment is trying to achieve, without even a sketch of a plan.</p></details><details class="t13ne-detail"><summary><strong>Detail: </strong> Salient </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Salient details are just the most important things that anyone might need to know. "So... We are <em>all</em> going to die."</p></details></details>
