		 <details class="t13ne-ordeal-fancy"><summary><strong>Ordeal</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-ordeal-type"><strong>Ordeal-Type </strong> — Opposed Ordeal</p><p class="t13ne-stages"><strong>Stages </strong> — 8</p><p class="t13ne-ordeal-spread"><strong>Ordeal-Spread </strong> — Map-Geography</p><p class="t13ne-stakes"><strong>Stakes </strong> — Blended Extreme-Paradoxical</p><p class="t13ne-test"><strong>Test </strong> — Destruction (Miasma-Trial)</p><p class="t13ne-obstacles"><strong>Obstacles </strong> — 2 Obstacles in every Non-Court Stage, 3 in every Court Stage</p><p class="t13ne-suggested-action"><strong>Suggested-Action </strong> — Assault</p><details class="t13ne-stage"><summary><strong>Stage: </strong> Fork </summary><p class="t13ne-description">A fork in the road (two directions to choose from), perhaps a navigation way-point, a place to search, or a chance to spot dangers ahead. A decision point, where you may be choosing between two equally perilous ways to proceed.</p></details><details class="t13ne-motional"><summary><strong>Motional: </strong> Dangerous Footing Flee </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Run! No, across the cracking ice. It is right behind you!</p></details><details class="t13ne-fight"><summary><strong>Fight: </strong> King of the Monsters </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The Fight is against a huge Monster, such as a Kaiju (a Location with Monster Facets). This may be Demonic in nature too, or just extremely radioactive or something. <!-- t13ne-shortcode "%7B%22type%22%3A%22facetsuitaspect%22%2C%22suit%22%3A%224%22%2C%22aspect%22%3A%22Monster%22%7D"// --></p><div class="t13ne-tablewrap"><table class="t13ne-table Monster"><thead><tr><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</th><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Gossamer</td><td><strong>Wiry</strong> —The Monster is immensely physically powerful, although often its form may not reveal it. It often demonstrates supernatural power as well as strength and toughness. A small, strong Goblin, a robot that looks like a little girl, a cybernetically enhanced rat, or even a killer doll are all examples of a Gossamer Monster. The Wiry Monster can refill Gossamer Score cards to its Monster Pool whenever it is underestimated. A Wiry monster may add their current Monster Pool size to any Score or Pips to do with their Monstrous strength or toughness. The Monster must spend at least one card from their Monster Pool when they physically attack.</td></tr><tr><td>Inertia</td><td><strong>Unstoppable</strong> —The Monster is inexorable, it does not stop and cannot be killed by any normal means. They start play with a Monster Pool of Inertia Score cards. Unstoppable Monsters can ignore a number of Wounds equal to the number of cards in their Monster Pool. Wounds stacked beyond the Monster Pool affect them as normal. Inexorable Monsters can also ignore all Hitches with a Boon less than their Inertia Score while they have cards in their Monster Pool. Whenever an Unstoppable Monster is actually damaged it must spend cards from its Monster Pool while remaining motionless for Inertia Score as Chi Extend Duration to discard all Wounds less than Mortal in nature, and Stabilise all Mortal + Wounds (which may then be ignored if the Unstoppable has enough cards remaining in its Monster Pool). Examples of the Inertial Monster type include the Hungry Dead (Zombies) and most robotic assassins (especially ones from the future).</td></tr><tr><td>Key</td><td><strong>Hunter</strong> —The Monster is a hunter or tracker, it should favour harrying attacks and hit and run tactics. Typically such a Monster will vanish or flee if it is wounded, only to reappear when healed. The hunter may be fooled by false trails, but never for long. Hunters may add Key Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they spot a track, clue, or similar, of the subject of their hunt. Hunters receive a bonus equal to the number of cards in the Monster Pool to any tracking, attacking or hunting actions if they also spend a card from the Monster Pool on the action. Hunters are usually immune to Carnage and Mortal Wounds until the Zenith of the story (such wounds are taken as Crippling Wounds instead, but at a cost of one card from the Monster Pool). In T13, the archetypal version of this Monster is a Bulmäs Pack member from the Wild Hunt, but any Werewolf or Animal Shifter would probably qualify, as would robot cowboys in theme parks that have gone wrong, and any fantasy world's Elven ranger. </td></tr><tr><td>Miasma</td><td><strong>Mutant</strong> —The monster is a mutant or chimerical creature made from parts of others. This monster type is the oldest perhaps, you’ll find examples throughout the ancient world, particularly in Greek myth. The Mutant will often be larger, and may be malformed or unsymmetrical, with odd numbers of limbs, eyes, horns, or even heads. The Chimera will blend multiple animals together to create a new creature that combines features of all the “parent” creatures, preserving the best of all species. Mutant Monsters start with Miasma Score Monster Pool. They add Monster Pool Size as the Boon of Proficiency less "Mutation" Annex that they can use to grow larger or mimic their “parents” abilities. 1950s B-movie monsters are often of this type, giant ants, lizards, spiders, wasps, and even rabbits have been used. Classically of course, Cyclopses, Centaurs, Minotaurs, Satyrs, Gryphons (or Griffins), hippogriffs, dragons, sphinxes, manticores and of course the Chimera itself are all examples. You can also find examples in superheroes, especially those created by radioactive arachnids, or those with an X-gene.</td></tr><tr><td>Trial</td><td><strong>Killer</strong> —The Monster is a deadly Combat specialist of some sort. Killers add Trial Draw cards to their Monster Pool each time they kill someone. Killers automatically add +1 Score/Pip per card of Monster Pool to any attack, usually as Penetration or Range costs. Killers must spend Monster Pool cards whenever they will make a Trick. Killers can be drug-addled assassins, crazed shooters, or just steel-souled soldiers.</td></tr><tr><td>Yonder</td><td><strong>Alien</strong> —The Monster is an alien, an outsider. It may just be a metaphor for alienation, or literally be an extra-(or ultra)-terrestrial. Such monsters are usually obvious and inhuman, not just to look at, but in the way they think and behave. They have apparently supernatural powers and abilities, but often make do with advanced technologies (in the form of additional Descendants). They may draw and play Yonder Draw cards to and from their Monster Pool with their alien / inhuman / weird actions. Their Monster Pool number of cards Reduced adds Success Levels to any action to do with motion, survival or the alien's technologies. From little grey Eusaiveans, through to pod people and pepper-pot shaped engines of hate, all the way to face-hugging xenomorphic space-wasps, there are plenty of aliens.</td></tr></tbody><tfoot><tr><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</td><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</td></tr></tfoot></table></div><p></p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Red/Purple Carnivore</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> Hunters, Broccen, Vermis, Labyrinthine, All-Walkers</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> Labyrinth</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong> Labrys patterned Theriocephalic (Animal-Headed) Humanoids</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> Kings represent paths in the Labyrinth, and Cæster in the centre as well. A normal Bulmäs that tries to walk the Labyrinth would be lost, and would then be recruited into the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth can connect any point in space and time to any other, allowing Bulmäs to consciously time-travel through the Lea.  A King can allow a Bulmäs to create or reload a Chronolith or side-step depending on the power-level of the Bulmäs.</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-cardwrap-jsvg" id="cardrewritebox52" data-mode="jsvg" data-wyrd="1" data-yarn="1"><img src="https://www.cjmoseley.dev.cc/wp-content/plugins/t13ne/images/cards-svg/Joker1.svg" class="t13ne-svgcard"><div class="t13ne-svgpips"><div class="t13ne-textpips">15<!-- write suit--><span class="t13ne-symbol"><span class="t13ne-blackcard">✱</span></span><details class="t13ne-suit"><summary></summary><p class="t13ne-name"><strong>Suit: </strong>Wildcard <span class="t13ne-blackcard">✱</span></p><p><strong>Ordeal Trump: </strong><span class="t13ne-trump">Anything</span></p><p class="t13ne-desc"><strong>Description: </strong>Wild cards can pretend to be any Suit.</p><p class="t13ne-tarot"><strong>Tarot Meaning: </strong>Wild, chaotic, dreamlike or insane</p><p class="t13ne-otext"><strong>Ordeal Text: </strong>Ordeal Types: Wild Ordeals should either take place far from civilization or should be beyond normal in scope or scale somehow.</p><p class="t13ne-parry"><strong>Parry: </strong><strong>Lucky Parry:</strong> The Defender may discard all cards from the Attack, but both weapons take the Parry card as damage.</p><p class="t13ne-soak"><strong>Soak: </strong><strong>Wild:</strong> Wild Soak discards the entire attack and the Soak/Block cards, except for one randomly selected card that gains Penetration of +15.</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-svg-facet"><div>All</div></div><div class="t13ne-wound"><div><strong class="t13ne-woundname">Carnage</strong><details><summary></summary><strong>Carnage</strong><p class="t13ne-base-pips">Base Pips to summon: 21</p><p class="t13ne-min-pips">Minimum Pips Required: 14</p><p class="t13ne-desc">The Target should be dead, all exploded or turned to stone, or something. Sometimes though, they keep on going... for a while. And just occasionally, they get better.</p><details class="t13ne-roll"><summary>Rules</summary>Create a "Dead/Destroyed" Flaw Hitch, or increase the Boon of any Flaw Hitch by 1 Boon per Miasma Boon treated as Yarn Limit Duration outside of Ordeals) until healed. May also alter the target’s Incarna Facet choice (turning to stone, etc). Alternatively Decrease Character Scale by 1 or remove a Character's access to the Wounded Alternate.</details><details class="t13ne-ordeal"><summary>Ordeal Rules</summary>Immediately Treat Personality / Master Descendant as Wounded. At the end of the Round either create a new "Dead / Destroyed" Flaw Hitch (Half the Incarna Boon + Miasma Score) or increase a Flaw Hitch by Miasma Score. Alternatively Decrease Scale by 1, or alter Incarna / Persona / Core choices. If the Hitch Boon rises above Incarna Boon the Wound increases to Carnage+</details><details class="t13ne-psych"><summary>Psych Wounds</summary><details class="t13ne-negative"><summary>Negative Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Can create a Flaw Hitch of any Type, as the Ref decides. Consciousness is always lost until the wound is Stabilised. Note may remove a Character's access to the Wounded Alternate.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Creates Maximised Flaw Hitches of any type but favours... Fear, Madness, Vague (Amnesia), Paranoia, Loss of Sense (e.g. Hysterical Blindness) or Taboo Hitches. Consciousness is always lost until Healed and may remove a Character's access to the Wounded Alternate. Can create a new Split Alternate.</p></details><details class="t13ne-positive"><summary>Positive Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>May Gain a new Power Annex / Descendant. Can Create a Flaw Hitch of any type, Consciousness is always lost until Stabilised, and access to the Alternate may be lost.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong> May create or Resolve Flaw Hitches of any type but favours... Duty, Dependance, Madness, Vague (Amnesia), Paranoia, Loss of Sense (e.g. Hysterical Blindness) or Taboo Hitches. Consciousness is always lost until Healed. Either creates a new Alternate, Intergrates an Alternate one Step toward Coalesce, or Gain a new Power Annex / Descendant.</p></details></details></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-tarot-svg"><details><summary class="t13ne-wyrdtarot"><strong> Wyrd Tarot: </strong> Wildcard</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong> Normal: </strong>May be played as any Non-Court card (or as an Ordeal Card Joker with +22 Pips of Liberty Penetration)</p><p class="t13ne-crossed"><strong> Crossed: </strong>May be played as any Non-Court card</p></details></div><div class="t13ne-yarn-fancy">
			<details><summary><strong>Yarn</strong> <br>The World</summary>
			<span class="t13ne-description">The World card represents the whole world, not just the Earth, or any particular planet, or dimension but the Omniverse as a whole. In fact, any wholeness, or completion is indicated by the card, as well as the wilderness and wild places in particular (due to the wild-card nature of the Joker). The World is generally seen as a positive card, but it can be negative in the sense of endings mean farewells, good byes and moving on from one phase to another.</span>
			<span class="t13ne-significator">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-significator"><strong>Significator</strong></summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-plot"><strong>Plot: </strong> The World Plot is always a tale that spans multiple Locations, often actually across multiple worlds or time periods. The World Plot is generally at least an Epic or Cycle, and can be useful for world-building, if you have no better ideas. World Plots consist of lots of apparently unconnected sub-Plots that examine often random Conflicts (that are still aspects of the World's Conflict.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-char"><strong>Character: </strong> The World Significator indicates a Character who are very experienced and confident. They Gain Yin when they act confident, arrogant, or nonchalant. They Gain Yang when they can share their knowledge of a situation or thing. They Gain Chi when they say farewell, good bye, or wish someone well. They also gain +1 Success Level to all actions that they claim they have tried before.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-scene"><strong>Scene: </strong> The World Plot is always a tale that spans multiple Locations, often actually across multiple worlds or time periods. The World Plot is generally at least an Epic or Cycle, and can be useful for world-building, if you have no better ideas. World Plots consist of lots of apparently unconnected sub-Plots that examine often random Conflicts (that are still aspects of the World's Conflict.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-scene">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-scene-type">
						<strong>Scene: </strong> Weft
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Wefts work the Conflict through 3 Parts: <ul><li>First by the Characters Recoiling from the Snag (They emotionally respond to the previous event) which does not require a Yarn card</li><li>Secondly Sweeping (where the Characters recover from what happened) which does require another Yarn Card</li><li>Finally, Picking (Where the Characters will have a choice how the Story will proceed) which does not require Yarn Cards, but can be represented by more cards during card play.</li></ul>During Wefts the Pressed side makes a little headway, or gets a little breather.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
						<strong>Conflict Embodiment: </strong> External
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">One side of the Conflict (usually the Pressed side) is embodied within the Characters (sometimes an NPC), the Dominant side is embodied as something external to all the Characters. Embodiments might include Tones, Locations, Descendants, Quests, Ordeals/Tests and suitable Obstacles.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>

			<span class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
						<strong>Facet Embodiment: </strong> Quest
					</summary>
					<section class="t13ne-description">The Conflict Embodies one Facet as a Quest that the Character must attempt. This can be great for creating Chapters of Epics and is an obvious way of deciding NPC Ends, but be wary of using Quests in a substitute for Character development. Great fun, but best used sparingly.</section>
				</details>
			</span>
			 <div class="t13ne-archetypes"><details><summary><strong>Archetype Character: </strong>Dragon</summary> <details class="t13ne-Persona"><summary><strong>Persona: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Reaper</span>
      </summary>
      <details class="t13ne-motivation"><summary><strong>Motivation: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Reapers are materialists, they may be collectors, trying to complete their collection, or just want more than anyone else. Fundamentally their motivation is one of greed.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-avoid"><summary><strong>Avoid: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Reapers do not like to give away anything, without receiving something in compensation, or acknowledging the debt.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-gain-chi"><summary><strong>Gain Chi: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description"><span>Gain Chi when you make, take, purchase or accept something, or ask for more.</span><p>Ordeal gain Chi when you draw a ♣.</p></span></details></details>   <details class="t13ne-core"><summary><strong>Core: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Traveller</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">The Character has an additional Annex (1-11 Skill, 12-21 Talent, 22-26 Power) that grants an extra mode of Travel. E.g. Fleet-footed Skill, Flying Talent or a Lea Walking Power.</span></details><details class="t13ne-hitch"><summary><strong>Hitch: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Fear</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">Either suffering from (as on a phobia), or causing Fear</span></details><p class="t13ne-description">Dragons are huge, treasure hoarding monsters... Wait, you know what a Dragon is. In T13 dragons are associated with Vermis Bulmäs in most universes, fantasy universes will have native non Vermis Dragons..</p></details></div>
			 <span class="t13ne-hook">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-hook">
						<strong>Hook: </strong> Wildcard
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Hook is just one of those out there crazy starts to a story. Perhaps they come around on a beach near a crashed plane, or wrecked boat, with no memories of where they were going or why. Or maybe they explode from the ground, unsure how or why they were there.</aside>
					<details class="t13ne-hook-aspect">
						<summary><strong>Hook Aspect: </strong>Hitches</summary>
						<section class="t13ne-description">The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.</section>
					</details>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-obstacle-fancy"><details class="t13ne-obstacle"><summary><strong>Obstacle:</strong>Destructive</summary><p class="t13ne-description">A Destructive Trap is a one shot trap, it might demolish the building, drop all the floors, flood the valley the building is in, destabilize the volcano, or unleash giant rolling balls to chase intruders out and block the exits. Once triggered there is no stopping or undoing the devastation and carnage that will follow. Luckily such traps usually have a number of Phases that they take to complete, and you usually get at least one Phase of hearing the thing fire up as a warning.</p></details></span>
			<span class="t13ne-gain">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-gain">
						<strong>Gain </strong> 
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Add Alternate</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-fray">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-fray">
						<strong>Fray: </strong> Wilderness Encounter
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">A holdover from Old School Gaming. The encounter is a where the PCs meet a larger group of inferior foes (for example 2d20 Vexes, or 2d6 Cast Extras) in the Wild. Encounters are not always balanced, and the PCs may have Flee turning the High Stakes Ordeal into a Chase. Encounters are usually followed by a Gain.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-snag">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-snag">
						<strong>Snag: </strong> Double Trouble
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Add an additional Fray that must be accomplished alongside the first.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-sweeping">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-sweeping">
						<strong>Sweeping: </strong> Exologue
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Sweeping is an Exologue, where the Character spends a Short Act of a few Scenes in the world of one of their Alternates. This can be used to allow the Pressed Embodiment recovery time in a different time-line (another universe, a past life, etc).</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
		 <details class="t13ne-revelation-fancy"><summary><strong>Revelation</strong></summary><details class="t13ne-about"><summary><strong>About: </strong> Hidden Influences </summary><p class="t13ne-description">There are Hidden influences in any universe. Sometimes they are the Increated working behind the scenes, other times they are the manipulative Faeries, ensuring the Doom of Wyrd, still other groups include time-travellers and inter-dimensional visitors who have all their own agendas. This Revelation reveals something about one of these groups.</p></details><details class="t13ne-info"><summary><strong>Info: </strong> Ref Special </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The Yarn cards can't decide everything that is happening, this one is all down to the Ref to decide.</p></details><details class="t13ne-vector"><summary><strong>Vector: </strong> Mystical </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information arrives in a mystical or via magickal means. This might be the result of scrying or a message spell.</p></details><details class="t13ne-alternate"><summary><strong>Alternate: </strong> Quest </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The Revelation is actually a Quest that must be followed to find out the important information.</p></details><details class="t13ne-detail"><summary><strong>Detail: </strong> Wild </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Wild details might be details about how wild the situation or a Character is or may just be wild card details, randomly providing truths and lies across the board.</p></details></details>
		 <details class="t13ne-ordeal-fancy"><summary><strong>Ordeal</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-ordeal-type"><strong>Ordeal-Type </strong> — Opposed Ordeal</p><p class="t13ne-stages"><strong>Stages </strong> — 12</p><p class="t13ne-ordeal-spread"><strong>Ordeal-Spread </strong> — Map-Geography</p><p class="t13ne-stakes"><strong>Stakes </strong> — Blended Low-Soul</p><p class="t13ne-test"><strong>Test </strong> — Luck (Liberty-Wyrd)</p><p class="t13ne-obstacles"><strong>Obstacles </strong> — 3 Obstacles per Non-Court Stage, 4 per Court Stage</p><p class="t13ne-suggested-action"><strong>Suggested-Action </strong> — Anything</p><details class="t13ne-stage"><summary><strong>Stage: </strong> Maelstrom </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way ahead is chaos-ridden. Perhaps bullets strafe the landscape, or shells explode across No-man's land, or maybe the waters swirl in huge whirlpool. Then again perhaps it is just a swirl of litter and flashing lights that blocks the way.</p></details><details class="t13ne-motional"><summary><strong>Motional: </strong> Circuit Race </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Race around the Stages of the Ordeal repeatedly. Often a vehicle race, but it can be any form as required.</p></details><details class="t13ne-fight"><summary><strong>Fight: </strong> Insult Tennis </summary><p class="t13ne-description">For whatever reason during the combat actual damage is unlikely, perhaps the combatants are Mech-pilots, or these are two immortals crossing swords. The real contest is in the barbs, insults and quips that they make. The right insult, in this context, can be deadly.</p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Rainbow</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> All</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> Black paths and Migratory</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong> Native Form — Dragon</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> These paths open and close naturally through the Lea from time to time, they are easily walked by all Bulmäs, but Vermis and All-Walkers may walk them in Swarm or Dragon configurations.</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-cardwrap-jsvg" id="cardrewritebox53" data-mode="jsvg" data-wyrd="1" data-yarn="1"><img src="https://www.cjmoseley.dev.cc/wp-content/plugins/t13ne/images/cards-svg/Joker2.svg" class="t13ne-svgcard"><div class="t13ne-svgpips"><div class="t13ne-textpips">15<!-- write suit--><span class="t13ne-symbol"><span class="t13ne-blackcard">✱</span></span><details class="t13ne-suit"><summary></summary><p class="t13ne-name"><strong>Suit: </strong>Wildcard <span class="t13ne-blackcard">✱</span></p><p><strong>Ordeal Trump: </strong><span class="t13ne-trump">Anything</span></p><p class="t13ne-desc"><strong>Description: </strong>Wild cards can pretend to be any Suit.</p><p class="t13ne-tarot"><strong>Tarot Meaning: </strong>Wild, chaotic, dreamlike or insane</p><p class="t13ne-otext"><strong>Ordeal Text: </strong>Ordeal Types: Wild Ordeals should either take place far from civilization or should be beyond normal in scope or scale somehow.</p><p class="t13ne-parry"><strong>Parry: </strong><strong>Lucky Parry:</strong> The Defender may discard all cards from the Attack, but both weapons take the Parry card as damage.</p><p class="t13ne-soak"><strong>Soak: </strong><strong>Wild:</strong> Wild Soak discards the entire attack and the Soak/Block cards, except for one randomly selected card that gains Penetration of +15.</p></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-svg-facet"><div>All</div></div><div class="t13ne-wound"><div><strong class="t13ne-woundname">Carnage</strong><details><summary></summary><strong>Carnage</strong><p class="t13ne-base-pips">Base Pips to summon: 21</p><p class="t13ne-min-pips">Minimum Pips Required: 14</p><p class="t13ne-desc">The Target should be dead, all exploded or turned to stone, or something. Sometimes though, they keep on going... for a while. And just occasionally, they get better.</p><details class="t13ne-roll"><summary>Rules</summary>Create a "Dead/Destroyed" Flaw Hitch, or increase the Boon of any Flaw Hitch by 1 Boon per Miasma Boon treated as Yarn Limit Duration outside of Ordeals) until healed. May also alter the target’s Incarna Facet choice (turning to stone, etc). Alternatively Decrease Character Scale by 1 or remove a Character's access to the Wounded Alternate.</details><details class="t13ne-ordeal"><summary>Ordeal Rules</summary>Immediately Treat Personality / Master Descendant as Wounded. At the end of the Round either create a new "Dead / Destroyed" Flaw Hitch (Half the Incarna Boon + Miasma Score) or increase a Flaw Hitch by Miasma Score. Alternatively Decrease Scale by 1, or alter Incarna / Persona / Core choices. If the Hitch Boon rises above Incarna Boon the Wound increases to Carnage+</details><details class="t13ne-psych"><summary>Psych Wounds</summary><details class="t13ne-negative"><summary>Negative Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>Can create a Flaw Hitch of any Type, as the Ref decides. Consciousness is always lost until the wound is Stabilised. Note may remove a Character's access to the Wounded Alternate.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong>Creates Maximised Flaw Hitches of any type but favours... Fear, Madness, Vague (Amnesia), Paranoia, Loss of Sense (e.g. Hysterical Blindness) or Taboo Hitches. Consciousness is always lost until Healed and may remove a Character's access to the Wounded Alternate. Can create a new Split Alternate.</p></details><details class="t13ne-positive"><summary>Positive Psych</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong>Normal</strong>May Gain a new Power Annex / Descendant. Can Create a Flaw Hitch of any type, Consciousness is always lost until Stabilised, and access to the Alternate may be lost.</p><p class="t13ne-extreme"><strong>Extreme</strong> May create or Resolve Flaw Hitches of any type but favours... Duty, Dependance, Madness, Vague (Amnesia), Paranoia, Loss of Sense (e.g. Hysterical Blindness) or Taboo Hitches. Consciousness is always lost until Healed. Either creates a new Alternate, Intergrates an Alternate one Step toward Coalesce, or Gain a new Power Annex / Descendant.</p></details></details></details></div></div><div class="t13ne-tarot-svg"><details><summary class="t13ne-wyrdtarot"><strong> Wyrd Tarot: </strong> Wildcard</summary><p class="t13ne-normal"><strong> Normal: </strong>May be played as any Non-Court card (or as an Ordeal Card Joker with +22 Pips of Wyrd Penetration)</p><p class="t13ne-crossed"><strong> Crossed: </strong>May be played as any Non-Court card</p></details></div><div class="t13ne-yarn-fancy">
			<details><summary><strong>Yarn</strong> <br>The Beyond</summary>
			<span class="t13ne-description">The Beyond card represents that which is outside. In many ways it is the opposite of the World, as that card implies a wholeness and completeness, the Beyond tells us that even the Omniverse has its impossibilities. The Beyond can directly represent unknown factors, or the factors from beyond creation that are called the Increated. The Beyond can also represent the Supernatural, as anything beyond nature is within the bounds of the Beyond.</span>
			<span class="t13ne-significator">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-significator"><strong>Significator</strong></summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-plot"><strong>Plot: </strong> The Beyond Plot is often a tale of horror (usually cosmic), as some force beyond normal is unleashed upon the world. Demons, Undead and other tools of the Increated are common to Beyond Plots. Technically any unknown can be a part of the Beyond, extraterrestrials, ghosts, spirits, a stranger from out of town, they can all be part of a Beyond tale. The only important factor is that it is Beyond normal in some way.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-char"><strong>Character: </strong> The Beyond Significator indicates a Character who is an outsider, alien or weirdo. They Gain Yin when they speak or act strangely, or ask odd questions. They Gain Yang when they answer a question, especially if it wasn't directed at them, or the answer is weird. They Gain Chi when they act as if something common or normal is unusual or funny, or they act as though they have forgotten (or never knew the meaning of) a common word. They also gain +1 Success Level to any strange, weird or "impossible" action.</aside>
					<aside class="t13ne-scene"><strong>Scene: </strong> The Beyond Plot is often a tale of horror (usually cosmic), as some force beyond normal is unleashed upon the world. Demons, Undead and other tools of the Increated are common to Beyond Plots. Technically any unknown can be a part of the Beyond, extraterrestrials, ghosts, spirits, a stranger from out of town, they can all be part of a Beyond tale. The only important factor is that it is Beyond normal in some way.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-scene">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-scene-type">
						<strong>Scene: </strong> Warp
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Warps work the Conflict. They are broken into 3 parts (and require another 2 cards): <ul><li>The Ends (What the Players want, usually described as Quests ). Does not require a Yarn Card.</li><li>The Fray (what keeps the Characters from their Ends, how do they work through that Quest?). The Fray requires an extra Yarn Card.</li><li>The Snag (The complication that renders the actions of the Characters winning through the Fray moot somehow). The Snag also requires another Yarn Card.</li></ul>During Warps things tend to get worse for the Pressed side of the Conflict.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-conflict-embodiment">
						<strong>Conflict Embodiment: </strong> External
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">One side of the Conflict (usually the Pressed side) is embodied within the Characters (sometimes an NPC), the Dominant side is embodied as something external to all the Characters. Embodiments might include Tones, Locations, Descendants, Quests, Ordeals/Tests and suitable Obstacles.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>

			<span class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-facet-embodiment">
						<strong>Facet Embodiment: </strong> Lore
					</summary>
					<section class="t13ne-description">The Conflict embodies as a special type of Descendant called a Lore. Lores are the rumours the Players hear about the enemy and themselves as the story goes on. Lores might grant extra Descendants (including Pacts and Locations) to any Character, as it is revealed that they are a powerful wizard, a Necromancer, with a skeleton horde, armed with magickal longsword, etc.</section>
				</details>
			</span>
			 <div class="t13ne-archetypes"><details><summary><strong>Archetype Character: </strong>Undead</summary> <details class="t13ne-Persona"><summary><strong>Persona: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Hermit</span>
      </summary>
      <details class="t13ne-motivation"><summary><strong>Motivation: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">The Hermit is motivated by either a sense of spiritual purity, or an asocial nature. They do not feel they gain anything by conversation, and may believe they lose something when they interact with others.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-avoid"><summary><strong>Avoid: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description">The Hermit will avoid crowds and conversations where possible, some will even takes vows of silence or holy orders in order to separate themselves from society.</span></details>
       <details class="t13ne-gain-chi"><summary><strong>Gain Chi: </strong></summary><span class="t13ne-description"><span>Gain Chi when you act silently, selfishly or alone. </span><p>Ordeal gain Chi when you draw a ♦ </p></span></details></details>   <details class="t13ne-core"><summary><strong>Core: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Evader</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">The character is immune to one attack type, one Facet's Attack, or one level of Wound  E.g. all Crippling Wounds, all Hack wounds, or all Normal Attacks.</span></details><details class="t13ne-hitch"><summary><strong>Hitch: </strong><span class="t13ne-output">Lament</span></summary><span class="t13ne-description">When something has been lost, or forgotten, or you never had what everyone else has. It is the Hitch of Orphans and Widows, of Lost Loves and Haunting Memories</span></details><p class="t13ne-description">Undead come in lots of different types, but you'd be surprised how many can be modelled with this Archetype.</p></details></div>
			 <span class="t13ne-hook">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-hook">
						<strong>Hook: </strong> Wildcard
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Hook is just one of those out there crazy starts to a story. Perhaps they come around on a beach near a crashed plane, or wrecked boat, with no memories of where they were going or why.</aside>
					<details class="t13ne-hook-aspect">
						<summary><strong>Hook Aspect: </strong>Hitches</summary>
						<section class="t13ne-description">The Hook targets the Character's Hitches, the bigger the better.</section>
					</details>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-obstacle-fancy"><details class="t13ne-obstacle"><summary><strong>Obstacle:</strong>Incursion</summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way is blocked by some direct expression of the Beyond, it could be a Hellmouth, a portal into U-space, a gate to Shades, or a tear in space-time, or simply a dark spot full of Neechies (the least of the Increated), looking for inspiration and nightmare fuel to become perhaps even a new Patron.</p></details></span>
			<span class="t13ne-gain">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-gain">
						<strong>Gain </strong> 
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">Add Alternate</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-fray">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-fray">
						<strong>Fray: </strong> Wandering Monster
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">A holdover from Old School Gaming. The encounter is against a random Monster (Force of Nature Extra or similar powerful individual) or a group of similar powered Characters (Cast Extras) or a large group (2 to 1 outnumbering) Chorus (Extras) or a tide of Vex Extras.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-snag">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-snag">
						<strong>Snag: </strong> Triple Threat
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">A Triple Threat Draws and Plays an additional 2 Cards one as a Second Fray Ordeal and one as a Snag </aside>
				</details>
			</span>
			<span class="t13ne-sweeping">
				<details>
					<summary class="t13ne-sweeping">
						<strong>Sweeping: </strong> Interlogue
					</summary>
					<aside class="t13ne-description">The Sweeping is an Interlogue, where we cut away from the Pressed Embodiment to find out what is happening to some other Characters usually in a different Plot, but it can be a Scene or Two for the Dominant Embodiment.</aside>
				</details>
			</span>
		 <details class="t13ne-revelation-fancy"><summary><strong>Revelation</strong></summary><details class="t13ne-about"><summary><strong>About: </strong> Magical / Divine Influences </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Gods and magicians walk amongst us, at least in the Omniverse they do. The gods have great power, but are often limited in how they can apply it, magicians are not so restricted, but don't have as much power. Anyway this card allows you to reveal something about how these mages and gods are reacting to events in the Story.</p></details><details class="t13ne-info"><summary><strong>Info: </strong> Player Decides </summary><p class="t13ne-description">So there's something the Players would really want to know (like who is out to kill them, and why, and what they need to do about it, but then the Player's choose which one to know.</p></details><details class="t13ne-vector"><summary><strong>Vector: </strong> Direct </summary><p class="t13ne-description">Direct Revelations are where the Villain monologues his plan to the captured hero, demonstrates his super-weapon to the United Nations, or shape-shifts in front of the Characters for the first time. Confessions, suicide notes, scientific papers, mission logs and journal and press conferences can all be Direct Revelations too. I'm sure you can think of others.</p></details><details class="t13ne-alternate"><summary><strong>Alternate: </strong> Story </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The information contains the entire story, within the story (or play within the play), if anyone can see.</p></details><details class="t13ne-detail"><summary><strong>Detail: </strong> Haphazard </summary><p class="t13ne-description">A random collection of lies and truths that weirdly seem to tie into each other.</p></details></details>
		 <details class="t13ne-ordeal-fancy"><summary><strong>Ordeal</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-ordeal-type"><strong>Ordeal-Type </strong> — Opposed Ordeal</p><p class="t13ne-stages"><strong>Stages </strong> — 12</p><p class="t13ne-ordeal-spread"><strong>Ordeal-Spread </strong> — Map-Geography</p><p class="t13ne-stakes"><strong>Stakes </strong> — Blended Low-Paradoxical</p><p class="t13ne-test"><strong>Test </strong> — Luck (Liberty-Wyrd)</p><p class="t13ne-obstacles"><strong>Obstacles </strong> — None</p><p class="t13ne-suggested-action"><strong>Suggested-Action </strong> — None</p><details class="t13ne-stage"><summary><strong>Stage: </strong> Dead Space </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The way ahead is a void, or huge open space. Maybe there's no cover, maybe not even air. Then again it could just be the space behind the walls, secret passages, or simply a way most people would never go.</p></details><details class="t13ne-motional"><summary><strong>Motional: </strong> Circuit Chase </summary><p class="t13ne-description">A Chase (or Flee) that goes around through the same Stages again. </p></details><details class="t13ne-fight"><summary><strong>Fight: </strong> Wave after Wave </summary><p class="t13ne-description">The fight consists of waves of combatants. Each Round a new Wave begins, a Round ends automatically if all Combatants on one side are incapacitated. Typically each wave is of a different type, often beginning with Vex Monsters, rising in type and adding another Monster Facet, or doubling in number each Wave. <!-- t13ne-shortcode "%7B%22type%22%3A%22facetsuitaspect%22%2C%22suit%22%3A%22all%22%2C%22aspect%22%3A%22Monster%22%7D"// --></p><div class="t13ne-tablewrap"><table class="t13ne-table Monster"><thead><tr><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</th><th>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Awe</td><td><strong>Terror</strong> —The Monster is a Fear or Anxiety embodied. They are quite diverse in form, but are always able to cause Psychology Test attacks such as Fear, Terror, or Horror to any that see them act. They may add Awe Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they cause negative emotions in another Character. They automatically play Monster Pool Reduced Cards as a Psychology Test or Psyche Attack on any intimidation or physical attack, but must add at least one Card from their Monster Pool to any Psyche Attack. Examples of this Monster include Ghosts, some serial killers and some Preternatural Cosmic Horrors.</td></tr><tr><td>Burden</td><td><strong>Object</strong> —The Monster is an object imbued with the semblance of life. Artificial in nature usually, the living object is usually tough and strong. An Object Monster is created with a Monster Pool equal to their Burden Score. Objects are able to multiply their Scores by 1+Monster Pool Size when appropriate to their form and function. The Object Monster may instantly Prepare cards from its Monster Pool, often as a Prepared Defence or Stunt Preparation, as appropriate for the Object's form or function.</td></tr><tr><td>Craft</td><td><strong>Maker</strong> —The Monster is actually a creator of other Monsters. The spawn are often less powerful than the creator, but in some cases are unique and more powerful. The Creator may not be directly encountered until late in the Story. The Creator can draw Craft Draw cards and add them to its Monster Pool each time it creates or summons an Extra or Descendant. At least 1 card must be used from the Monster Pool each Summoning or Creation to help pay the Summoning costs (or as an Obstacle or NPC as Yarn-Telling). All currently summoned Extras and Descendants receive a bonus to their Annex Score equal to the Creator's Monster Pool size.</td></tr><tr><td>Dominion</td><td><strong>Horde</strong> —The Monster is a group or swarm of lesser monsters, it might be an Army, a pack of Bulmäs, a Fortress or a Battle Moon. Horde is effectively a Pact Descendant. A Horde may pool Values to create larger rolls, however they may never create a Boon greater than their Value. Hordes may add Dominion Draw cards to their Monster Pool when the Horde increases in numbers. The Horde must Play at least one card from their Monster Pool on any action that splits the Horde (like losing an individual). E.g. two Vex Extras of Boon 10 (Value 45) that form a Horde become a single Extra of Boon 16 (Value 90), (with probably 1 card in the Monster Pool) but could never create a Boon greater than the individual's Value(45). If there were 11 Vexes, that would be Boon 44 (Value 495). 12 or One million Vexes would both give Boon 45 (Value 530) since that is the most powerful their Horde can get.</td></tr><tr><td>Enigma</td><td><strong>Skulker</strong> —The Monster is a shadowy figure pulling strings behind the scenes. This may just be an ordinary villain, but their ability to pass unnoticed should be bordering upon the unnatural. Perhaps they use psychic powers or magic to cloud the minds of witnesses, perhaps they are a hacking whiz, able to bounce phone calls through international relays with some multinational or another picking up the tab without realizing, perhaps they are one of the Huldra, the hidden people - who may be the Fæ or maybe they are actually the imaginary friend of one of the characters now made real(ish) by an alien or a Dæmon. Skulkers start with a Monster Pool of Enigma Score. They may add Monster Pool Size Reduced Success Levels to any stealthy or hidden actions or preparations. Skulkers may play Monster Pool cards as Preparation for anothers actions. E.g. The Hacker can prepare a Hack attack, that is performed by a Church Website in another town. A mind-bending villain could prepare a Psyche Defence in the mind of his puppet.</td></tr><tr><td>Fury</td><td><strong>Temperamental</strong> —The Monster is the embodiment of an Emotion, usually a negative one. Such Monsters are often capable of supernatural actions, such as telepathy, shape-shifting, or even undying. The Vampire is an embodiment of Lust, with powers of mind control, telepathy and suggestion, as well as shape-shifting abilities. The Werewolf is berserk rage, and almost indestructible unless Silver is used. The Beansidhe is the embodiment of mourning, and able to sense an oncoming death. When they experience their negative emotion the Monster may refill their Monster Pool to Fury Score cards. When appropriate to their Emotion they must add a Monster Pool card to the action and multiply the total Score by their remaining Monster Pool Reduced.</td></tr><tr><td>Gossamer</td><td><strong>Wiry</strong> —The Monster is immensely physically powerful, although often its form may not reveal it. It often demonstrates supernatural power as well as strength and toughness. A small, strong Goblin, a robot that looks like a little girl, a cybernetically enhanced rat, or even a killer doll are all examples of a Gossamer Monster. The Wiry Monster can refill Gossamer Score cards to its Monster Pool whenever it is underestimated. A Wiry monster may add their current Monster Pool size to any Score or Pips to do with their Monstrous strength or toughness. The Monster must spend at least one card from their Monster Pool when they physically attack.</td></tr><tr><td>Heresy</td><td><strong>Masker</strong> —The Monster is looks entirely like something else. Perhaps they are a Demon in the form of an innocent child, or maybe they are a shape-shifter or face-dancer, able to change their appearance as they like, then again perhaps they're just the old Janitor in a monster costume. The Masker may create a Heresy Score sized Monster Pool whenever they hide completely for Heresy Boon as Chi Extend Duration, or are believed in their lies. The Monster Pool adds its Size Reduced to any Score to do with hiding, maintaining the mask, or lying. The Masker must play at least one card from their Monster Pool any time they wish to change their Mask or appearance.</td></tr><tr><td>Inertia</td><td><strong>Unstoppable</strong> —The Monster is inexorable, it does not stop and cannot be killed by any normal means. They start play with a Monster Pool of Inertia Score cards. Unstoppable Monsters can ignore a number of Wounds equal to the number of cards in their Monster Pool. Wounds stacked beyond the Monster Pool affect them as normal. Inexorable Monsters can also ignore all Hitches with a Boon less than their Inertia Score while they have cards in their Monster Pool. Whenever an Unstoppable Monster is actually damaged it must spend cards from its Monster Pool while remaining motionless for Inertia Score as Chi Extend Duration to discard all Wounds less than Mortal in nature, and Stabilise all Mortal + Wounds (which may then be ignored if the Unstoppable has enough cards remaining in its Monster Pool). Examples of the Inertial Monster type include the Hungry Dead (Zombies) and most robotic assassins (especially ones from the future).</td></tr><tr><td>Jeer</td><td><strong>Mockery</strong> —The monster is a mockery — It might be illusionary, or Undead (usually a corpse animated by a low-level Increated), but it may be a ghost, a machine or even an animal that mocks something else. The Mockery is created with a Monster Pool of Jeer Score cards, that must be played to maintain the Mockery (when disbelieved, attacked, etc). While the Monster Pool exists the Monster can pull and play Monster Pool Size Reduced cards (initially Jeer Draw)for any action the Mockery form takes that it has no suitable Annexes for. E.g. A Skeleton Mockery might have a "Mortality Reminder" Annex, but is given a Monster Pool of 5, so it can initially pull and play 2 cards for its mocking actions (like moving around and clawing the living). However, once attacked (or disbelieved) the Skeleton must spend at least one card from the Mockery Pool, thereafter it can only draw and Play 1 card for any Action that doesn't involve reminding someone of their own Mortality.</td></tr><tr><td>Key</td><td><strong>Hunter</strong> —The Monster is a hunter or tracker, it should favour harrying attacks and hit and run tactics. Typically such a Monster will vanish or flee if it is wounded, only to reappear when healed. The hunter may be fooled by false trails, but never for long. Hunters may add Key Draw cards to their Monster Pool whenever they spot a track, clue, or similar, of the subject of their hunt. Hunters receive a bonus equal to the number of cards in the Monster Pool to any tracking, attacking or hunting actions if they also spend a card from the Monster Pool on the action. Hunters are usually immune to Carnage and Mortal Wounds until the Zenith of the story (such wounds are taken as Crippling Wounds instead, but at a cost of one card from the Monster Pool). In T13, the archetypal version of this Monster is a Bulmäs Pack member from the Wild Hunt, but any Werewolf or Animal Shifter would probably qualify, as would robot cowboys in theme parks that have gone wrong, and any fantasy world's Elven ranger. </td></tr><tr><td>Liberty</td><td><strong>Nightmare</strong> —The Monster is nightmarish, or dreamlike. Such Monsters either exist in a purely psychological realm, appearing as dreams and hallucinations, that cannot really be fought and don’t physically exist, or they are extremely dangerous and powerful Monsters that really exist and may be magical or supernatural in power. Nightmares may add Liberty Draw cards to their Monster Pool if they cross a threshold, liminal space or are dreamed about. While they have cards in their Monster Pool they may add Liberty Boon Dice to everything they do. The Nightmare must spend at least one card from their Monster Pool when they do something impossible. They might use it to fly through the air, instantly teleport, let bullets pass through themselves, or appear to be a real thing and not a dream. Flower-faced Humanoids and Canines from a nightmare realm, handsome soap-sellers with an odd idea for gentlemen’s clubs, facially-burned, gardening-gloved janitors turned serial killers, six foot tall invisible rabbits, night hags, shadow beings seen when falling asleep, and any character in a dream, are all good examples of the Liberty Monster.</td></tr><tr><td>Miasma</td><td><strong>Mutant</strong> —The monster is a mutant or chimerical creature made from parts of others. This monster type is the oldest perhaps, you’ll find examples throughout the ancient world, particularly in Greek myth. The Mutant will often be larger, and may be malformed or unsymmetrical, with odd numbers of limbs, eyes, horns, or even heads. The Chimera will blend multiple animals together to create a new creature that combines features of all the “parent” creatures, preserving the best of all species. Mutant Monsters start with Miasma Score Monster Pool. They add Monster Pool Size as the Boon of Proficiency less "Mutation" Annex that they can use to grow larger or mimic their “parents” abilities. 1950s B-movie monsters are often of this type, giant ants, lizards, spiders, wasps, and even rabbits have been used. Classically of course, Cyclopses, Centaurs, Minotaurs, Satyrs, Gryphons (or Griffins), hippogriffs, dragons, sphinxes, manticores and of course the Chimera itself are all examples. You can also find examples in superheroes, especially those created by radioactive arachnids, or those with an X-gene.</td></tr><tr><td>Nature</td><td><strong>Elemental</strong> —The Monster is an elemental force of nature, perhaps a literal elemental or more likely metaphorical. The Elemental will add Nature Draw cards to its Monster Pool when it encounters a natural source of its element, or when it causes collateral damage to a new location. Elementals gain +1 Score per Monster Pool card to any action to do with their natural element. The Elemental must spend cards from its Monster Pool on Physical, Energy or Magical attacks based on its Element. Salamanders, Sylphs, Gnomes, and Undines, Giants (of most types), Storms as well as Tsunamis, landslides, pyroclastic flows and even blizzards (most of which might be Giants anyway).</td></tr><tr><td>Orthodox</td><td><strong>Tyrant</strong> —The Orthodox Monster is a tyrant, often a part of the system (in that it acts as a method of control over a population). A Tyrant monster may have troops that protect it, or work for it, or it may simply be so personally powerful that it can achieve its own goals. Tyrants may add Orthodox Draw to their Monster Pool every time they are Cruel or give orders. Tyrants can draw and Play current Monster Pool Size Reduced cards on the actions of their subordinates and underlings. However, the Tyrant can play their Monster Pool on their own actions, and must play at least one card from their Monster Pool if they directly act towards their goals.</td></tr><tr><td>Phoenix</td><td><strong>Paragon</strong> —The Monster is the embodiment of a positive force or emotion. Such Monsters are often a counter point, or are opposed to, another Monster. Phoenix Monsters are usually capable of causing positive (and sometimes negative) emotions via a Psychology Test / Psyche Attack in those that see them, and while they may be positive in nature, they are not necessarily good, or altruistic in their actions. The Paragon may have a Monster Pool of cards equal to their Phoenix Score and add cards from the Pool to any action they wish. The Paragon can discard half of their Monster Pool and multiply their Score by 1+ their Phoenix Dice. The Paragon's Monster Pool is replenished in full if the Paragon can find an external example of their chosen emotion. The Phoenix itself is the embodiment of Resurrection, Hope and Faith, but is quite capable of burning destruction. The Golden Goose is another example, the embodiment of charity, but it evokes profound avarice in those who would own it. The unicorn is a symbol of purity, but is dangerous in the extreme to those it considers impure. Depictions of Angels are also perfect examples of positive embodiments.</td></tr><tr><td>Quiet</td><td><strong>Hush</strong> —The Quiet Monster is exactly that, almost (or entirely) silent. It is the creeping death of a hidden illness, or the cat-footed predator striking without warning that vanishes without trace. Hush Monsters get to begin with Quiet Score cards in their Monster Pool. Hushes may add +1 for each card in their Monster Pool when try to they act silently. When a Hush creature makes a noise (often a scream or howl), it must play at least Quiet Draw cards from the Monster Pool upon the action. Creatures like giant owls, snakes or cats, that stalk and hunt silently are good examples, as is a really creepy, floating mime. Many ghosts are also silent, until they turn and scream creating a Duck attack.</td></tr><tr><td>Rook</td><td><strong>Guardian</strong> —The Monster is a Guardian, it guards a certain territory, path, treasure or person. Guardians may add Rook Draw to their Monster Pool whenever they act as a guard, try to interfere with an attack, or are attacked. The Guardian reduces all attacks by a number of Pips equal to the current Monster Pool automatically. The Guardian will however automatically play cards from its Monster Pool to defend itself (via any Active or Passive defence method that makes sense to the Referee. From Smaug to Fafnir dragons are often Guardians, looking after a particular treasure or demanding tributes from a territory.</td></tr><tr><td>Sin</td><td><strong>Villain</strong> —The Sin Monster is an embodiment of a specific vice, taboo, or sin, often in the form of a human Villain (often with animalistic features or tendencies). They usually have Annexes and Hitches that reflect their embodied vice or sin, and are often at least partially supernatural. Villains add Sin Draw cards to their Monster Pool when they, or another (usually both) indulges in their particular vice or behaviour. Which can be as broad as "Evil" if you like. Villains may add +1 Score for each card in their Monster Pool for any action that furthers their diabolical plans. Villains automatically spend Monster Pool cards on escape actions when attacked, creating at least Sin Draw Obstacles from the Monster Pools cards. The genocidal or narcissistic maniac, of the sort British Intelligence seems to tangle with repeatedly, could be a Sin Monster. The ever-hungry cannibal spirit, the Wendigo is definitely an example, as is the Sexual-predatory Lust-embodied Vampire.</td></tr><tr><td>Trial</td><td><strong>Killer</strong> —The Monster is a deadly Combat specialist of some sort. Killers add Trial Draw cards to their Monster Pool each time they kill someone. Killers automatically add +1 Score/Pip per card of Monster Pool to any attack, usually as Penetration or Range costs. Killers must spend Monster Pool cards whenever they will make a Trick. Killers can be drug-addled assassins, crazed shooters, or just steel-souled soldiers.</td></tr><tr><td>Virtue</td><td><strong>Friend</strong> —The Monster is a ‘Friendly’ monster, that will almost certainly be misunderstood and attacked by some. They may add Virtue Draw cards to their Monster Pool by being kind to another, or when they are attacked. A friendly Monster must protect their friends from harm, and must spend their Monster Pool to defend them. Friendly Monsters can add Virtue Score to any positive action when playing a card from their Monster Pool. Examples might include a cynical, but big-hearted ogre; a being created from stitched together corpses; a dark-elf ranger with a unicorn necklace; or perhaps even an Electric Mouse.</td></tr><tr><td>Wyrd</td><td><strong>Doom-weaver</strong> —The monster is a creature of the Fæ, it can draw and play Yarn cards or Wyrd Tarot cards (as appropriate). The Wyrd Monster may play cards from its Monster Pool as Wyrd Tarot or Yarn cards. The Monster may draw and add Wyrd Draw cards to its Monster Pool whenever it makes a choice, decision, or judgement. The Doom-weaver may draw and play Wyrd Draw cards as Ordeal cards whenever anyone plays Wyrd Tarot or Yarn cards. They must play a card from the Monster Pool if they have to undo a choice, decision or judgement they have made. Doom-Weavers come in many forms from the Cait-Sith, the Beansidhe, to the Faery nobles of Ironmaster.</td></tr><tr><td>Yonder</td><td><strong>Alien</strong> —The Monster is an alien, an outsider. It may just be a metaphor for alienation, or literally be an extra-(or ultra)-terrestrial. Such monsters are usually obvious and inhuman, not just to look at, but in the way they think and behave. They have apparently supernatural powers and abilities, but often make do with advanced technologies (in the form of additional Descendants). They may draw and play Yonder Draw cards to and from their Monster Pool with their alien / inhuman / weird actions. Their Monster Pool number of cards Reduced adds Success Levels to any action to do with motion, survival or the alien's technologies. From little grey Eusaiveans, through to pod people and pepper-pot shaped engines of hate, all the way to face-hugging xenomorphic space-wasps, there are plenty of aliens.</td></tr><tr><td>Zeal</td><td><strong>Radical</strong> —The Monster is an extremist, usually religious or political in nature. When acting according to their nature, or following their belief system, they may draw Zeal Draw Ordeal cards into their Monster Pool, they may Play cards from their Monster Pool to directly increase a Wound as they cause it, or directly reduce a Wound they receive (Pip for Pip). However, if they are forced to act outside of their nature, they are restricted by an automatic GRT equal to their Zeal Draw. The monster could be an evil priest, a terrorist, a devil, a djinn, or perhaps even the Angel of Death.</td></tr></tbody><tfoot><tr><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Facet&nbsp;&nbsp;</td><td>&nbsp;&nbsp;Monster&nbsp;&nbsp;</td></tr></tfoot></table></div><p></p></details></details>
		 </details>
			</div>
				<div class="t13ne-card-lea"><details class="t13ne-lea"><summary><strong>Lea</strong></summary><p class="t13ne-colour"><strong>Path Colour —</strong> Grey</p><p class="t13ne-bulmas"><strong>Allowed Bulmäs —</strong> Sleeping</p><p class="t13ne-animal-category"><strong>Animal Category —</strong> None</p><p class="t13ne-animal-example"><strong>Example Animal —</strong> A Plant / Fossil</p><p class="t13ne-notes"><strong>Notes —</strong> The Grey path allows a Bulmäs Soul to sleep in the landscape. Their Soul may live in a skull, horn, tree or fossil for years, millennia or millions of years, until it is passed on, by ingestion of a fruit or nut, or by a Soul-Release ceremony. Fossil Bulmäs include Moon-Staves, Flint Weapons (Axes or Knives) and Elf-shot Armour that can only be worn by a Bulmäs who have a Soul related to the Fossil Soul.</p></details></div></div></div>
